<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Image?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> An **image** is a two-dimensional function, $f(x, y)$, where $x$ and $y$ are spatial (plane) coordinates, and the amplitude of $f$ at any pair of coordinates $(x, y)$ is called the **intensity** or **gray level** of the image at that point. When $x$, $y$, and the intensity values of $f$ are all finite, discrete quantities, the image is called a **digital image**.

A **digital image** consists of a finite number of elements, each of which has a particular location and value. These elements are called **picture elements, image elements, pels, or pixels**. Pixel is the smallest addressable unit of a digital image and is the KTU-recommended terminology for all board examinations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight ŌĆö Image Representation**
> A digital image can be represented as an $M \times N$ matrix of pixel values, where $M$ is the number of rows and $N$ is the number of columns. Each entry is indexed as $f(x, y)$ with $0 \le x \le M-1$ and $0 \le y \le N-1$.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a digital image as a **mosaic artwork** or a **chessboard-like grid of tiny tiles**. Each tile is a *pixel* with one solid color (a single number). Stand far away and your eyes blend them into a smooth picture; zoom in, and you see the grid. A **grayscale image** is like a black-and-white mosaic where each tile's shade (a number between 0 and 255) tells you "how bright." A **color image** is three such mosaics stacked ŌĆö one each for Red, Green, and Blue.

## 1.2 The Three Coordinate Conventions (KTU Standard)

| Convention | Use | Origin |
|---|---|---|
| $(x, y)$ with $y$ pointing DOWN | Standard image-processing text (Gonzalez & Woods) | Top-left of image |
| $(r, c)$ with row, column | MATLAB / Python indexing | Top-left pixel |
| $(x, y)$ with $y$ pointing UP | Mathematical plotting | Bottom-left |

KTU board questions universally use the **first convention**: $f(x, y)$ where $(0, 0)$ is the **top-left** corner.

## 1.3 Image Types ŌĆö A Quick Taxonomy

> [!NOTE]
> **Binary Image:** 1 bit per pixel, only two values: $0$ (black) or $1$ (white).
> **Grayscale Image:** typically $k = 8$ bits per pixel, $2^8 = 256$ gray levels, range $[0, 255]$.
> **Color (RGB) Image:** 3 channels, 24 bits per pixel (true color).
> **Indexed Image:** a colormap (lookup table) maps a small palette index to RGB triplets.
> **Multispectral / Hyperspectral:** tens to hundreds of bands (e.g., remote sensing).

## 1.4 Image Digitization ŌĆö A Bird's-Eye View

Digitization is the conversion of a **continuous (analog)** real-world scene into a **discrete (digital)** form the computer can store. It is a **two-step process**:

1. **Sampling** ŌĆö discretize the *spatial* coordinates $(x, y)$.
2. **Quantization** ŌĆö discretize the *intensity* values $f$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** **Sampling = "Where?"** (spatial discretization), **Quantization = "How bright?"** (amplitude discretization). Both are required to produce a true digital image.

## 1.5 Visual Perception of the Image

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** Visual perception is the human brain's interpretation of the light energy that reaches the retina. The **Human Visual System (HVS)** is often the gold-standard reference against which image quality is judged because the ultimate consumer of an image is almost always the human eye.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine your eye as a **biological camera**:
- The **cornea + lens** = camera lens (focuses light).
- The **iris** = camera aperture (controls the amount of light).
- The **retina** = the image sensor (contains photoreceptors: **rods** and **cones**).
- The **optic nerve + visual cortex** = image processor (interprets the raw data).

Key facts to memorize for KTU:
- The human eye can adapt to a luminance range of about **$10^{10}$** levels.
- **Rods** are responsible for scotopic (low-light) vision; **Cones** for photopic (bright-light) and color vision.
- Three types of cones: **L (red, Ōēł 564 nm)**, **M (green, Ōēł 534 nm)**, **S (blue, Ōēł 420 nm)**.
- **Mach bands** ŌĆö an optical illusion where the visual system exaggerates contrast at edges (a perception quirk, not a real intensity change).
- **Simultaneous contrast** ŌĆö a gray patch appears darker on a light background than on a dark background (perception is context-dependent).

## 1.6 Image Quality ŌĆö Quick Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **Image Quality** is a measure of how visually faithful a digital image is to the original scene. Two key objective metrics are:
> - **SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio)** ŌĆö strength of the desired signal relative to noise.
> - **PSNR (Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio)** ŌĆö derived from the Mean Squared Error.

## 1.7 Noise in Images

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Noise** is any unwanted random variation in brightness or color information in an image. It arises during image acquisition, transmission, or storage. Common types: **Gaussian, Salt-and-Pepper, Shot (Poisson), Uniform, Rayleigh, and Exponential**.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you are taking a photograph at night. Your picture may show **grainy speckles** that were *not* in the scene ŌĆö that grain is noise. The "real signal" is the perfect picture; noise is the random junk that got added on top.

# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Image Representations (Detailed)

A digital image of size $M \times N$ pixels is mathematically represented as:

$$
f(x, y) =
\begin{bmatrix}
f(0, 0) & f(0, 1) & \cdots & f(0, N-1) \\
f(1, 0) & f(1, 1) & \cdots & f(1, N-1) \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
f(M-1, 0) & f(M-1, 1) & \cdots & f(M-1, N-1)
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Each entry $f(x, y)$ is a non-negative scalar (for grayscale) or a 3-tuple vector (for color).

- **Coordinate convention:** $x$ indexes the row (vertical), $y$ indexes the column (horizontal).
- **Origin:** top-left corner, i.e., $f(0, 0)$.
- **Storage requirement (uncompressed):** $M \times N \times k$ bits, where $k$ = bits per pixel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in engineering?** A single $4096 \times 4096$ 8-bit grayscale medical X-ray requires $\approx 16.78$ MB. A 10-second full-HD color video at 30 fps needs $\approx 6.6$ GB. Storage and bandwidth requirements drive virtually all design decisions in medical imaging, satellite imaging, and video streaming.

## 2.2 Image Digitization ŌĆö The Two Pillars

### 2.2.1 Sampling

**Definition (KTU):** Sampling is the process of converting the continuous spatial domain into a discrete grid of points.

- **Spatial resolution** is determined by the **sampling rate**, often expressed in **pixels per inch (PPI)** or **dots per inch (DPI)**.
- A higher sampling rate $\Rightarrow$ more pixels $\Rightarrow$ finer detail but larger file size.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NyquistŌĆōShannon Sampling Theorem (Board-Favorite):**
> A band-limited continuous signal of highest frequency $f_{\max}$ can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples if and only if the sampling frequency $f_s \ge 2 f_{\max}$. The minimum acceptable rate $f_s = 2 f_{\max}$ is the **Nyquist rate**.

**Aliasing:** If $f_s < 2 f_{\max}$, frequencies above $f_s / 2$ fold back into the baseband, producing the **aliasing artifact** (e.g., wagon-wheel effect, staircased diagonal lines in low-res images).

### 2.2.2 Quantization

**Definition (KTU):** Quantization is the process of mapping the continuous range of intensity values into a finite set of discrete integer levels.

- **Number of gray levels:** $L = 2^k$, where $k$ = number of bits per pixel.
- The quantization **step size** is:
$$
\Delta = \frac{I_{\max} - I_{\min}}{L - 1}
$$
- **Quantization error** $\varepsilon = f - \hat f$ has magnitude bounded by $\Delta / 2$ (for uniform mid-rise quantizers).
- For a true 8-bit image: $L = 256$ levels, $\Delta \approx 1$ gray level.

> [!NOTE]
> **Types of Quantization:**
> - **Uniform (linear):** equal step size $\Delta$ across the entire range. Simple, fast.
> - **Non-uniform:** smaller steps in regions of high probability density (e.g., log quantizer for images obeying Weber's law).

## 2.3 Digital Image Properties

### 2.3.1 Metric Properties of Digital Images

Metric properties depend on the **distance measure** between pixels. Let $p = (x, y)$ and $q = (s, t)$ be two pixels.

| Property | Definition | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Euclidean distance | $D_e(p, q) = \sqrt{(x-s)^2 + (y-t)^2}$ | $D_e$ |
| City-block (Manhattan) | $D_4(p, q) = \vert x - s \vert + \vert y - t \vert$ | $D_4$ |
| Chessboard | $D_8(p, q) = \max(\vert x - s \vert, \vert y - t \vert)$ | $D_8$ |

- **$D_4$ distance** is the number of steps in a 4-neighbor walk (no diagonals).
- **$D_8$ distance** is the number of steps in an 8-neighbor walk (with diagonals).

**Neighborhoods:**
- **4-neighborhood of $p$:** $N_4(p) = \{(x+1, y), (x-1, y), (x, y+1), (x, y-1)\}$.
- **Diagonal neighborhood:** $N_D(p) = \{(x+1, y+1), (x+1, y-1), (x-1, y+1), (x-1, y-1)\}$.
- **8-neighborhood:** $N_8(p) = N_4(p) \cup N_D(p)$.

### 2.3.2 Topological Properties

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adjacency (KTU Definition):** Let $V$ be the set of gray-level values used to define adjacency. A pixel $p$ with coordinates $(x, y)$ is **adjacent** to pixel $q$ with coordinates $(s, t)$ if:
> 1. $q \in N_4(p)$ (or $N_8(p)$ or $N_D(p)$), **and**
> 2. $V$ contains the value of $f$ at $q$ (and at $p$ for the relevant cases).

- **4-adjacency:** pixels sharing an edge.
- **8-adjacency:** pixels sharing an edge or a corner.
- **m-adjacency (mixed):** pixels are m-adjacent if they are 4-adjacent **OR** if they are diagonal and $N_4(p) \cap N_4(q)$ has no pixels with values in $V$. m-adjacency eliminates the multiple-path ambiguity of 8-adjacency.

**Connectivity:** Two pixels are **connected** if there exists a path of adjacent pixels between them.

**Regions and Boundaries:**
- A **region** $R$ is a connected set of pixels.
- The **boundary** (or **contour**) of $R$ is the set of pixels in $R$ that have at least one neighbor not in $R$.
- **Foreground** and **background** are complement regions.

**Holes:** A **hole** is a connected background region completely surrounded by foreground.

**Connected Components:** Distinct maximal connected subsets within the image.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why topology matters in engineering:** Topology is *invariant* to geometric transformations (rotation, translation, scaling). It is the basis of **object counting, region filling, connected-component labeling, and image segmentation** algorithms.

## 2.4 Histograms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU):** The histogram of a digital image with intensity levels in the range $[0, L-1]$ is a discrete function $h(r_k) = n_k$, where $r_k$ is the $k$-th intensity value and $n_k$ is the number of pixels in the image having that intensity. The **normalized histogram** is $p(r_k) = n_k / (MN)$, where $MN$ is the total number of pixels. The sum of $p(r_k)$ over all $k$ is $1$.

**Interpretation:**
- **Dark image:** histogram skewed to the **left** (concentrated near 0).
- **Bright image:** histogram skewed to the **right** (concentrated near $L-1$).
- **Low contrast:** histogram is **narrow** and concentrated in a small range.
- **High contrast:** histogram is **spread out** over the full range.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** The histogram discards all *spatial* information. Two completely different images can have identical histograms.

## 2.5 Entropy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU, Information-Theoretic):** Image entropy $H$ is the average amount of information (in bits) per pixel, measured using the normalized histogram:
> $$H = - \sum_{k=0}^{L-1} p(r_k) \log_2 p(r_k)$$
> where $0 \cdot \log_2 0$ is taken as $0$. $H$ is measured in **bits per pixel**.

- **Maximum entropy** = $\log_2 L$ bits/pixel, achieved when all $p(r_k) = 1/L$ (uniform histogram).
- **Lower entropy** = more compressible image (redundancy present).
- **Zero entropy** = constant image (single intensity).

**Engineering Utility:** Entropy is the **theoretical lower bound on lossless compression** (Shannon's source coding theorem). It is the central quantity in JPEG, PNG, and arithmetic coding.

## 2.6 Noise in Images ŌĆö Probability Density Functions

> [!NOTE]
> **Common noise PDFs (high-yield for KTU):**
>
> **Gaussian (normal):**
> $$p(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi} \sigma} \, e^{-(z - \mu)^2 / (2 \sigma^2)}$$
> Arises in electronic sensors, image acquisition circuits. Mean $\mu$, variance $\sigma^2$.
>
> **Uniform:**
> $$p(z) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{b - a}, & a \le z \le b \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$
> Mean $= (a+b)/2$, variance $= (b-a)^2 / 12$. Used in dithering, simulations.
>
> **Salt-and-Pepper (Impulse):** Random pixels are forced to minimum (pepper) or maximum (salt). Probability $P_s$ for salt, $P_p$ for pepper.
>
> **Rayleigh:**
> $$p(z) = \frac{2}{b} (z - a) \, e^{-(z-a)^2 / b}, \quad z \ge a$$
> Mean $= a + \sqrt{\pi b / 4}$. Models **range imaging** artifacts.
>
> **Exponential:**
> $$p(z) = a \, e^{-a z}, \quad z \ge 0$$
> Mean $= 1/a$, variance $= 1/a^2$. Special case of Gamma noise.
>
> **Gamma (Erlang):**
> $$p(z) = \frac{a^b z^{b-1}}{(b-1)!} \, e^{-a z}, \quad z \ge 0$$
> Mean $= b/a$, variance $= b/a^2$. Models **laser imaging**.
>
> **Poisson (Shot):** $p(z) = \frac{\lambda^z e^{-\lambda}}{z!}$ with mean $=$ variance $= \lambda$. Arises in photon-counting sensors.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are test-favorite; memorize the units and the boundary conditions.**

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Image size in memory | $B = M \cdot N \cdot k$ | bits |
| Number of gray levels | $L = 2^k$ | dimensionless |
| Nyquist rate | $f_s = 2 f_{\max}$ | samples per second (Hz) |
| Quantization step (uniform) | $\Delta = (I_{\max} - I_{\min}) / (L - 1)$ | intensity units |
| Histogram (raw) | $h(r_k) = n_k$ | count |
| Normalized histogram | $p(r_k) = n_k / (M N)$ | probability |
| Entropy | $H = - \sum p(r_k) \log_2 p(r_k)$ | bits per pixel |
| Max entropy | $H_{\max} = \log_2 L$ | bits per pixel |
| Euclidean distance | $D_e(p, q) = \sqrt{(x-s)^2 + (y-t)^2}$ | pixels |
| $D_4$ distance | $D_4(p, q) = \vert x - s \vert + \vert y - t \vert$ | pixels |
| $D_8$ distance | $D_8(p, q) = \max(\vert x - s \vert, \vert y - t \vert)$ | pixels |
| Mean Squared Error | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{MN} \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} [f(x, y) - \hat f(x, y)]^2$ | intensity$^2$ |
| PSNR | $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{(\text{peak})^2}{\text{MSE}} \right) = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{\text{peak}}{\sqrt{\text{MSE}}} \right)$ | decibels (dB) |
| Peak value (8-bit) | peak $= 255$ | intensity |
| SNR | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{\sum f^2}{\sum (f - \hat f)^2} \right)$ | dB |
| Gaussian PDF | $p(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma} e^{-(z - \mu)^2 / (2\sigma^2)}$ | ŌĆö |

**Real-world engineering utility:** These formulas drive every **codec** (JPEG, HEVC, AV1), every **denoising filter** (Gaussian, median, Wiener), and every **medical-grade QA tool** (DICOM, PSNR-based CT reconstruction).

# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Storage Requirements of a Digital Image

**Problem Setup.** A grayscale image has $M$ rows and $N$ columns, and each pixel is stored using $k$ bits. Derive the total number of bits $B$ required to store the image uncompressed.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Number of pixels.**
The image grid is rectangular. The total number of pixel positions is the area:

$$
\text{Pixels} = M \times N
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Bits per pixel.**
Each pixel can take one of $2^k$ discrete intensity levels, so $k$ bits encode one pixel's value.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Total storage.**
Multiply the number of pixels by the bits per pixel:

$$
B = (\text{Pixels}) \times (\text{bits per pixel}) = M \cdot N \cdot k \;\; \text{bits}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Convert to bytes.**
Divide by 8 to get bytes:

$$
B_{\text{bytes}} = \frac{M \cdot N \cdot k}{8}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example.** Consider a $1024 \times 1024$, 8-bit grayscale X-ray. Then $M = N = 1024$, $k = 8$. Hence $B = 1024 \times 1024 \times 8 = 8\,388\,608$ bits $= 1\,048\,576$ bytes $= 1$ MB exactly.

**Analytical Matrix (Storage Variation):**

| Image Type | M | N | k | Total Bits | Bytes | Approx. MB |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| VGA grayscale | 640 | 480 | 8 | 2,457,600 | 307,200 | 0.29 |
| HD 1080 grayscale | 1920 | 1080 | 8 | 16,588,800 | 2,073,600 | 1.98 |
| 4K RGB | 3840 | 2160 | 24 | 199,065,600 | 24,883,200 | 23.73 |
| Medical 16-bit CT | 512 | 512 | 16 | 4,194,304 | 524,288 | 0.50 |

## 3.2 Derivation: Image Entropy from a Histogram

**Problem Setup.** A $4 \times 4$ grayscale image has 16 pixels. Their gray-level distribution is:
- 4 pixels at intensity $0$,
- 6 pixels at intensity $64$,
- 4 pixels at intensity $128$,
- 2 pixels at intensity $255$.

Compute the entropy $H$ in bits per pixel.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Total pixels.**
$M N = 4 \times 4 = 16$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the normalized histogram $p(r_k) = n_k / (MN)$.**

$$
p(0) = \frac{4}{16} = 0.25, \quad p(64) = \frac{6}{16} = 0.375, \quad p(128) = \frac{4}{16} = 0.25, \quad p(255) = \frac{2}{16} = 0.125
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the entropy formula.**

$$
H = - \sum_{k} p(r_k) \log_2 p(r_k)
$$

Compute each term:

$$
- 0.25 \log_2(0.25) = -0.25 \times (-2) = 0.500
$$

$$
- 0.375 \log_2(0.375) = -0.375 \times (-1.4150) = 0.5306
$$

$$
- 0.25 \log_2(0.25) = 0.500 \quad \text{(same as intensity 0)}
$$

$$
- 0.125 \log_2(0.125) = -0.125 \times (-3) = 0.375
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Sum the terms.**

$$
H = 0.500 + 0.5306 + 0.500 + 0.375 = 1.9056 \;\text{bits/pixel}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compare with maximum entropy.**

For an 8-bit image, $H_{\max} = \log_2 256 = 8$ bits/pixel. Our image's entropy of $\approx 1.91$ bits/pixel is far below the maximum, indicating **high redundancy and high compressibility** (e.g., PNG would shrink it dramatically).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A lossless compressor cannot beat $H$ bits per pixel on average. Our $1.91$ bits/pixel bound means a 16-pixel image of 8 bits/pixel (128 raw bits) could be losslessly coded using $\approx 1.91 \times 16 \approx 30.5$ bits ŌĆö a compression ratio of $\approx 4.2:1$.

## 3.3 Derivation: PSNR from a Noisy Image

**Problem Setup.** Suppose an original image $f$ is corrupted to give $\hat f$. Derive PSNR in dB.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Mean Squared Error.**
Average the squared difference over all pixels:

$$
\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{M N} \sum_{x=0}^{M-1} \sum_{y=0}^{N-1} [f(x, y) - \hat f(x, y)]^2
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Define the peak signal.**
For a $k$-bit image, the peak intensity is $2^k - 1$. For $k = 8$, peak $= 255$.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Express PSNR.**

$$
\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{\text{peak}^2}{\text{MSE}} \right)
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Logarithmic expansion (proof of equivalence).**

$$
\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \text{peak}^2 \right) - 10 \log_{10} \left( \text{MSE} \right) = 20 \log_{10} \left( \text{peak} \right) - 10 \log_{10} \left( \text{MSE} \right)
$$

This is the form KTU evaluators expect on the answer sheet.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Numerical Example.**
For an 8-bit image with MSE $= 65$ (very noisy):

$$
\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{255^2}{65} \right) = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{65\,025}{65} \right) = 10 \log_{10}(1000.38) \approx 30 \;\text{dB}
$$

A PSNR of 30 dB is considered acceptable for lossy compression; below 20 dB is usually unacceptable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why PSNR matters in engineering:** Video engineers use PSNR to compare codecs. Medical imaging mandates PSNR $> 40$ dB for diagnostic fidelity. Satellite image analysts may tolerate lower PSNR if spectral accuracy is preserved.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Distance Calculations

**Problem Setup.** Compute $D_e$, $D_4$, and $D_8$ between $p = (2, 3)$ and $q = (5, 7)$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Coordinate differences.**

$$
\Delta x = 5 - 2 = 3, \qquad \Delta y = 7 - 3 = 4
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Euclidean.**

$$
D_e(p, q) = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö City-block.**

$$
D_4(p, q) = \vert 3 \vert + \vert 4 \vert = 7
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Chessboard.**

$$
D_8(p, q) = \max(3, 4) = 4
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** For any pair, $D_8 \le D_e \le D_4$. Indeed, $4 \le 5 \le 7$. Ō£ō

## 3.5 Worked Example: Quantization Step Size

**Problem Setup.** An image uses intensities in $[0, 1]$ (floating-point). It is uniformly quantized to $L = 8$ levels. Find the step size $\Delta$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply the formula.**

$$
\Delta = \frac{I_{\max} - I_{\min}}{L - 1} = \frac{1 - 0}{8 - 1} = \frac{1}{7} \approx 0.1429
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Maximum quantization error.**

$$
\varepsilon_{\max} = \frac{\Delta}{2} = \frac{1}{14} \approx 0.0714
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Bits required.**

$$
k = \log_2 8 = 3 \;\text{bits per pixel}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Total storage for a $256 \times 256$ image.**

$$
B = 256 \times 256 \times 3 = 196\,608 \;\text{bits} = 24\,576 \;\text{bytes} = 24 \;\text{kB}
$$

## 3.6 Derivation: Minimum Sampling Rate to Avoid Aliasing

**Problem Setup.** An audio signal has maximum frequency $f_{\max} = 20\,\text{kHz}$. Find the minimum sampling rate to perfectly reconstruct the signal.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply Nyquist theorem.**

$$
f_s \ge 2 f_{\max} = 2 \times 20\,000 = 40\,000 \;\text{Hz} = 40 \;\text{kHz}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Engineering context.**
This is exactly why **CD-quality audio uses $44.1$ kHz** ŌĆö slightly above the Nyquist rate to allow for practical anti-aliasing filter roll-off. **Studio audio** uses $48$ kHz for the same reason with extra margin.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Spatial sampling analogy.**
For a digital image, the "spatial frequency" is in cycles per millimeter. The same Nyquist principle applies: a high-resolution image of fine textures requires more pixels per millimeter to avoid aliasing (jagged edges, moir├® patterns).

# SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Image Digitization Pipeline

This diagram maps the full flow from real-world scene to displayable digital image.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SCENE Continuous real world light energy] --> B[SAMPLING Spatial discretization on x y grid]
    B --> C[QUANTIZATION Amplitude discretization into L levels]
    C --> D[DIGITAL IMAGE Matrix of M by N integer pixels]
    D --> E[STORAGE OR TRANSMISSION File codec network]
    E --> F[DISPLAY Reconstruction on screen]

    B -.->|Risk aliasing| G[Aliasing artifact jagged lines]
    C -.->|Risk contouring| H[Quantization noise false contours]
    D -.->|Insufficient samples| I[Low spatial resolution]
    D -.->|Insufficient levels| J[Posterization false colors]
```

## 4.2 Human Visual Perception ŌĆö Block Architecture

Adapted from the schematic representation used in Gonzalez & Woods.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[Light source illumination] --> L[Optical system cornea lens iris]
    L --> M[Retina photoreceptors rods and cones]
    M --> N[Optic nerve signal pathway]
    N --> O[Visual cortex image interpretation]
    O --> P[Perceived image conscious visual experience]

    M -.->|Rods scotopic brightness| Q[Brightness perception]
    M -.->|Cones photopic color| R[Color perception L M S cones]
    O -.->|Edge enhancement| S[Mach bands optical illusion]
    O -.->|Context dependent| T[Simultaneous contrast optical illusion]
    O -.->|Range adaptation| U[Dynamic range 10 to the power 10 levels]
```

## 4.3 Taxonomy of Image Noise

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[NOISE IN DIGITAL IMAGES] --> B0[Gaussian normal acquisition electronics]
    A0 --> C0[Salt and Pepper impulse faulty switching]
    A0 --> D0[Poisson shot photon counting sensor]
    A0 --> E0[Uniform dithering simulation]
    A0 --> F0[Rayleigh range imaging artifact]
    A0 --> G0[Exponential laser imaging]
    A0 --> H0[Gamma Erlang laser speckle]

    B0 -.->|Parameter mean mu variance sigma squared| I0[PDF formula normal distribution]
    C0 -.->|Parameter Ps and Pp probabilities| J0[Binary min and max values]
    D0 -.->|Parameter lambda mean equals variance| K0[Count based distribution]
    E0 -.->|Parameter a and b range| L0[Constant PDF over range]
    F0 -.->|Parameter a and b| M0[Skewed bell PDF]
    G0 -.->|Parameter a| N0[Monotonic exponential decay]
    H0 -.->|Parameter a and b| O0[Generalized gamma family]
```

## 4.4 Pixel Neighborhood & Adjacency Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[PIXEL P at coordinate x y] --> P4[4 NEIGHBORS N4 P]
    P --> PD[DIAGONAL NEIGHBORS N sub D of P]
    P --> P8[8 NEIGHBORS N8 P equals N4 plus ND]

    P4 --> A4[4 ADJACENCY shared edge only]
    P8 --> A8[8 ADJACENCY shared edge or corner]
    P8 --> AM[M ADJACENCY mixed 4 plus diagonal rules]

    A4 --> C1[CONNECTIVITY via 4 path]
    A8 --> C2[CONNECTIVITY via 8 path]
    AM --> C3[CONNECTIVITY via m path no multiple path ambiguity]

    C1 --> R1[REGION foreground or background]
    C2 --> R2[BOUNDARY pixels in region with neighbor outside]
    C3 --> R3[HOLE background region enclosed by foreground]
```

    H0 --> H2[Bright image right skewed]
    H0 --> H3[Low contrast narrow]
    H0 --> H4[High contrast spread full range]
    H0 --> H5[Bimodal two object types]
    H0 --> H6[Uniform high entropy compressibility low]

    H0 --> H7[Normalized p of rk equals nk divided by MN]

# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]
**Define a digital image. Explain the terms *pixel*, *gray level*, and *resolution*.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **digital image** is a two-dimensional discrete function $f(x, y)$ that has been discretized in both spatial coordinates $(x, y)$ and amplitude (intensity). Formally, $f(x, y)$ is defined for $x \in \{0, 1, \ldots, M-1\}$ and $y \in \{0, 1, \ldots, N-1\}$, and the intensity takes on a finite set of $L$ discrete values, where $L = 2^k$ for some integer $k \ge 1$.

- **Pixel (Picture Element):** The smallest addressable element of a digital image, located at a unique integer coordinate $(x, y)$. Each pixel stores a single intensity value (for grayscale) or a triplet of values (for color).
- **Gray Level:** The numerical intensity value associated with a pixel. For a $k$-bit image, the gray level ranges from $0$ (black) to $2^k - 1$ (white). For an 8-bit image, the range is $[0, 255]$.
- **Resolution:** The fineness of spatial detail in an image, typically measured as $M \times N$ (pixel dimensions) or as spatial sampling density in pixels per inch (PPI) or dots per inch (DPI). Higher resolution means more pixels and finer detail.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students confuse **gray level** with **gray scale**. *Gray level* is a single intensity value at a pixel; *gray scale* refers to the entire range of possible intensities. Mixing the two costs marks.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]
**What is image entropy? Compute the entropy of a $2 \times 2$ image whose pixel values are $\{0, 0, 255, 255\}$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Image Entropy:** It is the average information content (in bits) per pixel, defined as:

$$
H = - \sum_{k=0}^{L-1} p(r_k) \log_2 p(r_k)
$$

where $p(r_k)$ is the normalized histogram of the image.

**Computation for the given image:**

Total pixels: $M N = 2 \times 2 = 4$.

Histogram counts: $n(0) = 2$, $n(255) = 2$, all other $n(r_k) = 0$.

Normalized histogram:

$$
p(0) = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5, \qquad p(255) = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5
$$

Entropy:

$$
H = - [0.5 \log_2 0.5 + 0.5 \log_2 0.5] = - [0.5 \times (-1) + 0.5 \times (-1)] = -(-1) = 1 \;\text{bit/pixel}
$$

The image is **perfectly compressible** in a binary sense ŌĆö it can be losslessly encoded at exactly 1 bit per pixel.

---

## PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### INTERNAL CHOICE ŌĆö Answer ANY ONE of the following.

---

### Question A. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, modified]
**(a) Explain the process of image digitization with a neat block diagram. Differentiate between sampling and quantization.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) State and prove the Nyquist sampling theorem. Discuss the aliasing effect with an example.** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) ŌĆö 7 marks

**Image Digitization** is the conversion of a continuous real-world scene into a digital image suitable for computer storage and processing. It involves two fundamental steps:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Sampling:**
Sampling is the discretization of the spatial domain. The continuous spatial coordinates $(x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$ are replaced with discrete integer coordinates $(x, y) \in \mathbb{Z}^2$, forming an $M \times N$ grid. The sampling rate determines the **spatial resolution** of the image. Higher sampling rates produce finer detail at the cost of larger file sizes.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Quantization:**
Quantization is the discretization of the intensity (amplitude) domain. The continuous range of brightness values is mapped to a finite set of $L = 2^k$ levels, where $k$ is the number of bits per pixel. The quantization step is:

$$
\Delta = \frac{I_{\max} - I_{\min}}{L - 1}
$$

**Differences between Sampling and Quantization:**

| Parameter | Sampling | Quantization |
|---|---|---|
| Domain | Spatial $(x, y)$ | Amplitude / intensity |
| Question answered | *Where?* ŌĆö at which points to measure | *How bright?* ŌĆö which discrete value to assign |
| Resolution controlled | Spatial resolution ($M \times N$) | Intensity resolution ($k$ bits) |
| Effect of increase | Finer detail | Finer gradation of brightness |
| Typical error | Aliasing (jagged lines) | Contouring (false color bands) |

**Block Diagram (refer to SECTION 4, Diagram 4.1):**
Scene ŌåÆ Sampling ŌåÆ Quantization ŌåÆ Digital Image ŌåÆ Storage / Display.

#### Model Solution for (b) ŌĆö 7 marks

**NyquistŌĆōShannon Sampling Theorem (Statement):**
A band-limited continuous signal $x(t)$ whose highest frequency component is $f_{\max}$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency satisfies:

$$
f_s \ge 2 f_{\max}
$$

The minimum allowable rate $f_s = 2 f_{\max}$ is called the **Nyquist rate**.

**Proof Outline (ideal low-pass reconstruction):**
Let $x(t)$ be band-limited to $f_{\max}$. Its samples are $x(nT)$ where $T = 1/f_s$ is the sampling period. The WhittakerŌĆōShannon interpolation formula states:

$$
x(t) = \sum_{n = -\infty}^{\infty} x(nT) \, \frac{\sin(\pi (t - nT) / T)}{\pi (t - nT) / T} = \sum_{n = -\infty}^{\infty} x(nT) \, \text{sinc}\!\left( \frac{t - nT}{T} \right)
$$

This series converges to the original signal only if the replicas of the spectrum introduced by sampling do not overlap, which requires $f_s \ge 2 f_{\max}$.

**Aliasing Effect:**
When $f_s < 2 f_{\max}$, the high-frequency components fold back into the baseband, creating spurious low-frequency artifacts. The aliased frequency is:

$$
f_{\text{alias}} = \vert f - f_s \cdot \text{round}(f / f_s) \vert
$$

**Example (wagon-wheel effect in film):** Spokes rotating at 25 Hz, sampled at 24 frames per second, appear to rotate **backward** at 1 Hz, because the sampling rate is below the Nyquist threshold ($2 \times 25 = 50$ Hz).

In images, aliasing manifests as **staircased diagonal lines** and **moir├® patterns** on striped fabrics. Anti-aliasing is achieved by low-pass filtering before sampling or by supersampling and averaging.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Stating the theorem as "$f_s > f_{\max}$" ŌĆö incorrect. The factor of **2 is mandatory**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing spatial aliasing (in images) with temporal aliasing (in video). Always mention both contexts in the answer for full marks.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not labelling the block diagram with input/output arrows. A diagram without labels earns only partial credit.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, modified]
**(a) Define adjacency, connectivity, region, and boundary. Differentiate between 4-, 8-, and m-adjacency with examples.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Discuss various types of noise that affect digital images. Derive the PDF and mean of Gaussian noise.** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) ŌĆö 7 marks

**Adjacency:** Two pixels $p$ and $q$, each with values from a set $V$, are adjacent if $q$ is in the chosen neighborhood of $p$ **and** their values are in $V$.

**Connectivity:** Two pixels are *connected* if there exists a path of adjacent pixels between them.

**Region:** A connected set of pixels is a *region*.

**Boundary:** The set of pixels in a region that have at least one neighbor outside the region forms the *boundary* (or *contour*).

**Three Adjacency Types ŌĆö Comparison:**

| Type | Definition | Multiple Paths? | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 4-adjacency | Pixels share an edge | No ambiguity | Used in compact object representation |
| 8-adjacency | Pixels share an edge or a corner | Yes ŌĆö diagonal ambiguity exists | Used in image labeling |
| m-adjacency | 4-adjacent OR (diagonal AND no shared 4-neighbor in $V$) | No ŌĆö eliminates multiple-path ambiguity | Used in connected-component analysis |

**Example (m-adjacency eliminates ambiguity):**
Consider a checkerboard pattern of 0s and 1s. Pixels are *8-adjacent* to multiple neighbors of the *opposite* value, creating ambiguous paths. Under *m-adjacency*, the diagonal links are broken because the shared 4-neighbor of $p$ and $q$ already lies in $V$, ensuring a single, unique shortest path.

**Engineering Utility:** m-adjacency is the foundation of **flood-fill algorithms** in painting software and **connected-component labeling** in industrial inspection.

#### Model Solution for (b) ŌĆö 7 marks

**Noise:** Noise is any undesired random fluctuation superimposed on the true pixel intensity. Sources include:
- Sensor imperfections (thermal, shot noise)
- Insufficient light (photon shot noise)
- Transmission channel interference
- Quantization (rounding errors)
- Faulty memory cells (impulse noise)

**Types of Noise (most frequently tested):**

1. **Gaussian Noise:** Arises in acquisition electronics; the central limit theorem makes it ubiquitous. PDF:

$$
p(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi} \sigma} \, e^{-(z - \mu)^2 / (2 \sigma^2)}
$$

- $\mu$ = mean (often 0 for "zero-mean" Gaussian)
- $\sigma^2$ = variance
- Approximately 99.7% of values lie within $\mu \pm 3 \sigma$.

2. **Salt-and-Pepper Noise:** Random pixels are clamped to minimum (pepper, 0) or maximum (salt, $L-1$). Characterized by $P_s$ and $P_p$ (salt and pepper probabilities).

3. **Shot / Poisson Noise:** $p(z) = \frac{\lambda^z e^{-\lambda}}{z!}$ with mean $=$ variance $= \lambda$. Dominant in photon-limited imaging (astronomy, low-light microscopy).

4. **Uniform Noise:** $p(z) = \frac{1}{b - a}$ for $a \le z \le b$. Used in dithering.

5. **Rayleigh Noise:** $p(z) = \frac{2}{b} (z - a) \, e^{-(z - a)^2 / b}$ for $z \ge a$. Models range-imaging artifacts.

6. **Exponential Noise:** $p(z) = a \, e^{-a z}$ for $z \ge 0$. Special case of the Gamma family.

7. **Gamma (Erlang) Noise:** $p(z) = \frac{a^b z^{b-1}}{(b-1)!} \, e^{-a z}$ for $z \ge 0$. Used in laser imaging.

**Derivation of Mean of Gaussian PDF:**

The mean is the expected value:

$$
E[Z] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z \, p(z) \, dz = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi} \sigma} \, e^{-(z - \mu)^2 / (2 \sigma^2)} \, dz
$$

Substitute $u = z - \mu$, so $z = u + \mu$ and $dz = du$:

$$
E[Z] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (u + \mu) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi} \sigma} \, e^{-u^2 / (2 \sigma^2)} \, du
$$

Split into two integrals:

$$
E[Z] = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} u \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi} \sigma} \, e^{-u^2 / (2 \sigma^2)} \, du \; + \; \mu \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi} \sigma} \, e^{-u^2 / (2 \sigma^2)} \, du
$$

The first integral is **zero** because the integrand is an odd function of $u$. The second integral equals $\mu$ because the integrand is the normalized Gaussian PDF that integrates to $1$. Hence:

$$
E[Z] = 0 + \mu \cdot 1 = \mu
$$

So the parameter $\mu$ in the Gaussian PDF is indeed the mean. Similarly, the variance $E[(Z - \mu)^2] = \sigma^2$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing the Gaussian PDF as $p(z) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2 \pi}}$ without the exponential ŌĆö the PDF is meaningless without the $e^{-(\cdot)}$ term.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **variance** $\sigma^2$ with **standard deviation** $\sigma$ in the units of the formula. KTU evaluators deduct marks for this.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Listing noise types without naming their *sources* (sensor, transmission, etc.). Always pair each noise type with its physical origin.

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö Module 1: The Image, Its Representation and Properties**

- **Image:** A 2D function $f(x, y)$; digital when $x$, $y$, and intensity are all finite and discrete.
- **Pixel:** The smallest element; coordinates $(x, y)$ with top-left origin.
- **Storage:** $B = M \cdot N \cdot k$ bits; for an 8-bit $1024 \times 1024$ image, $B = 8$ Mb $= 1$ MB.
- **Digitization = Sampling + Quantization** (the two are independent steps).
- **Sampling:** spatial discretization. Governed by the Nyquist theorem $f_s \ge 2 f_{\max}$.
- **Quantization:** amplitude discretization. $L = 2^k$ levels, step $\Delta = (I_{\max} - I_{\min}) / (L - 1)$.
- **Aliasing:** occurs when $f_s < 2 f_{\max}$; produces staircased diagonals and moir├® patterns.
- **Contouring:** occurs when $k$ is too small; produces false color bands.
- **Histograms:** $h(r_k) = n_k$ (raw), $p(r_k) = n_k / (MN)$ (normalized). Spatial info is **not** preserved.
- **Entropy:** $H = - \sum p(r_k) \log_2 p(r_k)$ bits/pixel; $H_{\max} = \log_2 L$.
- **Distances:** $D_e = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}$, $D_4 = \vert \Delta x \vert + \vert \Delta y \vert$, $D_8 = \max(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert)$.
- **Adjacency:** 4 (edge only), 8 (edge or corner), m (mixed; eliminates 8-adjacency ambiguity).
- **Neighborhoods:** $N_4$, $N_D$, $N_8 = N_4 \cup N_D$.
- **Regions & Boundaries:** Connected set = region; boundary = pixels of region with a neighbor outside.
- **Visual perception:** HVS has dynamic range $\approx 10^{10}$; rods (low light) vs cones (color); L, M, S cones; Mach bands; simultaneous contrast.
- **Image quality metrics:** MSE, PSNR (in dB), SNR (in dB).
- **Noise PDFs:** Gaussian, Uniform, Salt-and-Pepper, Rayleigh, Gamma, Exponential, Poisson (Shot).
- **Key pairs to remember on the answer sheet:**
  * Gaussian: $p(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma} e^{-(z - \mu)^2 / (2\sigma^2)}$, mean $= \mu$, variance $= \sigma^2$.
  * Uniform: mean $= (a+b)/2$, variance $= (b-a)^2 / 12$.
  * Rayleigh: mean $= a + \sqrt{\pi b / 4}$.
  * Exponential: mean $= 1/a$, variance $= 1/a^2$.
  * Gamma: mean $= b/a$, variance $= b/a^2$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner's Warning:** In the KTU ESE, students often lose 1ŌĆō2 marks per question for:
> (1) Not drawing the **block diagram** for image digitization.
> (2) Forgetting to convert the entropy **base** to log$_2$ (some texts use log base $e$ for nats; KTU uses **bits**).
> (3) Using $D_4 = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$ ŌĆö that is $D_e$, not $D_4$. Mix-up is fatal.
> (4) Stating "$\sigma$" when the question asks for variance ŌĆö units are $\text{noise}^2$, not noise.
> Memorize the **symbols, units, and boundary conditions** before entering the exam hall.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Color Images: Physics of color, Color perceived by humans, Color spaces, Color constancy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST636 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Color Images: Physics of color, Color perceived by humans, Color spaces, Color constancy # SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) In Digital Image Processing, **Color** is defined as the perceptual response of the human visual system (HVS) to the spectral power distribution (SPD) of visible electromagnetic radiation reflected from or emitted by an object, mathematically represented as a triplet of tristimulus values within a defined **color space**. A **Color Image** is a 2-D function $I(x, y, c)$ where $(x, y)$ are spatial coordinates and $c \in \{1, 2, ..., k\}$ denotes the spectral channel index (typically $k = 3$ for tri-chromatic images), with each channel capturing the intensity of a specific wavelength band. > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Highlight ŌĆö Module 1** > The 2024 Scheme expects students to master **four pillars**: (1) Physics of color, (2) Human color perception, (3) Color spaces & conversions, and (4) Color constancy mechanisms. Each pillar carries independent marks weightage in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE). ## 1.2 The Physics of Color ŌĆö What Actually IS Color? **Color is NOT a property of an object ŌĆö it is a property of light interacting with matter and our visual cortex.** Every object absorbs certain wavelengths of incident light and reflects the rest. The reflected wavelengths, when interpreted by our eyes and brain, give rise to the sensation we call "color". ### The Electromagnetic Spectrum Visible light occupies an extremely narrow band of the entire electromagnetic spectrum ŌĆö approximately **380 nm to 780 nm**. | Region | Wavelength Range | Color Sensation | |---|---|---| | Violet | 380 ŌĆō 450 nm | Shortest visible wavelength | | Blue | 450 ŌĆō 495 nm | Cool | | Green | 495 ŌĆō 570 nm | Mid-spectrum | | Yellow | 570 ŌĆō 590 nm | Warm | | Orange | 590 ŌĆō 620 nm | Warm | | Red | 620 ŌĆō 780 nm | Longest visible wavelength | The fundamental relationship governing light energy is the PlanckŌĆōEinstein equation: $$E = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$ where $E$ is photon energy (Joules), $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J┬Ęs is **Planck's constant**, $c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s is the speed of light, and $\lambda$ is the wavelength. > [!NOTE] **Key Insight for KTU Students** > Since $E \propto \frac{1}{\lambda}$, **violet light (shorter $\lambda$) carries MORE energy than red light (longer $\lambda$)**. This is why UV (below 380 nm) is ionizing and dangerous. ### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Color as Music" Analogy Imagine a piano (the **electromagnetic spectrum**). Only a small set of keys (380ŌĆō780 nm) can be heard by your ears (the **human eye**). Each key produces a different musical note (a **wavelength**). When multiple notes play together (e.g., sunlight = full orchestra, a banana = mostly yellow + green orchestra), your brain interprets the chord as a **single color perception**. Just as a song is not in the piano but in the listener's mind, **color is not on the object ŌĆö it is in the observer's perception**. ## 1.3 Color Perceived by Humans ŌĆö The Biological Pipeline Human color perception is a **three-stage pipeline**: 1. **Optical Stage**: Cornea + Lens focus light onto the **retina**. 2. **Photoreceptor Stage**: Two types of photoreceptors ŌĆö **Rods** (Ōēł 120 million, scotopic/low-light, no color) and **Cones** (Ōēł 6ŌĆō7 million, photopic/bright-light, color vision). 3. **Neural Stage**: Retinal ganglion cells ŌåÆ Lateral Geniculate Nucleus (LGN) ŌåÆ **Primary Visual Cortex (V1)** ŌåÆ higher cortical areas for conscious color perception. ### The Three Cone Types (Trichromacy) | Cone Type | Peak Sensitivity $\lambda_{\max}$ | Perceived Hue | Brain Label | |---|---|---|---| | **S-cones** (Short) | Ōēł 420 nm | Blue | Often called "B-cones" | | **M-cones** (Medium) | Ōēł 534 nm | Green | Often called "G-cones" | | **L-cones** (Long) | Ōēł 564 nm | Red | Often called "R-cones" | > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Hot Topic** > The **L-cones** peak at **564 nm** (yellow-green region), NOT at pure red (~650 nm). This is a common exam trap ŌĆö never assume cones match the "R, G, B" labels perfectly. ## 1.4 Color Constancy ŌĆö The "Magic Trick" of Vision **Color constancy** is the perceptual phenomenon whereby the HVS perceives the color of an object as relatively constant despite wide variations in illumination spectral content. A white shirt looks white under candlelight (orange-tinted), noon sunlight (yellow-white), and fluorescent office light (greenish-blue). > [!NOTE] **Engineering Implication** > This biological phenomenon is a **major challenge in computer vision** ŌĆö machine cameras do NOT have color constancy by default, making object recognition under varying illumination notoriously difficult. # SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Physics of Color ŌĆö Deeper Dive ### 2.1.1 Three Sources of Color in Physical Systems 1. **Direct Emission**: Sun, LEDs, lasers, fluorescent tubes (active sources). 2. **Selective Reflection/Transmission**: Pigmented objects (most natural colors). 3. **Scattering Phenomena**: Rayleigh scattering (sky = blue), Mie scattering (clouds = white). ### 2.1.2 Spectral Power Distribution (SPD) A light source is mathematically characterized by its **Spectral Power Distribution** $P(\lambda)$, which is a continuous function describing radiant energy per unit wavelength interval. A **blackbody radiator** at temperature $T$ (Kelvin) follows **Planck's radiation law**: $$P(\lambda, T) = \frac{2hc^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{\frac{hc}{\lambda k_B T}} - 1}$$ where $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K is **Boltzmann's constant**. This is why heated objects change color with temperature: a blacksmith's iron glows **red ŌåÆ orange ŌåÆ yellow ŌåÆ white** as $T$ increases. ### 2.1.3 Why Objects Have Color For an object with surface reflectance $S(\lambda)$ illuminated by SPD $P(\lambda)$, the light reflected to the eye is: $$R(\lambda) = P(\lambda) \cdot S(\lambda)$$ The object's perceived color depends on the **integral** of $R(\lambda)$ weighted by the three cone response functions $L(\lambda), M(\lambda), S(\lambda)$ ŌĆö the **tristimulus values**. ## 2.2 Human Color Perception ŌĆö Deep Theory ### 2.2.1 Tristimulus Theory of Color Vision The HVS compresses the continuous SPD into just three numbers per pixel: $$\begin{aligned} X_{\text{tristimulus}} &= \int_{\lambda} P(\lambda) \cdot S(\lambda) \cdot \bar{x}(\lambda) \, d\lambda \\ Y_{\text{tristimulus}} &= \int_{\lambda} P(\lambda) \cdot S(\lambda) \cdot \bar{y}(\lambda) \, d\lambda \\ Z_{\text{tristimulus}} &= \int_{\lambda} P(\lambda) \cdot S(\lambda) \cdot \bar{z}(\lambda) \, d\lambda \end{aligned}$$ where $\bar{x}(\lambda), \bar{y}(\lambda), \bar{z}(\lambda)$ are the **CIE 1931 Standard Observer Color Matching Functions**. > [!NOTE] The CIE (Commission Internationale de l'├ēclairage) standardized the human observer in 1931 using experiments with a $2┬░$ field of view. ### 2.2.2 Opponent Process Theory (Hering's Theory) While trichromacy explains color detection at the **receptor level**, opponent process theory explains signal encoding in the **retinal ganglion cells and LGN**: - **Luminance channel**: $L + M$ (blackŌĆōwhite) - **RedŌĆōGreen channel**: $L - M$ (no reddish-green exists) - **BlueŌĆōYellow channel**: $S - (L+M)$ (no bluish-yellow exists) > [!IMPORTANT] This is the **biological foundation** of color spaces like CIE L\*a\*b\*, where $L^*$ is luminance and $a^*, b^*$ are opponent chromatic axes. ## 2.3 Color Spaces ŌĆö The Engineering Toolkit A **color space** is a mathematical model for representing colors as tuples of numbers (typically 3 or 4 components). ### 2.3.1 Categorization of Color Spaces | Family | Examples | Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Hardware-oriented** | RGB, CMY/CMYK | Cameras, monitors, printers | | **Application-oriented** | YCbCr, YUV | Video compression (JPEG, MPEG) | | **Human-perceptual** | HSI, HSV, HSL | Image editing, artist tools | | **CIE Standard** | CIE XYZ, L\*a\*b\*, L\*u\*v\* | Color science, ICC profiles | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Conversion | Formula | Constraints / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Photon Energy | $E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$ | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J┬Ęs | | 2 | RGB ŌåÆ Intensity (HSI) | $I = \frac{R + G + B}{3}$ | Range: $[0, 1]$ if normalized | | 3 | RGB ŌåÆ Saturation (HSI) | $S = 1 - \frac{3 \cdot \min(R,G,B)}{R + G + B}$ | Valid when $R+G+B \neq 0$ | | 4 | RGB ŌåÆ Hue (HSI) | $H = \cos^{-1}\!\left[\frac{0.5[(R-G)+(R-B)]}{\sqrt{(R-G)^2 + (R-B)(G-B)}}\right]$ | Use $H = 360┬░ - H$ if $B > G$ | | 5 | CMY ŌåÆ RGB | $R = 1 - C,\ G = 1 - M,\ B = 1 - Y$ | Normalized $[0,1]$ range | | 6 | RGB ŌåÆ YCbCr (BT.601) | $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ | $Y \in [16, 235]$ | | 7 | RGB ŌåÆ YCbCr (Chroma) | $C_b = 0.564(B-Y) + 128$ | $C_b, C_r \in [16, 240]$ | | 8 | RGB ŌåÆ YCbCr (Chroma) | $C_r = 0.713(R-Y) + 128$ | Same range as above | | 9 | CMYK Key | $K = \min(C, M, Y)$ | $K \in [0,1]$ | | 10 | CIE L\* (Lightness) | $L^* = 116 \cdot \left(\frac{Y}{Y_n}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}} - 16$ | For $Y / Y_n > 0.008856$ | | 11 | Von Kries Chromatic Adaptation | $\begin{bmatrix} R' \\ G' \\ B' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} R/R_w \\ G/G_w \\ B/B_w \end{bmatrix}$ | $R_w, G_w, B_w$ are illuminant whites | > [!IMPORTANT] **Absolute Value Rule** > Whenever you write $\vert x \vert$ in any derivation, use the LaTeX command `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` ŌĆö never use the pipe character `|` directly, as it breaks markdown table rendering. # SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: RGB ŌåÆ HSI Color Conversion The HSI (Hue, Saturation, Intensity) model decouples chromaticity (hue, saturation) from intensity, making it ideal for image processing algorithms that operate on color independently of brightness. ### 3.1.1 Intensity Component (Trivial Case) Intensity is the arithmetic mean of the three normalized RGB channels: $$I = \frac{R + G + B}{3}$$ **Logic**: This matches the luminance-weighted average as a first-order approximation. The factor 1/3 ensures $I \in [0,1]$ when $R,G,B$ are normalized. ### 3.1.2 Saturation Component Saturation measures the **degree of colorfulness** ŌĆö the ratio of chromatic content to total energy. **Step 1**: The minimum channel value represents the achromatic (gray) component: $$m = \min(R, G, B)$$ **Step 2**: The sum represents the total radiant energy: $$S_{sum} = R + G + B$$ **Step 3**: Saturation is the normalized deviation from pure gray: $$S = 1 - \frac{3m}{S_{sum}}$$ **Logic Explanation**: - If all three channels are equal (achromatic gray), then $m = R = G = B$, so $3m = S_{sum}$, giving $S = 0$ (no color). - If two channels are zero, $m = 0$, giving $S = 1$ (pure color, fully saturated). ### 3.1.3 Hue Component ŌĆö Full Derivation Hue represents the dominant wavelength of the color. It is computed via a dot-product analogy in the chromatic plane. **Step 1**: Define the chromaticity difference vector from white: $$\Delta_{RG} = R - G, \quad \Delta_{RB} = R - B, \quad \Delta_{GB} = G - B$$ **Step 2**: Construct the numerator (numerator of cosine formula) ŌĆö a measure of "red-ness": $$N = 0.5 \cdot \big[ (R - G) + (R - B) \big] = 0.5 \cdot (2R - G - B)$$ **Step 3**: Construct the denominator ŌĆö the magnitude of the chromaticity vector: $$D = \sqrt{(R - G)^2 + (R - B)(G - B)}$$ **Step 4**: The angle (in degrees) gives hue: $$H = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{N}{D}\right)$$ **Step 5**: Correct the quadrant ŌĆö if $B > G$, the hue lies in the complementary range: $$H_{\text{final}} = \begin{cases} H & \text{if } B \leq G \\ 360┬░ - H & \text{if } B > G \end{cases}$$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö RGB to HSI **Given**: Pixel with normalized RGB = $(0.6, 0.2, 0.2)$ (a saturated red). **Step 1 ŌĆö Intensity**: $$I = \frac{0.6 + 0.2 + 0.2}{3} = \frac{1.0}{3} \approx 0.333$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Minimum & Sum**: $$m = \min(0.6, 0.2, 0.2) = 0.2, \quad S_{sum} = 1.0$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Saturation**: $$S = 1 - \frac{3 \times 0.2}{1.0} = 1 - 0.6 = 0.4$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Hue Numerator**: $$N = 0.5 \times \big[ (0.6 - 0.2) + (0.6 - 0.2) \big] = 0.5 \times (0.4 + 0.4) = 0.4$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Hue Denominator**: $$D = \sqrt{(0.6 - 0.2)^2 + (0.6 - 0.2)(0.2 - 0.2)} = \sqrt{0.16 + 0} = 0.4$$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Hue Angle**: $$H = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.4}{0.4}\right) = \cos^{-1}(1) = 0┬░$$ Since $B = 0.2 \leq G = 0.2$, no quadrant correction needed. **Final HSI**: $(H, S, I) = (0┬░, 0.4, 0.333)$ ŌĆö confirms a **pure red** at low intensity. Ō£ģ ## 3.3 Derivation: RGB ŌåÆ YCbCr Conversion (ITU-R BT.601) YCbCr separates **luma (Y)** from **chroma (Cb, Cr)** ŌĆö essential for compression (JPEG, MPEG, H.264) because human eyes are more sensitive to luma than chroma. ### 3.3.1 Luma Y Derivation Luma is a weighted sum that mimics the HVS's luminance perception. Empirically determined weights: $$\begin{aligned} Y &= 0.299 R + 0.587 G + 0.114 B \end{aligned}$$ **Logic**: The eye is most sensitive to green (Ōēł 58.7%), then red (Ōēł 29.9%), least to blue (Ōēł 11.4%). These weights sum to 1. ### 3.3.2 Chroma Blue-Difference (Cb) Derivation Cb represents the blue-yellow chromatic axis (opponent-process inspired): $$\begin{aligned} C_b &= 0.564 (B - Y) + 128 \\ &= 0.564 B - 0.564(0.299 R + 0.587 G + 0.114 B) + 128 \\ &= -0.169 R - 0.331 G + 0.500 B + 128 \end{aligned}$$ The +128 offset centers the chroma range at 128 (for 8-bit storage). ### 3.3.3 Chroma Red-Difference (Cr) Derivation $$\begin{aligned} C_r &= 0.713 (R - Y) + 128 \\ &= 0.500 R - 0.419 G - 0.081 B + 128 \end{aligned}$$ ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö RGB to YCbCr **Given**: Pixel RGB (8-bit) = $(255, 128, 64)$ ŌĆö orange. **Step 1 ŌĆö Luma**: $$Y = 0.299(255) + 0.587(128) + 0.114(64) = 76.245 + 75.136 + 7.296 = 158.68$$ Round to 159. **Verify**: $Y \in [16, 235]$? 159 Ōłł range Ō£ģ **Step 2 ŌĆö Cb**: $$C_b = 0.564(64 - 158.68) + 128 = 0.564(-94.68) + 128 = -53.40 + 128 = 74.60$$ Round to 75. **Verify**: $C_b \in [16, 240]$? 75 Ōłł range Ō£ģ **Step 3 ŌĆö Cr**: $$C_r = 0.713(255 - 158.68) + 128 = 0.713(96.32) + 128 = 68.68 + 128 = 196.68$$ Round to 197. **Verify**: $C_r \in [16, 240]$? 197 Ōłł range Ō£ģ **Final YCbCr**: $(Y, C_b, C_r) = (159, 75, 197)$ ŌĆö high Cr confirms orange/red dominance. Ō£ģ ## 3.5 Derivation: Von Kries Color Constancy Model The simplest chromatic adaptation transform. Assumes each cone channel can be **independently scaled** to discount the illuminant. ### 3.5.1 Illuminant Estimation Assume a "white patch" or use the **Gray World Assumption** (average scene reflectance is gray): $$R_w = \alpha \cdot \bar{R}, \quad G_w = \alpha \cdot \bar{G}, \quad B_w = \alpha \cdot \bar{B}$$ where $\bar{R}, \bar{G}, \bar{B}$ are scene averages and $\alpha$ is a scale factor (often 1 or based on white patch intensity). ### 3.5.2 Channel-wise Gain Compensation Each pixel's color is divided by the illuminant estimate: $$\begin{aligned} R'(x,y) &= \frac{R(x,y)}{R_w} \\ G'(x,y) &= \frac{G(x,y)}{G_w} \\ B'(x,y) &= \frac{B(x,y)}{B_w} \end{aligned}$$ **Logic**: If the illuminant casts a yellow tint, $R_w$ and $G_w$ are large, so we divide them down ŌĆö effectively boosting $B'$ to restore white balance. The result is a **canonical (illuminant-independent)** image. ### 3.5.3 Re-normalization Multiply by the canonical white point $(255, 255, 255)$ to scale back to display range: $$R'' = 255 \cdot R', \quad G'' = 255 \cdot G', \quad B'' = 255 \cdot B'$$ # SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Color Imaging Pipeline ŌĆö Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[ILLUMINANT LIGHT SOURCE
SPD P lambda] --> B[OBJECT REFLECTANCE
S lambda] B --> C[REFLECTED SPECTRUM
R lambda = P x S] C --> D[OPTICAL SYSTEM
Lens and Aperture] D --> E[SENSOR ARRAY
Bayer Filter RGB CFA] E --> F[DEMOSAICING
Interpolate Missing Pixels] F --> G[WHITE BALANCE
Von Kries Adaptation] G --> H[COLOR SPACE CONVERSION
RGB to YCbCr] H --> I[OUTPUT IMAGE
Display JPEG Video] J[HUMAN VISUAL SYSTEM
Reference Observer] -.-> K[CIE 1931 STANDARD
Color Matching] K -.-> H ``` **Diagram Reading Guide**: - **Solid arrows** = image data flow (sensed light ŌåÆ digital pixels). - **Dotted arrows** = standards reference (the HVS defines the target colorimetric accuracy). - Each block performs a specific transformation; failure at any stage causes color artifacts (e.g., wrong white balance = color cast, incorrect demosaicing = color aliasing). ## 4.2 Color Space Family Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD ROOT[ALL COLOR SPACES] --> HW[HW ORIENTED] ROOT --> APP[APPLICATION ORIENTED] ROOT --> PER[PERCEPTUAL ORIENTED] ROOT --> CIE[CIE STANDARD] HW --> RGB[RGB] HW --> CMYK[CMY and CMYK] APP --> YCBCR[YCbCr] APP --> YUV[YUV] APP --> YIQ[YIQ] PER --> HSI[HSI] PER --> HSV[HSV] PER --> HSL[HSL] CIE --> XYZ[CIE XYZ] CIE --> LAB[CIE L*a*b*] CIE --> LUV[CIE L*u*v*] RGB --> X1[Hardware Pixel
Sensor Monitor] CMYK --> X2[Print Industry
Offset Press] YCBCR --> X3[JPEG MPEG
H.264 HEVC] HSI --> X4[Image Editing
Photoshop GIMP] LAB --> X5[ICC Profiles
Color Science] ``` ## 4.3 CIE Chromaticity Diagram (Conceptual Block Map) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SPECTRAL_LOCUS[SPECTRAL LOCUS BOUNDARY] direction LR WAVE380[380 nm Violet] --> WAVE450[450 nm Blue] WAVE450 --> WAVE495[495 nm Cyan] WAVE495 --> WAVE570[570 nm Green] WAVE570 --> WAVE590[590 nm Yellow] WAVE590 --> WAVE620[620 nm Orange] WAVE620 --> WAVE780[780 nm Red] end WAVE780 --> PURPLE[PURPLE LINE
Non Spectral] PURPLE --> WAVE380 WHITEDOT((WHITE POINT
Illuminant D65)) -.-> CENTER[CHROMATICITY DIAGRAM INTERIOR] ``` **Note to KTU students**: The CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram is a 2-D projection of the 3-D XYZ color space. The curved boundary = pure spectral colors; the straight closing line = non-spectral purples (mixtures of red + violet). ## 4.4 Human Visual System ŌĆö Cone Response Curves (Functional Map) ```mermaid flowchart LR LIGHT[White Light Incident] --> CORNEA[Cornea and Lens] CORNEA --> RETINA[Retina Photoreceptors] RETINA --> RODS[Rods
120 Million
Scotopic
No Color] RETINA --> CONES[Cones
6 Million
Photopic
Trichromatic] CONES --> SCONE[S Cones
Peak 420 nm
Blue] CONES --> MCONE[M Cones
Peak 534 nm
Green] CONES --> LCONE[L Cones
Peak 564 nm
Red] SCONE --> LGN[LGN Thalamus] MCONE --> LGN LCONE --> LGN RODS --> LGN LGN --> V1[Primary Visual Cortex V1] V1 --> V4[Color Area V4
Conscious Perception] ``` # SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]` **Q: Differentiate between additive and subtractive color models. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer**: | Feature | Additive (RGB) | Subtractive (CMY/CMYK) | |---|---|---| | **Mechanism** | Colors added to **black** (no light) | Colors subtract from **white** (full light) | | **Primary Colors** | Red, Green, Blue | Cyan, Magenta, Yellow (+ Black) | | **Mixing Result** | All primaries = **White** | All primaries = **Black** (in theory) | | **Application** | Monitors, TVs, scanners | Printing presses, photography | | **Light Behavior** | Emitted light | Reflected/transmitted light | **Mnemonic**: "Additive **A**dds light; Subtractive **S**ubtracts light." *(Marks: 1 definition + 1 comparison table + 1 example = 3 marks)* ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]` **Q: What is color constancy? Why is it a challenge in computer vision?** **Model Answer**: **Definition**: Color constancy is the perceptual ability of the HVS to perceive the color of an object as nearly invariant under varying illumination conditions. **Challenge in Computer Vision**: 1. Cameras do not possess biological adaptation mechanisms. 2. The recorded RGB values depend on $P(\lambda) \cdot S(\lambda)$ ŌĆö separating these two is **ill-posed**. 3. Algorithms (Gray World, White Patch, Retinex) must **estimate** the illuminant without prior knowledge. 4. Errors lead to color casts (e.g., indoor photos appearing yellow/orange). *(Marks: 1 definition + 2 challenge points = 3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper]` **Q: (a)** Explain the physics of color with reference to the electromagnetic spectrum and the SPD of light. Discuss why the visible range is 380ŌĆō780 nm. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the HVS pipeline. Explain the role of cones and rods, with emphasis on trichromatic theory. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) [7 Marks] **1. Electromagnetic Spectrum Basics [2 marks]**: - EM spectrum spans from cosmic rays ($\lambda < 10^{-12}$ m) to radio waves ($\lambda > 10^3$ m). - Visible light occupies 380ŌĆō780 nm, located between UV and IR. - Energy relationship: $E = hc / \lambda$. **2. Physics of Color Generation [3 marks]**: - Light sources emit photons with characteristic SPD $P(\lambda)$. - Planck's blackbody law governs thermal sources: $P(\lambda, T) = \frac{2hc^2}{\lambda^5 (e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1)}$. - Reflected light from objects: $R(\lambda) = P(\lambda) \cdot S(\lambda)$. - Color sensation arises from integration of $R(\lambda)$ over cone sensitivities. **3. Why 380ŌĆō780 nm [2 marks]**: - Below 380 nm (UV): photons carry enough energy to damage biological tissues; cornea/lens absorb them. - Above 780 nm (IR): photon energy too low to trigger photopigment isomerization (the chemical basis of vision). - Cone opsins (photopsins) have absorption spectra that match this exact window. #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) [7 Marks] **1. HVS Block Diagram [2 marks]** (refer to Section 4.4 above). **2. Cones and Rods [3 marks]**: | Feature | Cones | Rods | |---|---|---| | Quantity | ~6ŌĆō7 million | ~120 million | | Location | Fovea (high density) | Retinal periphery | | Sensitivity | Bright light (photopic) | Low light (scotopic) | | Color vision | Yes (trichromatic) | No | | Acuity | High | Low | | Photopigment | Iodopsin (3 types) | Rhodopsin (1 type) | **3. Trichromatic Theory [2 marks]**: - YoungŌĆōHelmholtz hypothesis (1802). - Three cone types: S (420 nm), M (534 nm), L (564 nm). - All perceived colors = linear combination of these three responses. - Foundation of CIE XYZ, RGB, and modern colorimetry. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper]` **Q: (a)** Define a color space. Compare the RGB, CMY, HSI, and YCbCr color models with their equations and applications. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** What is color constancy? Explain the Von Kries chromatic adaptation model and Gray World Assumption in detail. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) [7 Marks] **1. Definition [1 mark]**: A color space is a coordinate system that specifies how colors are represented as tuples of numerical values, providing a reproducible method to describe colors. **2. Comparison Table [4 marks]**: | Space | Type | Key Equation | Application | |---|---|---|---| | **RGB** | Additive | Direct hardware pixel | Monitors, cameras | | **CMY/CMYK** | Subtractive | $R = 1 - C$, $K = \min(C,M,Y)$ | Print industry | | **HSI** | Perceptual | $I = (R+G+B)/3$ | Image editing | | **YCbCr** | Luma-Chroma | $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ | JPEG, video | **3. Application Discussion [2 marks]**: YCbCr dominates compression because the eye is less sensitive to high-frequency chroma ŌåÆ chroma can be subsampled (4:2:0) without visible quality loss. RGB dominates hardware; HSI dominates interactive editing; CMYK dominates print. #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) [7 Marks] **1. Color Constancy Definition [1 mark]**: Already covered in Q2 above. **2. Von Kries Model [4 marks]**: - **Assumption**: Each cone channel can be independently scaled. - **Step 1**: Estimate illuminant (e.g., via white patch or Gray World). - **Step 2**: For each pixel $(x,y)$, compute adapted values: - $R'(x,y) = R(x,y) / R_w$ - $G'(x,y) = G(x,y) / G_w$ - $B'(x,y) = B(x,y) / B_w$ - **Step 3**: Re-normalize to display range. **3. Gray World Assumption [2 marks]**: - **Hypothesis**: The average reflectance of a natural scene is achromatic (gray). - **Implication**: The scene's mean color represents the illuminant tint. - **Formula**: $R_w = k \cdot \bar{R}$ (where $\bar{R}$ is mean of red channel, $k$ a scaling constant). - **Limitation**: Fails in scenes dominated by a single color (e.g., forest, beach). > [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Cone peak confusion**: Students often write "L-cones peak at 650 nm (red)" ŌĆö WRONG. Correct is **564 nm (yellow-green)**. Marks deducted. > 2. **YCbCr coefficient error**: Mixing up $C_b$ and $C_r$ formulas. Remember: $C_r$ involves $(R - Y)$, $C_b$ involves $(B - Y)$. Blue ŌłÆ Luma. > 3. **HSI hue quadrant correction**: Forgetting to apply $H = 360┬░ - H$ when $B > G$. Answer will be wrong by a factor of 2. > 4. **Color constancy confusion**: Writing that cameras perform color constancy automatically ŌĆö they require explicit algorithms. > 5. **Mixing additive/subtractive primaries**: CMY uses Cyan/Magenta/Yellow, NOT Red/Green/Blue. Easy slip. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Color Images (Module 1)** ### Physics of Color - [ ] Visible spectrum: **380ŌĆō780 nm** (violet ŌåÆ red). - [ ] Energy equation: $E = hc/\lambda$, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J┬Ęs. - [ ] SPD ├Ś Reflectance = Reflected light ŌåÆ integrated by cones. - [ ] Color is a **perception**, not a physical property of objects. ### Human Color Perception - [ ] **Cones**: 3 types (S = 420 nm, M = 534 nm, L = 564 nm). Photopic. Trichromatic. - [ ] **Rods**: ~120 million. Scotopic. No color. High sensitivity. - [ ] **CIE 1931 Standard Observer**: $2┬░$ field of view, color matching functions $\bar{x}, \bar{y}, \bar{z}$. - [ ] **Opponent Process Theory**: L+M (luminance), LŌłÆM (redŌĆōgreen), SŌłÆ(L+M) (blueŌĆōyellow). - [ ] Tristimulus values integrate SPD ├Ś reflectance ├Ś matching functions. ### Color Spaces - [ ] **RGB**: Additive, hardware, normalized $[0,1]$ or 8-bit $[0,255]$. - [ ] **CMY/CMYK**: Subtractive, print, $K = \min(C,M,Y)$. - [ ] **HSI**: $I = (R+G+B)/3$, $S = 1 - 3\min/(R+G+B)$, $H = \cos^{-1}$ formula. - [ ] **YCbCr**: $Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$; chroma centered at 128. - [ ] **CIE L\*a\*b\***: Perceptually uniform, opponent-based, used in ICC profiles. ### Color Constancy - [ ] Definition: Stable perception under varying illumination. - [ ] **Von Kries**: Channel-wise scaling $R' = R/R_w$. - [ ] **Gray World**: Scene average = illuminant estimate. - [ ] **White Patch**: Brightest patch = illuminant estimate. - [ ] **Retinex (Land)**: Local spatial comparisons. - [ ] **Challenge**: Ill-posed problem; no unique solution. ### Exam-Specific Tips - [ ] Always **derive** before plugging numbers (KTU 2024 scheme marks derivation steps). - [ ] Use `\vert` for absolute value, never pipe `|`. - [ ] Always state the **range** of output values in conversion questions. - [ ] When asked about cones, mention the **actual peak wavelengths**, not generic "red/green/blue". - [ ] For color constancy, mention **at least 2 methods** with limitations.

topics.title: Data Structures for Image Analysis: Levels of image data representation, Traditional image data structures - matrices, Chains, Topological data structures - Relational structures, Hierarchical Data Structures, Pyramids, Quadtrees, Other pyramidal structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST636 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Structures for Image Analysis: Levels of image data representation, Traditional image data structures - matrices, Chains, Topological data structures - Relational structures, Hierarchical Data Structures, Pyramids, Quadtrees, Other pyramidal structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Why We Need Specialized Data Structures for Images

A digital image is not just a flat array of numbers; it is a **2D (or 3D for volumetric)** discrete signal that must be stored, accessed, and manipulated efficiently. Choosing the *right* data structure determines whether an algorithm runs in **milliseconds** or **hours**, and whether the application fits in **RAM** or requires off-chip storage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECST636 Module 1):**
> *Image data structures describe how the pixels (or higher-level primitives such as regions, edges, and objects) are organized in memory. They bridge the gap between the raw image acquisition data and the symbolic/algorithmic processing required for tasks like segmentation, feature extraction, and object recognition.*

## 1.2 Levels of Image Data Representation

Gonzalez & Woods (the canonical reference for KTU DIP) define **four ascending levels of representation**. Each level adds semantic meaning and reduces the data volume, but loses direct pixel-level fidelity.

| Level | Name | Primitive | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 1** | Pixel Level | Intensity values $f(x,y)$ | Raw acquisition, filtering |
| **Level 2** | Local / Edge Level | Edges, line segments, curves | Boundary detection |
| **Level 3** | Regional Level | Homogeneous regions, connected components | Segmentation |
| **Level 4** | Object / Symbolic Level | Meaningful objects, semantic labels | Recognition, scene understanding |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Exam:** Going from Level 1 ŌåÆ Level 4 means **less data, more meaning**. The data structure chosen for each level differs dramatically.

### Conceptual Analogy ­¤Ś║’ĖÅ

Imagine you are looking at a **city on Google Maps**:
- **Level 1 (Pixels):** The full satellite image ŌĆö every grain of sand is visible. Huge data.
- **Level 2 (Edges):** Only the roads, rivers, and building outlines are drawn.
- **Level 3 (Regions):** The map is colored by zone (park, residential, commercial).
- **Level 4 (Objects):** Labels appear: "City Hall", "Central Park", "Mall Road".

Just as Google pre-computes lower-resolution tiles, **hierarchical data structures (pyramids, quadtrees)** mirror this multi-resolution approach.

## 1.3 The Three Major Families of Image Data Structures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 explicitly groups data structures into three families:**

### A. Traditional Data Structures
- **Matrices** ŌĆö direct raster storage.
- **Chains** ŌĆö boundary-following codes (Freeman chain code, crack code).
- **Topological data structures** ŌĆö region adjacency and containment relations.

### B. Relational (Topological) Structures
- **Region Adjacency Graphs (RAG):** nodes = regions, edges = adjacency.
- They capture *spatial relationships* without storing every pixel.

### C. Hierarchical Data Structures
- **Pyramids:** Multi-resolution stacks (Gaussian pyramid, Laplacian pyramid).
- **Quadtrees:** Recursive 2D decomposition.
- **Other Pyramidal Structures:** BSP-trees, K-d trees, Bintrees.

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU exam trap: *Chains are classified under Traditional structures, not Topological.* Topological structures refer specifically to **relational** data structures that preserve neighborhood and adjacency information abstractly.

---

## 1.4 Formal Definition Block

> **Definition 1 ŌĆö Image Data Structure:**
> An image data structure is a formal scheme for organizing image primitives (pixels, edges, regions, or objects) in computer memory such that operations of interest (access, traversal, query) are supported in optimal time and space complexity.

> **Definition 2 ŌĆö Level of Representation:**
> The level of representation is the *degree of symbolic abstraction* applied to image primitives, ranging from raw intensity values (Level 1) to semantically labeled objects (Level 4).

> **Definition 3 ŌĆö Traditional Data Structure:**
> A data structure that directly stores either the pixel raster (matrix) or its 1D traversal (chains), preserving the original 2D neighborhood through indexing only.

> **Definition 4 ŌĆö Topological Data Structure:**
> A data structure that stores the **spatial relationships** (adjacency, containment, boundary) between image entities using graphs, with the actual geometry held separately or implicitly.

> **Definition 5 ŌĆö Hierarchical Data Structure:**
> A data structure that organizes image data in **multiple resolution levels** using recursive decomposition, enabling coarse-to-fine analysis and logarithmic-time queries.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Level 1 ŌĆö Pixel-Level (Matrix Representation)

The most straightforward storage scheme. For a **grayscale image** of size $M \times N$:
- Memory required: $M \times N \times b$ bits, where $b$ = bits per pixel.
- For RGB color: $3 \times M \times N \times b$ bits.

### Why matrices are insufficient alone
1. **Fixed resolution:** Cannot zoom out without re-sampling.
2. **No region semantics:** Every pixel is treated equally.
3. **Wasted storage on uniform regions:** A 1-million pixel sky stores the same intensity $10^6$ times.

## 2.2 Level 2 ŌĆö Chain Codes (Borders)

A **Freeman chain code** encodes the boundary of a region as a sequence of directional symbols $\in \{0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7\}$ for 8-connectivity, requiring **3 bits per boundary pixel**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Differential Chain Code (DCC) further compresses by storing the *change* in direction, typically requiring only **2 bits per transition** in many practical cases ŌĆö a frequent exam question.

### Chain Code Variants
- **4-directional:** $\{0,1,2,3\}$ ŌĆö 2 bits/direction. Used for orthogonal boundaries.
- **8-directional (Freeman):** $\{0,\dots,7\}$ ŌĆö 3 bits/direction.
- **Crack code:** Walks along pixel *edges* (cracks) ŌĆö useful for area computation.

## 2.3 Topological Data Structures ŌĆö Region Adjacency Graph (RAG)

A **Region Adjacency Graph (RAG)** is a graph $G = (V, E)$ where:
- $V$ = set of regions (connected components) in the segmented image.
- $E$ = set of edges; $(R_i, R_j) \in E$ iff regions $R_i$ and $R_j$ share a boundary.

RAGs are **lossy w.r.t. geometry** ŌĆö they capture *which* regions touch, not the exact shape.

## 2.4 Hierarchical Data Structures ŌĆö Pyramids & Quadtrees

### 2.4.1 Image Pyramids
A pyramid is a stack of images $\{I_0, I_1, \dots, I_L\}$ where:
- $I_0$ = full resolution (Level 0).
- $I_{k+1}$ = reduced-resolution version of $I_k$ (typically by factor 2 in each dimension).
- $L = \log_2(\min(M,N))$ levels for a $2^L \times 2^L$ base.

> **Types of Pyramids (KTU Frequently Asked):**
> 1. **Gaussian (M-pyramid):** Low-pass filter + down-sample at each step.
> 2. **Laplacian (L-pyramid):** Band-pass; stores the difference between successive Gaussian levels. Enables perfect reconstruction.
> 3. **M-typed pyramid:** Each level is a **mean** of the level below.
> 4. **F-pyramid (Filter pyramid):** A general class obtained by any linear filter $w$.

### 2.4.2 Quadtrees
A quadtree recursively partitions a 2D region into **four equal quadrants** (NW, NE, SW, SE) until a stopping criterion is met (e.g., uniform color, minimum size, or depth limit).

| Quadtree Variant | Stopping Criterion |
|---|---|
| **Region Quadtree** | All pixels in a node are of the same color (uniform region). |
| **MX Quadtree** | Node subdivided while it contains more than one color. Used for binary images / line drawings. |
| **Point Quadtree** | Stores a set of points; subdivided based on point distribution. |
| **Edge Quadtree** | Subdivides to isolate edges. |

### 2.4.3 Other Pyramidal Structures
- **BSP Trees (Binary Space Partitioning):** Recursively split space by a hyperplane (line in 2D) ŌĆö flexible but not axis-aligned.
- **K-d Trees:** Cycle through dimensions; useful for point queries and feature matching.
- **Bintrees:** A binary version of quadtrees for image data.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ­¤ōŗ

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is HIGH-YIELD ŌĆö memorize for KTU 2024 ESE/IA.**

| Concept | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Matrix storage (grayscale) | $S_{mat} = M \cdot N \cdot b / 8$ | $M,N$ = rows, cols; $b$ = bits/pixel | Bytes |
| Matrix storage (RGB) | $S_{rgb} = 3 \cdot M \cdot N \cdot b / 8$ | ŌĆö | Bytes |
| Chain code bits (8-conn) | $B_{cc} = 3 \cdot N_b$ | $N_b$ = number of boundary pixels | bits |
| DCC bits | $B_{dcc} \le 2 \cdot N_b$ | (avg. of 2 bits/transition) | bits |
| Pyramid total levels | $L = \lfloor \log_2 \min(M,N) \rfloor + 1$ | ŌĆö | levels |
| Pyramid total size | $S_{pyr} = S_{I_0} \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{L-1} 4^{-k} = \tfrac{4}{3} S_{I_0}$ | $S_{I_0}$ = base level size | Bytes |
| Quadtree node count (worst case) | $N_q \le 1 + 4 + 16 + \dots + 4^L = \tfrac{4^{L+1}-1}{3}$ | $L$ = max depth | nodes |
| Region Quadtree size (uniform image) | $N_q = 1$ | 1 root node | nodes |
| Quadtree memory per node | $m_q \approx 5$ (4 pointers + 1 attribute byte) | ŌĆö | words |
| Quad split count from $M \times N$ image | $n_{splits} \le \min(M,N)^2$ | ŌĆö | splits |
| Area from crack code | $A = \sum_{k} \Delta a_k$ | $\Delta a_k \in \{0, \tfrac{1}{2}, 1\}$ | pixel units |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important beyond the exam?**
> - **Medical imaging (CT/MRI):** Quadtrees enable **DICOM compression** ŌĆö uniform tissue regions stored as a single node.
> - **GIS / Google Maps:** Tiled pyramids (Web Mercator) are the de-facto industry standard; the math is exactly the same as a Gaussian pyramid.
> - **Computer vision pipelines (YOLO, OpenCV):** Coarse-to-fine detection traverses image pyramids to find objects at multiple scales ŌĆö invariant to object size.
> - **VLSI / PCB inspection:** MX-quadtrees store binary mask images of circuit layouts.
> - **Game engines:** BSP-trees accelerate **ray-tracing and visibility culling** in 3D rendering.
