```mermaid
    D --> E[Noise Analysis Stage]
    E --> F{Noise Type Identification}
    F -->|Gaussian Uniform| G[Spatial Domain Filters]
    F -->|Salt and Pepper Impulse| H[Order Statistic Filters]
    F -->|Periodic Sinusoidal| I[Frequency Domain Notch Filters]
    G --> J[Restored Image f hat x y]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Quality Metrics PSNR SSIM]
```

### 4.2 Decision Tree ŌĆö Filter Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observe Noise Pattern] --> B{Is noise additive independent}
    B -->|No| C[Hard Restoration Problem]
    B -->|Yes| D{Is noise spatial or periodic}
    D -->|Periodic| E[Transform to Frequency Domain]
    E --> F[Apply Notch or Band Reject Filter]
    F --> G[Inverse FFT]
    D -->|Spatial| H{Is noise Gaussian}
    H -->|Yes| I{Is edge preservation critical}
    H -->|No| J{Is it impulse noise}
    I -->|Yes| K[Adaptive Wiener Filter]
    I -->|No| L[Arithmetic or Geometric Mean]
    J -->|Yes| M[Median or Alpha Trimmed]
    J -->|No| N[Specialised PDF matched filter]
    G --> O[Restored Image Output]
    K --> O
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow ŌĆö Periodic Noise Removal

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Degraded Image g x y] --> B[Compute 2D FFT]
    B --> C[Log Magnitude Spectrum]
    C --> D[Detect Bright Spots Periodic Noise]
    D --> E[Define Notch Centres u0 v0 and u1 v1]
    E --> F[Construct Notch Reject H u v]
    F --> G[Multiply G u v with H u v]
    G --> H[Inverse 2D FFT]
    H --> I[Real Part Output]
    I --> J[Restored Image f hat x y]
```

### 4.4 Hierarchy ŌĆö Order Statistic Filter Family

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Order Statistic Filters] --> B[Median Filter]
    A --> C[Max and Min Filters]
    A --> D[Midpoint Filter]
    A --> E[Alpha Trimmed Mean Filter]
    B --> F[Salt and Pepper Removal]
    C --> G[Salt Removal via Min]
    C --> H[Pepper Removal via Max]
    D --> I[Gaussian and Uniform Noise]
    E --> J[Mixed Impulse plus Gaussian]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024]**

Define image degradation. What are the components of the degradation model?

**Model Answer:**

Image degradation is the process by which an original image $f(x, y)$ gets corrupted during acquisition, transmission, or storage, resulting in an observed image $g(x, y)$ that deviates from the original.

The general degradation model is:

$$g(x, y) = H\big[f(x, y)\big] + \eta(x, y)$$

where:
- $H[\cdot]$ = degradation operator (spatial blur, motion, atmospheric)
- $\eta(x, y)$ = additive noise term
- $H = 1$ for pure noise corruption (the "easy" case)

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern):** Definition (1 mark), Equation (1 mark), Component explanation (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**

List any four important noise PDFs used in image restoration. State the best filter for each.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Noise Type | PDF Form | Best Filter |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gaussian | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\sigma}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | Arithmetic Mean / Wiener |
| 2 | Salt & Pepper (Impulse) | $P_a, P_b$ at $a, b$ | Median |
| 3 | Uniform | $\dfrac{1}{b-a}$ on $[a, b]$ | Midpoint |
| 4 | Periodic (Sinusoidal) | $A\sin(2\pi(u_0 x + v_0 y))$ | Notch / Band-reject |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the image degradation model in the spatial and frequency domains. Explain the role of the degradation function $H$ and noise term $\eta$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a $5 \times 5$ image patch corrupted with salt & pepper noise, demonstrate the working of the median filter with a numerical example.

---

### Model Solution ŌĆö Question A

**(a) Degradation Model Derivation:**

**Spatial Domain:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
g(x, y) &= H\big[f(x, y)\big] + \eta(x, y) \\[4pt]
\text{For easy restoration:} \quad g(x, y) &= f(x, y) + \eta(x, y)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Frequency Domain (2D DFT applied):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G(u, v) &= H(u, v) \cdot F(u, v) + N(u, v) \\[4pt]
\text{When } H = 1: \quad G(u, v) &= F(u, v) + N(u, v)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Role of components:**
- **$H$ (Degradation Function):** Models spatial blur, motion, atmospheric turbulence. For "easy" cases, $H = 1$.
- **$\eta$ (Noise):** Additive random corruption from sensor electronics, transmission channel.
- **Restoration Goal:** Find $\hat{f}(x, y)$ such that $\hat{F}(u, v) \approx F(u, v)$.

**(b) Median Filter Numerical Example:**

Consider the following $5 \times 5$ corrupted patch (salt & pepper: 0 = pepper, 255 = salt):

```
 52  55  61  58  50
 49  255 60  62  53   ŌåÉ 255 is salt impulse
 51  54  0   56  55   ŌåÉ 0 is pepper impulse
 48  57  59  61  52
 50  53  58  54  49
```

**Step 1 ŌĆö Centre the 3├Ś3 window on the salt pixel (row 2, col 2):**

```
 52  55  61
 49  255 60
 51  54  0
```

**Step 2 ŌĆö Sort the 9 values:**

$$0,\ 49,\ 51,\ 52,\ 54,\ 55,\ 60,\ 61,\ 255$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Pick the median (5th value of sorted list):**

$$\hat{f}(2, 2) = 54$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Repeat for all corrupted pixels.** Result: impulse noise removed, edges preserved.

---

**Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the concept of periodic noise in digital images. With a suitable diagram, describe the working of an **Ideal Notch Reject filter** for its removal.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A 4├Ś4 image block has uniform noise with $a = 10$, $b = 40$. Calculate the mean and variance. If an arithmetic mean filter of window size $2 \times 2$ is applied to a Gaussian noise with $\sigma^2 = 64$, what is the variance of the filtered image?

---

### Model Solution ŌĆö Question B

**(a) Periodic Noise and Notch Filter:**

**Periodic Noise:** Caused by electromagnetic interference during image acquisition, appearing as repetitive patterns (stripes, moir├®) in spatial domain and as **bright discrete spikes** in the frequency spectrum.

**Mathematical Model:**

$$g(x, y) = f(x, y) + A \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi(u_0 x + v_0 y)}{M}\right)$$

**Ideal Notch Reject Filter (one pair at $(u_0, v_0)$ and $(-u_0, -v_0)$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{NR}(u, v) &= \prod_{k=1}^{n} H_k(u, v) \\[4pt]
H_k(u, v) &= \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } D_k(u, v) \le D_0 \\ 1 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \\[4pt]
D_k(u, v) &= \left[(u - u_k)^2 + (v - v_k)^2\right]^{1/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Procedure:**
1. Compute $G(u, v) = \text{FFT}[g(x, y)]$
2. Identify noise spikes at $(u_0, v_0), (-u_0, -v_0)$
3. Define $H_{NR}(u, v)$ with cut-off radius $D_0$
4. Multiply: $\hat{F}(u, v) = H_{NR}(u, v) \cdot G(u, v)$
5. Reconstruct: $\hat{f}(x, y) = \text{Re}\big\{\text{IFFT}[\hat{F}(u, v)]\big\}$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Uniform noise parameters.**

$$\mu = \frac{a + b}{2} = \frac{10 + 40}{2} = 25$$

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{(b - a)^2}{12} = \frac{(30)^2}{12} = \frac{900}{12} = 75$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Arithmetic mean filter for Gaussian noise.**

For window size $N = 2 \times 2 = 4$:

$$\sigma^2_{\text{filtered}} = \frac{\sigma^2_\eta}{N} = \frac{64}{4} = 16$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Standard deviation after filtering.**

$$\sigma_{\text{filtered}} = \sqrt{16} = 4$$

**Conclusion:** Noise variance is reduced from 64 to 16, giving a **75% reduction** in noise power.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $H$ vs $\eta$:** Many students write the degradation model incorrectly as $g = f + H$. Remember: $H$ is a *function/operator*, $\eta$ is the *noise*. Marks lost if order is reversed.
> 2. **Median filter output:** Students often pick the *mean* of sorted list. KTU expects the **middle value**, not the average.
> 3. **Notch filter centre:** A notch filter is a **pair** at $(u_0, v_0)$ and $(-u_0, -v_0)$. Marking is done for both centres, not just one.
> 4. **PSNR formula units:** Always specify dB. Writing PSNR = 22.11 without unit may lose 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Filter formula limits:** Contra-harmonic mean ŌĆö students forget that $Q = 0$ reduces to arithmetic mean and $Q = -1$ gives harmonic mean. Examiners test this frequently.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Degradation model:** $g(x, y) = H[f(x, y)] + \eta(x, y)$; for "easy" restoration $H = 1$.
- [ ] **Seven key noise PDFs:** Gaussian, Salt & Pepper, Uniform, Exponential, Rayleigh, Gamma/Erlang, Periodic ŌĆö each with closed-form expression.
- [ ] **Filter-to-noise matching:** Median Ōåö Salt & Pepper; Arithmetic Mean Ōåö Gaussian; Notch Ōåö Periodic; Alpha-trimmed Ōåö Mixed.
- [ ] **Variance reduction rule:** Averaging $N$ i.i.d. samples ŌåÆ variance reduces by factor $N$.
- [ ] **Uniform noise:** $\mu = (a+b)/2$, $\sigma^2 = (b-a)^2 / 12$ ŌĆö frequently asked derivation.
- [ ] **Notch filter centres come in pairs:** Always at $(u_0, v_0)$ **and** $(-u_0, -v_0)$.
- [ ] **PSNR formula:** $\text{PSNR} = 10\log_{10}(L^2 / \text{MSE})$ in **dB**.
- [ ] **Order statistic filter family:** Median, Max, Min, Midpoint, Alpha-trimmed ŌĆö all derivatives of ranked pixel values in a window.
- [ ] **Contra-harmonic mean Q-sign rule:** $Q > 0$ eliminates pepper; $Q < 0$ eliminates salt.
- [ ] **Geometric vs Arithmetic mean:** Geometric preserves detail better; Arithmetic smooths more aggressively.
- [ ] **Butterworth order $n$:** Higher $n$ ŌåÆ sharper transition but more ringing.
- [ ] **For KTU:** Always draw filter response plots and mention band-centre $D_0$ and bandwidth $W$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inverse Filtering
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST636 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inverse Filtering

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Inverse Filtering** is a fundamental frequency-domain image restoration technique that attempts to recover an original (undegraded) image $f(x,y)$ from a degraded observation $g(x,y)$ by analytically inverting the known degradation function $h(x,y)$. In the discrete frequency domain, the estimated original spectrum is obtained by dividing the degraded spectrum by the degradation transfer function:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Inverse filtering forms the theoretical backbone of Module 2 (Image Restoration). Students must master its derivation from the degradation model, its frequency-domain formulation, and critically, its **inherent instability in the presence of noise**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are looking at a photograph through a **smudged window** (the degradation). You can still see the scene, but it is blurry. If you knew *exactly* how the smudge distorted each light ray (the Point Spread Function), you could in theory "undo" the smudging mathematically ŌĆö divide out the smudge effect to recover the clear scene.

This mathematical "undoing" is **Inverse Filtering**.

**Real-world analogy:** Think of it like audio equalization. If a speaker system has a known frequency response curve $H(f)$, and you hear a muffled sound $G(f)$, you can compensate by boosting the frequencies that were attenuated ŌĆö i.e., multiplying by $1/H(f)$. Inverse filtering does exactly this for images, but in **2D spatial frequency** $(u,v)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Inverse filtering is essentially **division in the frequency domain**, which corresponds to **deconvolution** in the spatial domain. It is the simplest possible restoration operator.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **H(u,v)** ŌĆö Degradation transfer function (often the Optical Transfer Function, OTF).
- **N(u,v)** ŌĆö Additive noise spectrum, modeled as zero-mean Gaussian with variance $\sigma^2$.
- **SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio)** ŌĆö $\frac{|F(u,v)|^2}{|N(u,v)|^2}$, measured in **decibels (dB)**.
- **Singularity Threshold** ŌĆö $|H(u,v)| < \epsilon$ (a small constant, typically $\epsilon = 0.001$ or smaller) marks the region where inverse filtering becomes numerically unstable.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Degradation Model (Foundation)

The classical degradation model used in KTU Module 2 is the **linear, position-invariant (LPI)** system corrupted by additive noise:

$$g(x,y) = h(x,y) \ast f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

Where:
- $f(x,y)$ = original (undegraded) image
- $h(x,y)$ = degradation (blur) function / Point Spread Function (PSF)
- $\ast$ = 2D convolution operator
- $\eta(x,y)$ = additive noise
- $g(x,y)$ = observed/degraded image

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply the 2D Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** to both sides. By the **convolution theorem**, convolution in spatial domain becomes multiplication in frequency domain:

$$G(u,v) = H(u,v)F(u,v) + N(u,v)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Neglect the noise term** (the simplest, noise-free assumption used to derive the *ideal* inverse filter):

$$G(u,v) \approx H(u,v)F(u,v)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Solve for $F(u,v)$** by dividing both sides by $H(u,v)$:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Inverse DFT** to obtain the spatial-domain estimate:

$$\hat{f}(x,y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{\hat{F}(u,v)\}$$

### 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

| Step | Mathematical Action | Engineering Justification |
|------|---------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | Transform to frequency domain | Decouples convolution into algebraic multiplication ŌĆö much easier to invert |
| 2 | Drop noise $N(u,v)$ | Provides a tractable starting point; leads to the *ideal* inverse filter |
| 3 | Divide by $H(u,v)$ | Mathematical inversion of the degradation system |
| 4 | Inverse DFT | Returns the estimate to the spatial domain for display/storage |

### 2.4 The Critical Limitation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Inverse Filtering Fails in Practice:** When $H(u,v) \to 0$ (zeros or near-zeros of the degradation function), the ratio $\frac{1}{H(u,v)} \to \infty$. Any residual noise $N(u,v)$ at those frequencies gets **amplified without bound**, producing severe artifacts. This is the central pitfall examined in KTU board questions.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Domain |
|---|----------|-------------|--------|
| 1 | $g(x,y) = h(x,y) \ast f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$ | Degradation model | Spatial |
| 2 | $G(u,v) = H(u,v)F(u,v) + N(u,v)$ | Degradation model | Frequency |
| 3 | $\hat{F}(u,v) = \dfrac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$ | Ideal inverse filter (no noise) | Frequency |
| 4 | $\hat{F}(u,v) = \dfrac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)} - \dfrac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$ | Inverse filter with noise | Frequency |
| 5 | $\hat{f}(x,y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\!\left[\hat{F}(u,v)\right]$ | Recovered image | Spatial |
| 6 | $M(u,v) = \dfrac{1}{H(u,v)}$ | Inverse filter transfer function | Frequency |
| 7 | $\mathrm{SNR}_{out}(u,v) = \vert H(u,v)\vert^{2}\cdot\mathrm{SNR}_{in}(u,v)$ | SNR scaling under inverse filter | Frequency |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule:** In the table above, vertical bars $\vert x \vert$ are written as `\vert` to avoid breaking Markdown table syntax. Never use raw `|` inside a table cell.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Astronomy** ŌĆö Deblurring telescope images affected by atmospheric turbulence.
- **Medical imaging** ŌĆö Restoring motion-blurred MRI/CT scans.
- **Forensics** ŌĆö Recovering license plates from security footage.
- **Remote sensing** ŌĆö Compensating for sensor PSF in satellite imagery.
- **OCR pre-processing** ŌĆö Sharpening text before character recognition.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö From Degradation Model to Inverse Filter

We begin with the **spatial-domain degradation model**:

$$g(x,y) = h(x,y) \ast f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

Apply the **2D Discrete Fourier Transform** to both sides. The convolution theorem states that convolution in spatial domain becomes pointwise multiplication in the frequency domain:

$$\mathcal{F}\{g(x,y)\} = \mathcal{F}\{h(x,y) \ast f(x,y)\} + \mathcal{F}\{\eta(x,y)\}$$

$$\implies G(u,v) = H(u,v)\cdot F(u,v) + N(u,v)$$

Now we make the **idealizing assumption** of zero noise ($N(u,v) = 0$):

$$G(u,v) = H(u,v)\cdot F(u,v)$$

Assuming $H(u,v) \neq 0$ for the frequency of interest, divide both sides by $H(u,v)$:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

This is the **ideal inverse filter estimate** in the frequency domain. To recover the spatial image, apply the **Inverse Discrete Fourier Transform**:

$$\hat{f}(x,y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\!\left\{\frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}\right\}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Complete Formulation Including Noise

When noise is present and cannot be neglected, the exact rearrangement gives:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)} - \frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

The first term $\dfrac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$ is the desired clean estimate. The second term $\dfrac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$ is the **error term**. Because $\dfrac{1}{H(u,v)}$ can be very large (where $H$ is small), even tiny noise amplitudes get **catastrophically amplified**, corrupting the recovery.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Effect on SNR

The input SNR at a frequency $(u,v)$ is:

$$\mathrm{SNR}_{in}(u,v) = \frac{|F(u,v)|^{2}}{|N(u,v)|^{2}}$$

After applying the inverse filter, the recovered estimate is $\hat{F}(u,v) = F(u,v) + \dfrac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$. The noise term in the output is $\dfrac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$, so the output SNR becomes:

$$\mathrm{SNR}_{out}(u,v) = \frac{|F(u,v)|^{2}}{\left|\dfrac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}\right|^{2}} = \frac{|F(u,v)|^{2}}{|N(u,v)|^{2}} \cdot |H(u,v)|^{2}$$

Hence:

$$\mathrm{SNR}_{out}(u,v) = |H(u,v)|^{2}\cdot \mathrm{SNR}_{in}(u,v)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Since $|H(u,v)| \leq 1$ in any physically realizable blurring system, we always have $\mathrm{SNR}_{out} \leq \mathrm{SNR}_{in}$. The inverse filter **always degrades SNR**, and the degradation is severe wherever $|H(u,v)|$ is small.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A 1D toy signal is degraded by a blur whose transfer function is:

$$H(u) = 0.01 \quad \text{for all } u$$

The degraded spectrum is $G(u) = 2.0$ and the noise spectrum at the same frequency is $N(u) = 0.0001$. Compute the inverse-filter estimate and the noise contribution.

**Solution:**

Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the inverse filter response:

$$M(u) = \frac{1}{H(u)} = \frac{1}{0.01} = 100$$

Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the estimate:

$$\hat{F}(u) = \frac{G(u)}{H(u)} = \frac{2.0}{0.01} = 200$$

Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the noise contribution in the recovered spectrum:

$$\hat{N}(u) = \frac{N(u)}{H(u)} = \frac{0.0001}{0.01} = 0.01$$

Step 4 ŌĆö Compare signal-to-noise in the recovered estimate:

$$\mathrm{SNR}_{out} = \frac{|\hat{F}_{signal}|^{2}}{|\hat{N}|^{2}} = \frac{(200)^{2}}{(0.01)^{2}} = \frac{40000}{0.0001} = 4 \times 10^{8}$$

But note that **relative to the input**:

$$\mathrm{SNR}_{in} = \frac{(2.0)^{2}}{(0.0001)^{2}} = 4 \times 10^{8}$$

So in this *ideal* case (constant $H$), SNR is preserved. However, if $H(u) = 0.0001$ instead, then $M(u) = 10000$ and the noise contribution becomes $1.0$, overwhelming the signal ŌĆö illustrating the pathology.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Stability Across the Frequency Plane

| Region of $(u,v)$ | Typical $\vert H(u,v)\vert$ | Effect of Inverse Filter | Practical Verdict |
|-------------------|-----------------------------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| Low frequencies (DC & near) | $\approx 1$ | Modest amplification, OK | Safe |
| Mid frequencies | $0.1$ŌĆō$0.5$ | Noise boosted by $2\times$ŌĆō$10\times$ | Marginal |
| High frequencies | $\to 0$ | Noise boosted by $10^{3}$ŌĆō$10^{6}\times$ | **Catastrophic** ŌĆö must be truncated |
| Beyond cutoff of $H$ | $= 0$ (exact zeros) | Division by zero ŌĆö undefined | **Forbidden** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram ŌĆö Inverse Filtering Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the complete signal-flow architecture of an inverse filtering system, as typically expected in KTU Module 2 examination answers:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ORIGINAL IMAGE F_U_V] --> B[DEGRADATION SYSTEM H_U_V]
    B --> C[DEGRADED PLUS NOISE G_U_V]
    N[NOISE N_U_V] --> C
    C --> D[DFT TRANSFORM]
    D --> E[SPECTRUM G_U_V]
    E --> F[DIVISION NODE]
    X[KNOWN PSF H_U_V] --> G[INVERT 1 OVER H_U_V]
    G --> F
    F --> H[RECOVERED SPECTRUM F_HAT_U_V]
    H --> I[INVERSE DFT]
    I --> J[RESTORED IMAGE F_HAT_X_Y]
    J --> K[DISPLAY OR ANALYSIS]
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow ŌĆö Stability of Inverse Filter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START INVERSE FILTER] --> B{IS H_U_V ZERO?}
    B -->|YES| C[STOP DIVISION BY ZERO]
    B -->|NO| D{IS H_U_V BELOW EPSILON?}
    D -->|YES| E[TRUNCATE TO ZERO BAND LIMITED ESTIMATE]
    D -->|NO| F[COMPUTE 1 OVER H_U_V]
    F --> G[MULTIPLY WITH G_U_V]
    G --> H[APPLY INVERSE DFT]
    H --> I[OUTPUT RESTORED IMAGE]
    E --> J[USE PSEUDO INVERSE 1 OVER H ONLY WHERE SAFE]
    J --> I
```

### 4.3 Comparative Architecture ŌĆö Inverse vs. Wiener Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IDEAL_INVERSE
        A1[G_U_V] --> A2[DIVIDE BY H_U_V]
        A2 --> A3[F_HAT_U_V]
    end
    subgraph REGULARIZED_INVERSE
        B1[G_U_V] --> B2[MULTIPLY BY H STAR OVER MOD H SQUARED PLUS K]
        B2 --> B3[F_HAT_U_V]
    end
    subgraph WIENER
        C1[G_U_V] --> C2[MULTIPLY BY H STAR OVER MOD H SQUARED PLUS S_N OVER S_F]
        C2 --> C3[F_HAT_U_V]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three boxes?** The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically contrasts the **ideal inverse filter** (first box) with regularized forms (Wiener, Constrained Least Squares). Understanding this progression is essential for full marks on 14-mark questions.

### 4.4 Spectrum Behavior Schematic

```
|F_hat(u,v)|
     |
     |            catastrophic blow-up
     |            ^
     |           /
     |          /
     |   safe  /  
     | region /
     |_______/_________________________________>  (u,v) frequency
            low              mid            high
            (H Ōēł 1)       (H moderate)    (H ŌåÆ 0)
```

This schematic illustrates how the inverse filter estimate magnitude blows up at high frequencies where $H(u,v) \to 0$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**

*Define inverse filtering. Write its frequency-domain expression for image restoration.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Inverse filtering is a non-blind image restoration technique used to recover an undegraded image from a degraded observation when the degradation function is known. It works by dividing the degraded image's spectrum by the degradation function's spectrum.

In the frequency domain, assuming additive noise $N(u,v)$ is negligible, the estimate is:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

where $G(u,v)$ is the DFT of the degraded image and $H(u,v)$ is the degradation transfer function. The spatial-domain restored image is obtained by inverse DFT.

*(1 mark ŌĆö definition, 1 mark ŌĆö equation, 1 mark ŌĆö explanation of domain transformation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**

*State any two limitations of the inverse filter.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Noise Amplification:** Wherever $H(u,v)$ is small or zero, the ratio $\frac{1}{H(u,v)}$ becomes very large, catastrophically amplifying any additive noise $N(u,v)$ in the degraded image.

2. **Sensitivity to PSF Estimation Errors:** The inverse filter assumes perfect knowledge of $H(u,v)$. Small errors in estimating the PSF lead to large errors in the restored image, especially at high frequencies.

3. *(Optional 3rd)* **Singularities / Division by Zero:** The filter is undefined at frequencies where $H(u,v) = 0$.

*(1.5 marks per limitation with brief justification.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Starting from the spatial-domain degradation model, derive the frequency-domain expression of the **ideal inverse filter**. Clearly state the assumptions used. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain in detail why the ideal inverse filter fails in the presence of noise. Show mathematically how the output SNR relates to the input SNR. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1 ŌĆö State the spatial degradation model:**

$$g(x,y) = h(x,y) \ast f(x,y) + \eta(x,y) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the 2D DFT.** Using the convolution theorem:

$$G(u,v) = H(u,v)F(u,v) + N(u,v) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Assumption:** Neglect the noise term $N(u,v) \approx 0$ for the ideal case.

$$G(u,v) \approx H(u,v)F(u,v) \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Divide both sides by $H(u,v)$** (assuming $H(u,v) \neq 0$):

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)} \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply inverse DFT to obtain the spatial image:**

$$\hat{f}(x,y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\!\left\{\frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)}\right\}$$

**Assumptions made:**
- The system is **linear and position-invariant (LPI)**.
- The noise is negligible.
- The degradation function $H(u,v)$ is exactly known and non-zero at the frequencies of interest.

*(Mark distribution: 1 ŌĆö degradation model, 1 ŌĆö DFT application, 1 ŌĆö assumption, 1 ŌĆö division step, 1 ŌĆö inverse DFT, 1 ŌĆö assumption list, 1 ŌĆö overall coherence/notation.)*

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start from the exact frequency-domain model with noise:**

$$G(u,v) = H(u,v)F(u,v) + N(u,v)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Solve for $F(u,v)$ exactly:**

$$F(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)} - \frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Identify the error term.** The second term $\dfrac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$ represents noise amplification. When $|H(u,v)|$ is small, this term becomes very large.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Derive the SNR relation.** Define:

$$\mathrm{SNR}_{in} = \frac{|F(u,v)|^{2}}{|N(u,v)|^{2}}, \quad \mathrm{SNR}_{out} = \frac{|F(u,v)|^{2}}{\left|\frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}\right|^{2}}$$

Therefore:

$$\mathrm{SNR}_{out} = |H(u,v)|^{2}\cdot \mathrm{SNR}_{in}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Conclude.** Since $|H(u,v)| \leq 1$ for any physically realizable blur, $\mathrm{SNR}_{out} \leq \mathrm{SNR}_{in}$. At frequencies where $H \to 0$, the output SNR collapses to zero, making the restoration unusable. This is the fundamental limitation of the ideal inverse filter, motivating the use of **Wiener filtering** and **constrained least squares** filtering.

*(Mark distribution: 1 ŌĆö exact model, 1 ŌĆö error term, 1 ŌĆö explanation of amplification, 1 ŌĆö SNR definition, 1 ŌĆö SNR relation derivation, 1 ŌĆö conclusion, 1 ŌĆö motivation for advanced filters.)*

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Supplementary Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **degradation model** used in image restoration. Derive the equation governing inverse filtering in the frequency domain and state the assumptions. (7 marks)

**(b)** A degraded image has spectrum $G(u,v)$ and the degradation transfer function is given by:

$$H(u,v) = \begin{cases} 0.02 & \text{for } u^{2}+v^{2} \leq R^{2} \\ 0.001 & \text{for } u^{2}+v^{2} > R^{2} \end{cases}$$

The noise spectrum magnitude at a frequency outside the disc is $|N(u,v)| = 0.0005$. Compute the inverse filter estimate at such a frequency and discuss whether it is practically usable. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a)

Same derivation as **Question A part (a)** above. State the LPI degradation model, apply DFT, drop noise, divide, and state assumptions.

*(Full marks: 7)*

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the operating region.** We are at a frequency with $u^{2}+v^{2} > R^{2}$, so $H(u,v) = 0.001$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the inverse filter gain:**

$$M(u,v) = \frac{1}{H(u,v)} = \frac{1}{0.001} = 1000$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Estimate the noise amplification in the recovered spectrum:**

$$\hat{N}(u,v) = \frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)} = \frac{0.0005}{0.001} = 0.5$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Comment on practicality.** A noise amplification factor of **1000** means that even a tiny noise of $0.0005$ becomes $0.5$ in the recovered spectrum ŌĆö typically comparable to or larger than the actual image content at high frequencies. The restored image will be **dominated by amplified noise artifacts** and is **not practically usable** without additional regularization (e.g., Wiener filtering or a low-pass constraint).

**Conclusion:** The output SNR drops by a factor of $|H(u,v)|^{2} = 10^{-6}$, making the high-frequency region of the restored image essentially noise.

*(Mark distribution: 1 ŌĆö region identification, 1 ŌĆö gain computation, 1 ŌĆö noise amplification, 1 ŌĆö practical interpretation, 1 ŌĆö conclusion, 1 ŌĆö comparison with alternative methods, 1 ŌĆö clear presentation.)*

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Inverse Filtering Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the assumption** that $H(u,v) \neq 0$ ŌĆö students routinely divide blindly, losing 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $H(u,v)$ with $h(x,y)$** in notation. Use $H(u,v)$ only in frequency domain.
> 3. **Omitting the inverse DFT step** ŌĆö the final spatial-domain answer requires $\mathcal{F}^{-1}$.
> 4. **Failing to discuss noise** even when the question does not explicitly mention it. Always mention noise as a limitation in any inverse-filter question.
> 5. **Skipping the SNR derivation** in the 14-mark question ŌĆö this is the *highest-weight* sub-part.
> 6. **Writing raw `|` inside Markdown tables** ŌĆö this breaks formatting in digital answer sheets. Use `\vert` instead.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Inverse filtering** is the *simplest* image restoration technique: divide degraded spectrum by PSF spectrum.
- It is derived from the **linear, position-invariant degradation model** $g = h \ast f + \eta$.
- The frequency-domain formula is $\hat{F}(u,v) = G(u,v)/H(u,v)$, with **noise-free assumption**.
- The **biggest limitation** is **noise amplification** at frequencies where $|H|$ is small.
- Output SNR is scaled by $|H(u,v)|^{2}$ ŌĆö always **less than or equal to** input SNR.
- **Assumptions required:** LPI system, known PSF, $H(u,v) \neq 0$, negligible noise.
- **Alternatives in KTU syllabus:** Wiener filter, Constrained Least Squares (CLS), Geometric Mean filter ŌĆö all address the noise-amplification pathology of the ideal inverse filter.
- **Implementation note:** In practice, one truncates $1/H(u,v)$ to zero below a threshold $\epsilon$ to avoid division-by-zero, producing a **band-limited inverse filter**.
- **Exam mantra:** "Inverse filter is unstable ŌĆö Wiener is its regularized successor."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wiener Filtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST636 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wiener Filtering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wiener Filtering** is a fundamental linear **image restoration** technique in the field of Digital Image Processing that recovers a degraded image by producing an optimal estimate of the original (uncorrupted) image. It is formally defined as a stochastic frequency-domain filter that minimizes the **mean square error (MSE)** between the estimated image $\hat{f}(x,y)$ and the true original image $f(x,y)$, subject to the assumptions that both the image signal and the additive noise are **wide-sense stationary (WSS)** linear stochastic processes with known power spectral densities.

In the frequency domain, the Wiener filter is characterized by the transfer function:

$$W(u,v) = \frac{H^*(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^2 + S_{\eta}(u,v)/S_f(u,v)}$$

where $H(u,v)$ is the degradation function, $H^*(u,v)$ is its complex conjugate, $S_f(u,v)$ is the power spectral density of the original (signal) image, and $S_{\eta}(u,v)$ is the power spectral density of the noise. The ratio $S_{\eta}(u,v)/S_f(u,v)$ is termed the **noise-to-signal power ratio (NSR)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Wiener filtering falls under the core unit on *Image Restoration*. The examiner typically tests three aspects: (1) the analytical formula, (2) its behavior relative to the inverse filter, and (3) the case where NSR is unknown (leading to the parametric/constrained least-squares variant).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Noisy Megaphone" Intuition

Imagine a famous musician (the *true image* $f$) singing into a faulty megaphone in a noisy factory. The megaphone distorts certain frequencies (the *degradation function* $H$) and the factory adds background hum (the *noise* $\eta$). You, standing at the back of the room, hear the muffled, humming sound (the *degraded image* $g$). 

A naive approach would be to "undo" the megaphone's distortion exactly ŌĆö but that would also amplify the factory hum to a deafening level, drowning out the music (this is the **inverse filter** problem). 

The **Wiener filter** is like a smart audio engineer who knows the *statistical power* of both the singer's voice and the factory's hum. He crafts an equalizer that *gently* undoes the megaphone's distortion but **automatically backs off** at frequencies where the hum is louder than the voice, preventing noise amplification. The result is a clean, faithful reconstruction of the original song.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** The Wiener filter is a *compromise* filter. Where the signal is strong relative to noise, it behaves like an inverse filter (aggressive restoration). Where noise dominates, it behaves like a low-pass filter (cautious noise suppression).

## 1.3 The Degradation Model ŌĆö Foundation of Wiener Filtering

All Wiener-filter discussions begin with the standard **image degradation model**:

$$g(x,y) = h(x,y) * f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

In the frequency domain, convolution becomes multiplication:

$$G(u,v) = H(u,v)F(u,v) + N(u,v)$$

| Symbol | Physical Meaning | Unit / Domain |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $g(x,y)$ | Degraded (observed) image | Spatial intensity |
| $f(x,y)$ | Original (true) image | Spatial intensity |
| $h(x,y)$ | Degradation function (PSF) | Spatial kernel |
| $\eta(x,y)$ | Additive noise (often AWGN) | Spatial intensity |
| $H(u,v)$ | Degradation transfer function (OTF) | Frequency domain |
| $G(u,v)$ | DFT of degraded image | Frequency domain |
| $N(u,v)$ | DFT of noise | Frequency domain |

The goal of restoration is to estimate $\hat{f}(x,y) \approx f(x,y)$ given $g(x,y)$ and partial knowledge of $H$ and noise statistics.

## 1.4 Why Not Just Use the Inverse Filter?

The **inverse filter** simply divides the degraded image's spectrum by the degradation function:

$$\hat{F}_{inv}(u,v) = \frac{G(u,v)}{H(u,v)} = F(u,v) + \frac{N(u,v)}{H(u,v)}$$

The catastrophic term is $N(u,v)/H(u,v)$. Whenever $H(u,v) \rightarrow 0$ (at high frequencies, e.g., due to motion blur or atmospheric turbulence), this ratio **explodes**, amplifying noise to infinity. The Wiener filter, in contrast, *regularizes* this division by adding a noise floor in the denominator.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Minimum Mean Square Error (MMSE) Criterion

The Wiener filter is derived by minimizing the **expected squared error** between the true image $f(x,y)$ and its linear estimate $\hat{f}(x,y)$:

$$E[\,\vert f(x,y) - \hat{f}(x,y)\vert^2\,] \rightarrow \text{minimum}$$

Under the assumptions of:
1. **Wide-sense stationarity** of both signal and noise,
2. **Zero mean** for both processes,
3. **Linearity** of the estimator,
4. **Uncorrelated** signal and noise: $E[f(x,y)\eta(x',y')] = 0$,

the optimal solution in the frequency domain takes the elegant closed form of the Wiener transfer function.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of Wiener Filter Operation

The filter operates entirely in the **frequency domain** using the discrete Fourier transform (DFT):

1. **Step 1 ŌĆö Transform:** Compute the DFT of the degraded image: $G(u,v) = \mathcal{F}\{g(x,y)\}$.
2. **Step 2 ŌĆö Estimate Spectrum:** Apply the Wiener transfer function: $\hat{F}(u,v) = W(u,v) \cdot G(u,v)$.
3. **Step 3 ŌĆö Inverse Transform:** Compute the inverse DFT to obtain the restored image: $\hat{f}(x,y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{\hat{F}(u,v)\}$.
4. **Step 4 ŌĆö Optional Post-processing:** Take the real part (since the image is real-valued) and clip intensity values to the displayable range.

## 2.3 Two Critical Assumptions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stationarity Assumption:** The image and noise are assumed to be *wide-sense stationary*, meaning their mean and autocorrelation do not vary with spatial position. Real images are *not* perfectly stationary, so Wiener filtering is most effective on small image patches or texture-homogeneous regions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Known Power Spectra:** The filter requires $S_f(u,v)$ and $S_{\eta}(u,v)$. In practice, these are *estimated*, often as $S_f(u,v) \approx \vert F(u,v)\vert^2$ from a degraded observation and $S_{\eta}(u,v)$ from a flat region of the image.

## 2.4 The Three Filter Variants (KTU-Favorite Comparison)

KTU examiners frequently ask students to *contrast* the Wiener filter with related filters. Master these three:

| Filter Type | Formula | Noise Behavior | When to Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Inverse Filter** | $W(u,v) = 1/H(u,v)$ | Amplifies noise catastrophically | Theoretical baseline; noise-free case |
| **Wiener Filter** | $W(u,v) = H^*/\left(\vert H\vert^2 + S_\eta/S_f\right)$ | Suppresses noise gracefully | NSR is known or estimable |
| **Constrained LS** | Minimizes $\Vert g - H\hat{f}\Vert^2$ subject to $\Vert C\hat{f}\Vert^2 = \varepsilon$ | Smoothness-constrained | NSR unknown; one tunable parameter |

## 2.5 The Parametric / Constrained Least-Squares Wiener Filter

When the NSR is **unknown**, KTU syllabus introduces the **parametric Wiener filter**:

$$W(u,v) = \frac{H^*(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^2 + \gamma \, \vert P(u,v)\vert^2}$$

where $\gamma$ is a *user-tunable scalar* and $P(u,v)$ is the DFT of a **Laplacian-type high-pass regularization kernel** (penalizing high-frequency content to suppress noise). The student must understand:

- $\gamma \to 0$ recovers the **inverse filter** (aggressive, noisy).
- $\gamma \to \infty$ forces $\hat{F} \to 0$ (over-smoothed, blank output).
- An intermediate $\gamma$ yields the best visual quality.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Wiener filtering is the workhorse behind **deblurring** in medical imaging (MRI, CT), **astronomical imaging** (Hubble space telescope aberration correction), **forensic image enhancement**, and **old-photograph restoration** in cultural heritage preservation.

## 2.6 Limiting-Case Behavior of the Wiener Filter (High-Yield Analysis)

This is a favorite KTU conceptual question. Examine three limiting cases of $S_\eta / S_f$:

| Condition | NSR Value | Wiener Behavior | Filter Form |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| High SNR (clean image) | $S_\eta/S_f \to 0$ | Approximates inverse filter | $W \to 1/H$ |
| Low SNR (noisy image) | $S_\eta/S_f \to \infty$ | Acts as low-pass filter | $W \to S_f H^*/S_\eta$ |
| Balanced | Finite ratio | Compromise restoration | Full Wiener form |

This **adaptive behavior** is the central elegance of the Wiener filter ŌĆö the *same formula* self-regulates between aggressive restoration and noise suppression.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Wiener Filter in the Frequency Domain

We begin with the degraded image spectrum and seek a linear filter $W(u,v)$ such that:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = W(u,v) \cdot G(u,v) = W(u,v)\bigl[H(u,v)F(u,v) + N(u,v)\bigr]$$

The **mean square error** in the frequency domain (Parseval's theorem) is:

$$E\bigl[\,\vert F - \hat{F}\vert^2\,\bigr] = E\bigl[\,\vert F - WHF - WN\vert^2\,\bigr]$$

Expanding the squared magnitude using $\vert A \vert^2 = A \cdot A^*$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\,\vert F - WHF - WN\vert^2\,] &= E[\,(F - WHF - WN)(F^* - W^*H^*F^* - W^*N^*)\,] \\
&= E[\,\vert F\vert^2\,] - WH E[\,\vert F\vert^2\,] - W E[\,FN^*\,] \\
&\quad - W^*H^* E[\,\vert F\vert^2\,] + \vert W\vert^2 \vert H\vert^2 E[\,\vert F\vert^2\,] + \vert W\vert^2 E[\,\vert N\vert^2\,] \\
&\quad + W^* E[\,NF^*\,] + W^*H^* W E[\,NF^*\,] - W^* E[\,\vert N\vert^2\,]
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying the orthogonality principle and the assumption that signal and noise are uncorrelated ($E[FN^*] = E[NF^*] = 0$), the cross-terms vanish. The error simplifies to:

$$E = E[\,\vert F\vert^2\,] - WH S_f - W^*H^* S_f + \vert W\vert^2 \bigl(\vert H\vert^2 S_f + S_\eta\bigr)$$

where $S_f = E[\vert F\vert^2]$ is the signal power spectrum and $S_\eta = E[\vert N\vert^2]$ is the noise power spectrum.

To minimize with respect to $W^*$, take the complex derivative and set it to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial E}{\partial W^*} &= -H S_f + W \bigl(\vert H\vert^2 S_f + S_\eta\bigr) = 0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad W(\vert H\vert^2 S_f + S_\eta) &= H S_f
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $W$:

$$W(u,v) = \frac{H(u,v) \, S_f(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^2 S_f(u,v) + S_\eta(u,v)}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $S_f(u,v)$:

$$\boxed{\,W(u,v) = \frac{H^*(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^2 + \dfrac{S_\eta(u,v)}{S_f(u,v)}}\,}$$

This is the **canonical Wiener filter** formula. The conjugate $H^*$ (not $H$) appears in the numerator because the formal minimization with respect to $W$ (rather than $W^*$) yields $H^*$. The denominator contains the squared magnitude $\vert H\vert^2 = H \cdot H^*$, ensuring the filter is real-valued and positive.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Limiting-Case Inverse Filter Behavior

To formally show that the Wiener filter **reduces to the inverse filter** when noise is negligible:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W(u,v) &= \frac{H^*(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^2 + S_\eta(u,v)/S_f(u,v)}
\end{aligned}
$$

As $S_\eta \to 0$ (noise power vanishes):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{S_\eta \to 0} W(u,v) &= \frac{H^*(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^2 + 0} \\
&= \frac{H^*(u,v)}{H(u,v) H^*(u,v)} \\
&= \frac{1}{H(u,v)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is exactly the inverse filter. **Conclusion:** the Wiener filter is a *generalization* of the inverse filter.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Parametric Form

Starting from the constrained least-squares formulation, we minimize:

$$\mathcal{J}(\hat{f}) = \Vert g - H\hat{f}\Vert^2 + \gamma \Vert C\hat{f}\Vert^2$$

where $C$ is a smoothness-constraint operator (often the discrete Laplacian). Setting $\partial \mathcal{J}/\partial \hat{f} = 0$ and transforming to the frequency domain:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = \frac{H^*(u,v) G(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^2 + \gamma \vert C(u,v)\vert^2}$$

Therefore the parametric Wiener filter transfer function is:

$$W_\gamma(u,v) = \frac{H^*(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^2 + \gamma \vert P(u,v)\vert^2}$$

where $P(u,v)$ is the DFT of the constraint kernel (commonly a 3├Ś3 Laplacian). This derivation is a frequent 7-mark sub-part in KTU exams.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Computation)

**Problem:** A $4 \times 4$ image patch is blurred by a uniform $2 \times 2$ averaging kernel and corrupted by white noise. Given:
- $H(u,v) = 0.5 + 0.5\cos(2\pi u/M) + 0.5\cos(2\pi v/N)$ evaluated at $u=v=0$ gives $H(0,0) = 2.0$.
- $\vert H(1,0)\vert^2 = 0.25$, $\vert H(0,1)\vert^2 = 0.25$, $\vert H(1,1)\vert^2 = 0.0625$.
- $S_\eta = 0.01$ (constant white noise PSD), $S_f(1,0) = 0.5$, $S_f(0,1) = 0.5$, $S_f(1,1) = 0.2$.

**Find:** $W(u,v)$ at each of the above frequencies.

**Solution at $(0,0)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W(0,0) &= \frac{H^*(0,0)}{\vert H(0,0)\vert^2 + S_\eta/S_f(0,0)} \\
&= \frac{2.0}{4.0 + 0.01/100} \\
&\approx \frac{2.0}{4.0001} \\
&\approx 0.49999
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution at $(1,0)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W(1,0) &= \frac{H^*(1,0)}{0.25 + 0.01/0.5} = \frac{H^*(1,0)}{0.25 + 0.02} = \frac{H^*(1,0)}{0.27}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution at $(0,1)$:** By symmetry, $W(0,1) = W(1,0)$.

**Solution at $(1,1)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W(1,1) &= \frac{H^*(1,1)}{0.0625 + 0.01/0.2} = \frac{H^*(1,1)}{0.0625 + 0.05} = \frac{H^*(1,1)}{0.1125}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** At $(0,0)$ (DC component), the filter passes nearly half the signal ŌĆö consistent with the blur's low-pass nature. At higher frequencies, the filter *attenuates* more, suppressing the noise-dominated spectrum.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Frequency-Domain Processing Pipeline (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a complete Wiener filtering pipeline, showing how the degraded image flows through DFT, spectral filtering, and inverse DFT stages.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["DEGRADED IMAGE g x y"] --> nodeB["DFT STAGE\nFourier Transform"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["G u v\nDegraded Spectrum"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["WIENER FILTER CORE\nW u v Multiplication"]
    nodeE["DEGRADATION\nFUNCTION H u v"] --> nodeF["Conjugate H star u v"]
    nodeF --> nodeD
    nodeG["NOISE PSD\nS eta u v"] --> nodeH["NSR COMPUTATION\nS eta over S f"]
    nodeI["SIGNAL PSD\nS f u v"] --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeJ["ESTIMATED SPECTRUM\nF hat u v"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["IDFT STAGE\nInverse Fourier Transform"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["RESTORED IMAGE\nf hat x y"]
    nodeE --> nodeM["MAGNITUDE\nSQUARED H"]
    nodeM --> nodeD
```

## 4.2 Filter Behavior Comparison Diagram

This second diagram illustrates the **adaptive behavior** of the Wiener filter as a function of the noise-to-signal ratio, contrasting it with the inverse filter.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["INPUT\nG u v"] --> nodeB{"NSR\nEVALUATION"}
    nodeB -->|"Low SNR\nHigh Noise"| nodeC["WIENER MODE\nLow Pass Attenuation\nActs as Damped Filter"]
    nodeB -->|"High SNR\nLow Noise"| nodeD["INVERSE MODE\nAggressive Restoration\nActs as 1 over H"]
    nodeB -->|"Balanced\nNSR Finite"| nodeE["COMPROMISE MODE\nSoft Restoration\nBalanced Spectrum"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["OUTPUT\nF hat u v"]
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
```

## 4.3 Algorithmic Decision Flow for Filter Selection

The flowchart below helps students decide *which* filter variant to choose under different information availability scenarios ŌĆö a common KTU viva question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["RESTORATION PROBLEM\nGIVEN g x y"] --> nodeB{"IS H u v\nKNOWN?"}
    nodeB -->|"No"| nodeC["BLIND\nDECONVOLUTION"]
    nodeB -->|"Yes"| nodeD{"IS NOISE\nKNOWN?"}
    nodeD -->|"Yes Full PSD"| nodeE["CLASSICAL\nWIENER FILTER"]
    nodeD -->|"Unknown NSR"| nodeF{"TUNABLE\nPARAMETER?"}
    nodeF -->|"Yes"| nodeG["PARAMETRIC\nWIENER FILTER"]
    nodeF -->|"No"| nodeH["ITERATIVE\nESTIMATION"]
    nodeE --> nodeI["RESTORED\nIMAGE f hat x y"]
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeC --> nodeI
```

## 4.4 Spectral Action Visualization (Text-Based Schematic)

To intuitively understand what the Wiener filter *does* in the frequency plane:

```
FREQUENCY DOMAIN ACTION MAP

        |H(u,v)|^2  (Degradation Energy)
        Ō¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ō
        Ō¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ōŌ¢ō
HIGH    Ō¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æŌ¢æ   <-- Noise Dominant
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024 | 3 Marks]**

**Q:** Define Wiener filtering. State the assumptions underlying its derivation.

**Model Answer:**

Wiener filtering is a frequency-domain image restoration technique that produces a statistically optimal estimate $\hat{f}(x,y)$ of an original image $f(x,y)$ from its degraded observation $g(x,y)$ by **minimizing the mean square error** $E[\vert f - \hat{f}\vert^2]$.

**Underlying Assumptions:**
1. The image signal and noise are **wide-sense stationary** random processes.
2. Both processes have **zero mean**.
3. The image and noise are **statistically uncorrelated**: $E[f \eta] = 0$.
4. The estimator is **linear** in the input.
5. The degradation function $H$ and the power spectral densities $S_f$ and $S_\eta$ are **known** (or estimable).

Under these conditions, the filter takes the closed form $W(u,v) = H^* / (\vert H\vert^2 + S_\eta/S_f)$.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 | 3 Marks]**

**Q:** How does the Wiener filter behave in the limiting cases of (i) very low noise and (ii) very high noise?

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Very low noise (high SNR, $S_\eta/S_f \to 0$):** The denominator's NSR term vanishes, and the filter reduces to:

$$W(u,v) \to \frac{H^*(u,v)}{H(u,v)H^*(u,v)} = \frac{1}{H(u,v)}$$

This is the **inverse filter**, which performs aggressive deblurring with no noise penalty.

(ii) **Very high noise (low SNR, $S_\eta/S_f \to \infty$):** The NSR term dominates the denominator, and the filter becomes:

$$W(u,v) \approx \frac{H^*(u,v) \, S_f(u,v)}{S_\eta(u,v)}$$

This behaves as a **low-pass attenuator**, suppressing noise at the cost of losing high-frequency image details.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the image degradation and restoration model. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the Wiener filter in the frequency domain, clearly stating all assumptions. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)

The image degradation model represents the physical process by which a clean image is corrupted. Mathematically:

$$g(x,y) = h(x,y) * f(x,y) + \eta(x,y)$$

| Component | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Original image | $f(x,y)$ | The pristine scene radiance |
| Degradation function | $h(x,y)$ | PSF ŌĆö blur, motion, defocus |
| Additive noise | $\eta(x,y)$ | Sensor noise, transmission errors |
| Degraded image | $g(x,y)$ | What the camera actually records |

In the frequency domain, using the convolution theorem:

$$G(u,v) = H(u,v) \, F(u,v) + N(u,v)$$

The **restoration process** aims to invert this: given $G$ and estimates of $H$ and $N$, recover $\hat{F}$ using a restoration filter $W(u,v)$:

$$\hat{F}(u,v) = W(u,v) \, G(u,v) \implies \hat{f}(x,y) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{\hat{F}(u,v)\}$$

The block flow is: **Degraded Image ŌåÆ DFT ŌåÆ Multiply by $W(u,v)$ ŌåÆ IDFT ŌåÆ Restored Image**.

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b)

The Wiener filter is derived by minimizing the mean square error. Defining the error:

$$e^2 = E[\,\vert F(u,v) - \hat{F}(u,v)\vert^2\,]$$

Substituting $\hat{F}(u,v) = W(u,v)G(u,v) = W(u,v)[HF + N]$:

$$e^2 = E[\,\vert F - WHF - WN\vert^2\,]$$

Using the orthogonality principle and the assumptions:
- (i) $E[FN^*] = E[NF^*] = 0$ (uncorrelated)
- (ii) $E[\vert F\vert^2] = S_f$, $E[\vert N\vert^2] = S_\eta$ (power spectra)

Differentiating $e^2$ with respect to $W^*$ and setting to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial e^2}{\partial W^*} &= -HS_f + W(\vert H\vert^2 S_f + S_\eta) = 0 \\
\Rightarrow W(u,v) &= \frac{H(u,v) S_f(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^2 S_f(u,v) + S_\eta(u,v)} \\
\Rightarrow W(u,v) &= \frac{H^*(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^2 + S_\eta(u,v)/S_f(u,v)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Wiener filter transfer function**. The derivation hinges on the uncorrelatedness of signal and noise and the stationarity of both processes.

---

### Question B
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper 2024 | 14 Marks ŌĆö Internal Choice Alternative]**

**(a)** Compare the inverse filter and the Wiener filter. Under what conditions does the Wiener filter reduce to the inverse filter? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** What is the parametric Wiener filter? Explain the role of the regularization parameter $\gamma$ and derive its transfer function from the constrained least-squares criterion. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)

| Property | Inverse Filter | Wiener Filter |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Objective** | Exact algebraic inversion | MMSE-optimal estimation |
| **Formula** | $W = 1/H$ | $W = H^* / (\vert H\vert^2 + S_\eta/S_f)$ |
| **Noise handling** | None ŌĆö amplifies noise | Explicitly regularized |
| **Information needed** | $H$ only | $H$, $S_f$, $S_\eta$ |
| **Stability** | Unstable when $H$ has zeros | Stable at all frequencies |

**Reduction to inverse filter:** When noise is absent ($S_\eta = 0$):

$$\lim_{S_\eta \to 0} W = \frac{H^*}{H H^* + 0} = \frac{1}{H}$$

Thus the Wiener filter is a **statistical generalization** of the inverse filter.

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b)

The **parametric Wiener filter** addresses the practical case where the NSR is unknown but the user can tune a single scalar parameter $\gamma$. It is derived from the **constrained least-squares (CLS) criterion**:

$$\mathcal{J}(\hat{f}) = \Vert g - H\hat{f}\Vert^2 + \gamma \Vert C\hat{f}\Vert^2$$

where $C$ is a smoothness operator (typically a discrete Laplacian) and $\gamma$ controls the trade-off between fidelity and smoothness.

Setting $\partial \mathcal{J}/\partial \hat{f} = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(H^T H + \gamma C^T C)\hat{f} &= H^T g \\
\Rightarrow \hat{F}(u,v) &= \frac{H^*(u,v) \, G(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^2 + \gamma \vert C(u,v)\vert^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

The transfer function is:

$$W_\gamma(u,v) = \frac{H^*(u,v)}{\vert H(u,v)\vert^2 + \gamma \vert P(u,v)\vert^2}$$

**Role of $\gamma$:**
- **$\gamma \to 0$:** Reduces to inverse filter ŌĆö sharp but noisy.
- **$\gamma \to \infty$:** Output approaches zero ŌĆö over-smoothed.
- **Optimal $\gamma$:** Found by trial-and-error or by adjusting until the residual $\Vert g - H\hat{f}\Vert^2$ matches the expected noise variance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A very common mistake students make is *forgetting to take the complex conjugate* in the numerator of the Wiener filter. The correct numerator is $H^*(u,v)$ (the complex conjugate of the degradation function), not $H(u,v)$. The denominator correctly uses $\vert H(u,v)\vert^2 = H(u,v) \cdot H^*(u,v)$, which is real and non-negative. Writing $H$ instead of $H^*$ in the numerator is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Formula:** $W(u,v) = H^*(u,v) / \bigl(\vert H(u,v)\vert^2 + S_\eta(u,v)/S_f(u,v)\bigr)$ ŌĆö memorize verbatim.
- **Derivation Entry Point:** Minimize $E[\vert F - WHF - WN\vert^2]$ w.r.t. $W^*$, apply uncorrelatedness to kill cross-terms, solve for $W$.
- **Assumptions (5 total):** Stationarity, zero mean, linearity, uncorrelated signal-noise, known spectra.
- **Inverse Filter Limit:** Wiener $\to$ Inverse when $S_\eta \to 0$.
- **Low SNR Limit:** Wiener $\to$ Low-pass attenuator.
- **Parametric Form:** Use when NSR is unknown; $\gamma$ is the user-tunable smoothness parameter.
- **Conjugate in Numerator:** The single most-frequently-missed detail in KTU papers.
- **Domain of Operation:** Entirely frequency-domain; pair DFT + IDFT brackets the multiplication.
- **Common Pairing Questions:** Wiener vs. Inverse, Wiener vs. Constrained LS, Wiener vs. Geometric Mean.
- **Practical Estimation:** $S_f$ often estimated as $\vert G\vert^2 - \vert N\vert^2$ when noise is known.
- **High-Yield Keywords:** MMSE, wide-sense stationary, NSR, OTF, regularization, Lagrange multiplier (CLS).
- **Quick-Recall Heuristic:** "Wiener = Inverse + Noise Floor in the Denominator."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Image Segmentation: Thresholding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST636 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Image Segmentation: Thresholding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Image Segmentation

**Image Segmentation** is the process of partitioning a digital image into multiple meaningful regions (sets of pixels) such that the pixels in each region share homogeneous visual characteristics (intensity, color, or texture), while pixels belonging to different regions are visually distinct from one another.

Formally, if $I$ is the complete image domain, segmentation produces a set of disjoint, non-empty regions $R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n$ such that:

$$\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} R_i = I \quad \text{and} \quad R_i \cap R_j = \emptyset \;\; \forall \; i \neq j$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Segmentation is the bridge between low-level pixel processing and high-level object recognition. Every pixel must belong to **exactly one** region in a hard segmentation. The four classical approaches taught in the KTU syllabus are: **Thresholding**, Edge-Based, Region-Based, and Clustering/Graph-Cut methods.

---

## 1.2 What is Thresholding?

**Thresholding** is the simplest, fastest, and most widely used image segmentation technique that separates foreground objects from the background by comparing pixel intensities against one or more threshold value(s) $T$.

The fundamental single-threshold (bi-level) decision rule is mathematically expressed as:

$$g(x,y) = \begin{cases} 1 \; (\text{Object / Foreground}) & \text{if } f(x,y) \geq T \\ 0 \; (\text{Background}) & \text{if } f(x,y) < T \end{cases}$$

Where:
* $f(x,y)$ ŌåÆ grayscale intensity of the pixel at spatial coordinates $(x,y)$
* $T$ ŌåÆ the threshold intensity value (typically an integer between **0** and **L-1**, where $L = 256$ for 8-bit images)
* $g(x,y)$ ŌåÆ output binary segmentation mask
* $1$ ŌåÆ Object (commonly rendered as **white**, intensity = **255**)
* $0$ ŌåÆ Background (commonly rendered as **black**, intensity = **0**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Curfew Light Switch":** Imagine a dark room containing scattered white marbles. You set a brightness meter to a fixed cutoff of, say, 60%. Any pixel brighter than 60% is "switched ON" (white) ŌĆö these are your marbles. Anything darker stays OFF (black) ŌĆö this is the floor. That cutoff value (60%) is your **threshold T**, and the action of switching ON/OFF based on it is **thresholding**.

---

## 1.3 Why Thresholding? ŌĆö The Real-World Utility

Thresholding is the **first choice** in industrial inspection pipelines because of:

* **Speed:** $O(N)$ complexity for an image of $N$ pixels ŌĆö no iterative solvers needed.
* **Hardware Friendliness:** Easily implemented in FPGA, ASIC, or embedded cameras.
* **Reliability on Bimodal Histograms:** Performs near-perfectly when foreground and background produce two clearly separated intensity peaks.
* **Industrial Applications:** License plate recognition, blood cell counting in medical imaging, defect detection on PCB boards, document binarization for OCR, satellite cloud-cover mapping, and fruit sorting in agriculture.

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Cues for Students:** Open any grayscale image in MATLAB (`rgb2gray`) or Python and call `imhist(I)` to see whether the histogram is **bimodal** (two peaks) ŌĆö if yes, thresholding is your fastest ticket to a clean segmentation. If the histogram is flat or unimodal, you must move to more sophisticated region-based or clustering methods.

---

## 1.4 The Assumed Model Behind Thresholding

Thresholding implicitly assumes that pixels can be classified purely by their intensity value. For this assumption to hold, the scene must satisfy the **Reflectance-Illumination Model**:

$$f(x,y) = i(x,y) \cdot r(x,y)$$

Where $i(x,y)$ is the illumination component and $r(x,y)$ is the reflectance. Uniform lighting produces a clean bimodal histogram; uneven lighting destroys the bimodality and causes thresholding to fail (a problem fixed later by **adaptive/local thresholding**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Thresholding Techniques

The KTU 2024 syllabus organizes thresholding into the following hierarchical structure:

### A. Based on the Number of Thresholds
| Type | Description | Output Tones |
|---|---|---|
| **Bi-level (Single T)** | One threshold separates object from background | 2 |
| **Multi-level (Multiple T)** | Several thresholds classify pixels into many bands | $> 2$ |

### B. Based on How T is Determined
| Type | Description | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Global Thresholding** | A single $T$ for the entire image | Uniform illumination, bimodal histogram |
| **Local / Variable Thresholding** | $T$ depends on local neighborhood statistics | Uneven illumination, document analysis |
| **Dynamic / Adaptive Thresholding** | $T(x,y)$ varies smoothly across image | Shadowed scenes, textured backgrounds |

---

## 2.2 The Global Threshold Selection Algorithm (Gonzalez & Woods)

This iterative algorithm is a guaranteed KTU exam favorite. It refines an initial guess of $T$ until convergence:

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Initialize:** Select an initial estimate of $T$ (commonly, the mean intensity of the entire image).
2. **Partition:** Using $T$, segment the image into two pixel groups:
   * $G_1$ = pixels with intensity $f(x,y) \geq T$
   * $G_2$ = pixels with intensity $f(x,y) < T$
3. **Compute Means:** Calculate the mean intensity of each group:
   * $\mu_1$ = average intensity of pixels in $G_1$
   * $\mu_2$ = average intensity of pixels in $G_2$
4. **Update Threshold:** Compute a new threshold:
   $$T_{new} = \frac{\mu_1 + \mu_2}{2}$$
5. **Repeat Steps 2ŌĆō4** until the difference $|T_{new} - T_{old}|$ falls below a predefined error bound $\epsilon$ (typically $\epsilon = 0.5$ for integer thresholds).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence Guarantee:** This algorithm is guaranteed to converge in a finite number of iterations because the threshold space $[0, 255]$ is finite and bounded, and each iteration strictly reduces (or maintains) the within-group intensity variance.

---

## 2.3 Otsu's Method ŌĆö Maximum Between-Class Variance

**Otsu's method** (Nobuyuki Otsu, 1979) is the most cited fully-automatic global thresholding algorithm. It treats threshold selection as an **unsupervised clustering problem** and chooses $T$ that maximizes the **between-class variance** $\sigma_B^2(T)$.

### Mathematical Foundation (Line-by-Line Derivation)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Normalized Histogram:**
Let $n_i$ be the number of pixels with intensity $i$, and $N$ the total pixel count. The normalized probability mass function is:

$$p_i = \frac{n_i}{N}, \quad \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} p_i = 1$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Class Probabilities (Cumulative Sums):**
For a candidate threshold $t$, define the probability of the background class $C_0$ (intensities $[0, t]$) and the foreground class $C_1$ (intensities $[t+1, L-1]$):

$$w_0(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{t} p_i \quad , \quad w_1(t) = \sum_{i=t+1}^{L-1} p_i = 1 - w_0(t)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Class Mean Intensities:**
The weighted mean intensity of each class is:

$$\mu_0(t) = \frac{\sum_{i=0}^{t} i \cdot p_i}{w_0(t)} \quad , \quad \mu_1(t) = \frac{\sum_{i=t+1}^{L-1} i \cdot p_i}{w_1(t)}$$

The global image mean is:

$$\mu_T = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} i \cdot p_i = w_0 \mu_0 + w_1 \mu_1$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Between-Class Variance:**
$$\sigma_B^2(t) = w_0(t) \, w_1(t) \, \big[\mu_0(t) - \mu_1(t)\big]^2$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Optimal Threshold:**
$$\boxed{T^* = \arg\max_{0 \leq t < L} \;\; \sigma_B^2(t)}$$

The search scans all $L = 256$ candidate thresholds (for 8-bit images), which is computationally trivial in modern hardware.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Otsu's Formula:** The term $\mu_0 - \mu_1$ measures how **far apart** the two cluster centers are, while $w_0 w_1$ penalizes trivial splits (where one class contains almost no pixels). Maximizing their product is equivalent to maximizing the separability of the two classes ŌĆö exactly what a good threshold should achieve.

---

## 2.4 Local / Adaptive Thresholding

When illumination is non-uniform, a global $T$ fails because the *same* pixel intensity may correspond to *object* in a bright region but *background* in a shadowed region. The solution is to compute $T$ locally:

$$T(x,y) = m(x,y) + C$$

Where:
* $m(x,y)$ is the **local mean** intensity computed over an $n \times n$ neighborhood (typically $n \in \{15, 25, 51\}$).
* $C$ is an empirically chosen bias offset (often a small negative constant).

A more robust variant uses the **Gaussian-weighted mean**:

$$T(x,y) = \alpha \cdot \sigma(x,y) + \beta \cdot m(x,y) + C$$

This is the principle behind Sauvola's and Niblack's document binarization algorithms.

---

## 2.5 Multi-Level Thresholding Extension

For color or complex grayscale images, a single threshold is insufficient. Using Otsu's principle extended via **Kapur's maximum entropy** or **Tsallis entropy**, multiple thresholds $T_1, T_2, \dots, T_K$ partition the intensity axis into $K+1$ bands.

For $K = 2$, the objective becomes maximizing:

$$\sigma^2_B(T_1, T_2) = w_0 \mu_0^2 + w_1 \mu_1^2 + w_2 \mu_2^2$$

The brute-force complexity grows combinatorially: $O(L^K)$ ŌĆö hence, real systems use **Genetic Algorithms** or **PSO** for $K \geq 3$.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $g(x,y) = 1$ if $f(x,y) \geq T$, else $0$ | Basic bi-level thresholding | Output: $\{0, 1\}$ |
| 2 | $p_i = n_i / N$ | Normalized histogram | Sum = 1 |
| 3 | $w_k(t) = \sum_{i \in C_k} p_i$ | Class probability (weight) | $[0, 1]$ |
| 4 | $\mu_k(t) = \sum_{i \in C_k} i \, p_i \, / \, w_k$ | Class mean intensity | $[0, 255]$ |
| 5 | $\mu_T = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} i \, p_i$ | Global image mean | $[0, 255]$ |
| 6 | $\sigma_B^2(t) = w_0 w_1 [\mu_0 - \mu_1]^2$ | Between-class variance | $\geq 0$ |
| 7 | $\sigma_W^2(t) = w_0 \sigma_0^2 + w_1 \sigma_1^2$ | Within-class variance | $\geq 0$ |
| 8 | $\sigma_T^2 = \sigma_W^2 + \sigma_B^2$ | Total variance (conservation) | Constant |
| 9 | $T^* = \arg\max_t \, \sigma_B^2(t)$ | Otsu's optimal threshold | $[0, 255]$ |
| 10 | $T_{iter} = (\mu_1 + \mu_2)/2$ | Iterative threshold update | $[0, 255]$ |
| 11 | $T(x,y) = m(x,y) + C$ | Local/adaptive threshold | Varies spatially |
| 12 | $\eta = \sigma_B^2 / \sigma_T^2$ | Separability measure (0 to 1) | Unitless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Trap:** In KTU exam scripts and even in this digital sheet, never write absolute values like $|x|$ inside markdown tables ŌĆö it breaks the column structure. Always write $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

* **Medical Imaging:** Thresholding CT/MRI scans to isolate tumors, bone, or organs before 3D reconstruction.
* **Document Binarization:** Adaptive thresholding of historical manuscripts for OCR (Google Books pipeline).
* **PCB Defect Inspection:** Separating solder pads from board background in SMT assembly lines.
* **Agricultural Sorting:** Distinguishing ripe vs. unripe fruits based on color channels.
* **Traffic Systems:** License plate extraction for automated tolling.
* **Satellite Remote Sensing:** Cloud masking, water-body extraction, urban sprawl mapping.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Otsu's Criterion from First Principles

Otsu's method derives from the principle that the **best threshold** is the one that **maximizes inter-class separability**, formalized via the **Fisher Discriminant Criterion** from statistical pattern recognition.

### Starting Point: Total Variance Decomposition

The total variance of the image intensity distribution is:

$$\sigma_T^2 = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} (i - \mu_T)^2 \, p_i$$

This can be algebraically decomposed (a classical ANOVA result) into:

$$\sigma_T^2 = \sigma_W^2(t) + \sigma_B^2(t)$$

Where:
* $\sigma_W^2(t)$ = within-class variance (intra-cluster scatter)
* $\sigma_B^2(t)$ = between-class variance (inter-cluster scatter)

### Derivation of $\sigma_W^2(t)$:

The within-class variance for class $C_0$ is:

$$\sigma_0^2 = \sum_{i=0}^{t} \frac{(i - \mu_0)^2 \, p_i}{w_0}$$

Similarly for $C_1$. The combined within-class variance is:

$$\sigma_W^2(t) = w_0 \, \sigma_0^2 + w_1 \, \sigma_1^2$$

Expanding $w_0 \, \sigma_0^2$:

$$\sigma_W^2(t) = \sum_{i=0}^{t} (i - \mu_0)^2 \, p_i + \sum_{i=t+1}^{L-1} (i - \mu_1)^2 \, p_i$$

### Derivation of $\sigma_B^2(t)$:

$$\sigma_B^2(t) = w_0 (\mu_0 - \mu_T)^2 + w_1 (\mu_1 - \mu_T)^2$$

Substituting $\mu_T = w_0 \mu_0 + w_1 \mu_1$:

$$\begin{aligned} \sigma_B^2(t) &= w_0 (\mu_0 - w_0 \mu_0 - w_1 \mu_1)^2 + w_1 (\mu_1 - w_0 \mu_0 - w_1 \mu_1)^2 \\ &= w_0 (w_1 \mu_0 - w_1 \mu_1)^2 + w_1 (w_0 \mu_1 - w_0 \mu_0)^2 \\ &= w_0 w_1^2 (\mu_0 - \mu_1)^2 + w_0^2 w_1 (\mu_1 - \mu_0)^2 \\ &= w_0 w_1 (\mu_0 - \mu_1)^2 \, (w_1 + w_0) \\ &= w_0 w_1 (\mu_0 - \mu_1)^2 \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned}$$

**Physical Meaning:** The separability of the two intensity clusters is proportional to both their **probability mass** ($w_0 w_1$) and the **square of their mean separation** $(\mu_0 - \mu_1)^2$. Maximizing $\sigma_B^2$ simultaneously maximizes cluster size balance and separation distance.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö Otsu's Method on a Synthetic 8-Intensity Image

Suppose a 6├Ś6 grayscale image (36 pixels) has the following intensity histogram:

| Intensity $i$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Count $n_i$ | 2 | 4 | 6 | 4 | 2 | 8 | 6 | 4 |
| Probability $p_i = n_i / 36$ | 1/18 | 2/18 | 3/18 | 2/18 | 1/18 | 4/18 | 3/18 | 2/18 |

### Step 1: Compute Global Mean $\mu_T$

$$\begin{aligned} \mu_T &= \sum_{i=0}^{7} i \cdot p_i \\ &= \frac{0(2) + 1(4) + 2(6) + 3(4) + 4(2) + 5(8) + 6(6) + 7(4)}{36} \\ &= \frac{0 + 4 + 12 + 12 + 8 + 40 + 36 + 28}{36} \\ &= \frac{140}{36} = 3.889 \end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Test Candidate Thresholds

**Candidate $t = 3$ (split between intensity 3 and 4):**

$$\begin{aligned} w_0 &= \frac{2+4+6+4}{36} = \frac{16}{36} = 0.444 \\ w_1 &= \frac{2+8+6+4}{36} = \frac{20}{36} = 0.556 \\ \mu_0 &= \frac{0(2)+1(4)+2(6)+3(4)}{16} = \frac{28}{16} = 1.75 \\ \mu_1 &= \frac{4(2)+5(8)+6(6)+7(4)}{20} = \frac{108}{20} = 5.40 \\ \sigma_B^2(3) &= (0.444)(0.556)(1.75 - 5.40)^2 \\ &= (0.247)(13.32) = 3.29 \end{aligned}$$

**Candidate $t = 4$:**

$$\begin{aligned} w_0 &= \frac{18}{36} = 0.500 \\ w_1 &= \frac{18}{36} = 0.500 \\ \mu_0 &= \frac{0+4+12+12+8}{18} = \frac{36}{18} = 2.00 \\ \mu_1 &= \frac{40+36+28}{18} = \frac{104}{18} = 5.778 \\ \sigma_B^2(4) &= (0.500)(0.500)(2.00 - 5.778)^2 \\ &= (0.250)(14.27) = 3.567 \end{aligned}$$

**Candidate $t = 5$:**

$$\begin{aligned} w_0 &= \frac{22}{36} = 0.611 \\ w_1 &= \frac{14}{36} = 0.389 \\ \mu_0 &= \frac{0+4+12+12+8+40}{22} = \frac{76}{22} = 3.455 \\ \mu_1 &= \frac{36+28}{14} = \frac{64}{14} = 4.571 \\ \sigma_B^2(5) &= (0.611)(0.389)(3.455 - 4.571)^2 \\ &= (0.238)(1.244) = 0.296 \end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Select Optimal Threshold

$$\boxed{T^* = \arg\max_t \, \sigma_B^2(t) = 4}$$

Because $\sigma_B^2(4) = 3.567$ is the largest of all tested values. Pixels with intensity $\geq 4$ become **foreground**, those with intensity $< 4$ become **background**.

### Step 4: Separability Index

$$\eta = \frac{\sigma_B^2(T^*)}{\sigma_T^2} = \frac{3.567}{3.889} \approx 0.917$$

Since $\eta \rightarrow 1$, the threshold is excellent ŌĆö almost 92% of the intensity variance is explained by the two-class split.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö Iterative Global Thresholding

Using the same histogram above:

**Iteration 1:**
* Initial guess: $T_0 = 3.889 \approx 4$
* Partition: $G_1 = \{0,1,2,3\}$ and $G_2 = \{4,5,6,7\}$
* $\mu_1 = 1.75$ (from $G_1$), $\mu_2 = 5.778$ (from $G_2$)
* $T_1 = (1.75 + 5.778)/2 = 3.764 \approx 4$

**Iteration 2:**
* Use $T = 4$ ŌåÆ same partition ŌåÆ $T_2 = 3.764$

Since $|T_2 - T_1| = 0 < \epsilon$, **algorithm converges at $T^* = 4$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Notice the agreement:** Both Otsu's method and the iterative method independently converged on $T = 4$, but Otsu's method provides a *guaranteed optimal* solution under its separability criterion, whereas the iterative method depends on the initial guess.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Algorithm Comparison Table

| Criterion | Global (Iterative) | Otsu's Method | Local/Adaptive |
|---|---|---|---|
| Computational Cost | $O(N \cdot k)$, $k$ = iterations | $O(N + 256)$ | $O(N \cdot n^2)$ |
| Best for | Bimodal + uniform lighting | Bimodal, unsupervised | Uneven lighting |
| User Input Required | Initial $T$ guess | None | Window size, $C$ |
| Optimality Guarantee | Local optimum only | Globally optimal | Locally optimal |
| Sensitivity to Noise | High | Medium | Lower |
| Multi-Class Support | Manual extension | Easily extended | Complicated |
| KTU Exam Frequency | ŌśģŌśģŌśģŌśģŌśå | ŌśģŌśģŌśģŌśģŌśģ | ŌśģŌśģŌśģŌśåŌśå |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture of Image Segmentation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Input Grayscale Image f x y"] --> nodeB["Compute Histogram"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"Bimodal Check"}
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeD["Apply Thresholding"]
    nodeC -- No --> nodeE["Edge / Region Methods"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Global Threshold"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["Otsu Method"]
    nodeD --> nodeH["Local Adaptive"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["Binary Mask g x y"]
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Object Extraction"]
    nodeE --> nodeJ
```

## 4.2 Otsu's Method ŌĆö Complete Algorithmic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    stepA["Start: Load Image I"] --> stepB["Compute Histogram p_i"]
    stepB --> stepC["Compute Global Mean mu_T"]
    stepC --> stepD{"Loop t from 0 to L-1"}
    stepD --> stepE["Compute w0 and w1"]
    stepE --> stepF["Compute mu0 and mu1"]
    stepF --> stepG["Compute Sigma_B^2 of t"]
    stepG --> stepH["Store if Maximum"]
    stepH --> stepI{"t equals L-1?"}
    stepI -- No --> stepD
    stepI -- Yes --> stepJ["Output Optimal T Star"]
    stepJ --> stepK["Apply Threshold to f x y"]
    stepK --> stepL["Generate Binary Mask g x y"]
```

## 4.3 Iterative Global Thresholding ŌĆö Convergence Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startA["Initialize T with Mean Intensity"] --> partA["Partition Pixels into G1 and G2"]
    partA --> meanA["Compute Mean of G1 as mu_1"]
    partA --> meanB["Compute Mean of G2 as mu_2"]
    meanA --> calcT["T_new equals mu_1 plus mu_2 divided by 2"]
    meanB --> calcT
    calcT --> checkA{"Absolute T_new minus T_old less than epsilon?"}
    checkA -- No --> partA
    checkA -- Yes --> outputA["Output Converged T Star"]
```

## 4.4 Adaptive (Local) Thresholding ŌĆö Spatial Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inImg["Input Image f x y with Non-Uniform Illumination"] --> slideWin["Sliding n by n Window"]
    slideWin --> localMean["Compute Local Mean m x y"]
    slideWin --> localSD["Compute Local Standard Deviation"]
    localMean --> bias["Add Bias Constant C"]
    bias --> localT["Local Threshold T x y equals m x y plus C"]
    localT --> compare["Compare f x y with T x y"]
    inImg --> compare
    compare --> outMask["Output Adaptive Binary Mask"]
```

## 4.5 Functional Block Diagram ŌĆö Threshold Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    img["Grayscale Image"] --> q1{"Histogram Bimodal?"}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{"Lighting Uniform?"}
    q1 -- No --> region["Use Region Growing or Clustering"]
    q2 -- Yes --> otsu["Apply Otsu Method"]
    q2 -- No --> adapt["Apply Local Adaptive Thresholding"]
    otsu --> out["Binary Segmentation Mask"]
    adapt --> out
    region --> out
```
