Therefore: $$ f(i, j) = c(i, j) + \min \begin{Bmatrix} f(i-1, j-1) \\ f(i-1, j) \\ f(i-1, j+1) \end{Bmatrix} \quad \text{for } i \ge 1 $$ with **boundary condition** (row 0 has no predecessors, so the cumulative cost equals the local cost): $$ f(0, j) = c(0, j), \quad j = 0, 1, \dots, M-1 $$ For pixels at the **leftmost and rightmost columns**, the predecessor set is truncated to keep indices valid (a **wrap-free** boundary): $$ f(i, 0) = c(i, 0) + \min \begin{Bmatrix} f(i-1, 0) \\ f(i-1, 1) \end{Bmatrix} $$ $$ f(i, M-1) = c(i, M-1) + \min \begin{Bmatrix} f(i-1, M-2) \\ f(i-1, M-1) \end{Bmatrix} $$ The **optimal end column** is the one minimizing $f$ in the last row: $$ j^* = \arg\min_{0 \le j < M} f(N-1, j) $$ The **optimal border pixels** are recovered by backtracking from $(N-1, j^*)$ to row $0$, always choosing the predecessor that realized the minimum at each step. --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (4 ├Ś 5 Image) Consider a $4 \times 5$ cost image (rows 0ŌĆō3, columns 0ŌĆō4) where **low values indicate edges**. We will find the minimum-cost path from row 0 to row 3. **Step 1 ŌĆö Cost Image (input):** $$ C = \begin{bmatrix} 9 & 8 & 2 & 7 & 6 \\ 7 & 6 & 1 & 5 & 4 \\ 8 & 5 & 1 & 4 & 9 \\ 6 & 4 & 2 & 3 & 5 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Initialize cumulative image $F$ with row 0:** $$ F = \begin{bmatrix} 9 & 8 & 2 & 7 & 6 \\ \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot \\ \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot \\ \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot & \cdot \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Row 1 (i = 1):** apply $f(1, j) = c(1, j) + \min(f(0, j-1), f(0, j), f(0, j+1))$. For $j = 0$: $f(1, 0) = 7 + \min(9, 8) = 7 + 8 = 15$ For $j = 1$: $f(1, 1) = 6 + \min(9, 8, 2) = 6 + 2 = 8$ For $j = 2$: $f(1, 2) = 1 + \min(8, 2, 7) = 1 + 2 = 3$ For $j = 3$: $f(1, 3) = 5 + \min(2, 7, 6) = 5 + 2 = 7$ For $j = 4$: $f(1, 4) = 4 + \min(7, 6) = 4 + 6 = 10$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Row 2 (i = 2):** For $j = 0$: $f(2, 0) = 8 + \min(15, 8) = 8 + 8 = 16$ For $j = 1$: $f(2, 1) = 5 + \min(15, 8, 3) = 5 + 3 = 8$ For $j = 2$: $f(2, 2) = 1 + \min(8, 3, 7) = 1 + 3 = 4$ For $j = 3$: $f(2, 3) = 4 + \min(3, 7, 10) = 4 + 3 = 7$ For $j = 4$: $f(2, 4) = 9 + \min(7, 10) = 9 + 7 = 16$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Row 3 (i = 3):** For $j = 0$: $f(3, 0) = 6 + \min(16, 8) = 6 + 8 = 14$ For $j = 1$: $f(3, 1) = 4 + \min(16, 8, 4) = 4 + 4 = 8$ For $j = 2$: $f(3, 2) = 2 + \min(8, 4, 7) = 2 + 4 = 6$ For $j = 3$: $f(3, 3) = 3 + \min(4, 7, 16) = 3 + 4 = 7$ For $j = 4$: $f(3, 4) = 5 + \min(7, 16) = 5 + 7 = 12$ **Cumulative image so far:** $$ F = \begin{bmatrix} 9 & 8 & 2 & 7 & 6 \\ 15 & 8 & 3 & 7 & 10 \\ 16 & 8 & 4 & 7 & 16 \\ 14 & 8 & 6 & 7 & 12 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Identify optimal end-point:** examine row 3: $(14, 8, 6, 7, 12)$. The minimum is **6 at $j = 2$**, so $j^* = 2$, $F_{\text{opt}} = 6$. **Step 7 ŌĆö Backtrack the optimal path:** | Row | $j$ | Chosen predecessor (column) | Reason | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | | 3 | 2 | ŌĆö | End-point, $f = 6$ | | 2 | 2 | $j = 2$ came from $f(1, 2) = 3$ | min of $(8, 3, 7)$ is at $j=1$ giving 3, so $j=1$ from row 1 | | 1 | 1 | $f(0, 2) = 2$ was the min | min of $(9, 8, 2)$ is at $j=0$ (col 2 in row 0) | | 0 | 2 | start | Initialization | > [!NOTE] > **Re-tracing carefully with index convention** $j$ = column in **current** row's predecessor lookup: > - At $(3,2)$, $\min$ of $F(2,1)=8, F(2,2)=4, F(2,3)=7$ is at column **2** (value 4). So predecessor is $(2, 2)$. > - At $(2,2)$, $\min$ of $F(1,1)=8, F(1,2)=3, F(1,3)=7$ is at column **2** (value 3). So predecessor is $(1, 2)$. > - At $(1,2)$, $\min$ of $F(0,1)=8, F(0,2)=2, F(0,3)=7$ is at column **2** (value 2). So predecessor is $(0, 2)$. **Optimal Border Path** (row, col): $$ (0, 2) \;\to\; (1, 2) \;\to\; (2, 2) \;\to\; (3, 2) $$ This is the **central vertical contour** ŌĆö exactly what the cost image was designed to encourage. **Step 8 ŌĆö Verification:** total cost = $c(0,2) + c(1,2) + c(2,2) + c(3,2) = 2 + 1 + 1 + 2 = 6$ Ō£ō (matches $F_{\text{opt}}$). --- ### 3.3 Algorithmic Complexity Analysis | Metric | Value | Reason | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Time (Forward pass)** | $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot M)$ | One min-of-3 per pixel | | **Time (Backtracking)** | $\mathcal{O}(N)$ | Single walk from row $N-1$ to row $0$ | | **Total time** | $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot M)$ | Dominated by forward pass | | **Space (Full table)** | $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot M)$ | Store $F$ and predecessor pointers | | **Space (Rolling array)** | $\mathcal{O}(M)$ | Keep only last 2 rows + predecessor array | > [!TIP] > In KTU answers, **always mention $\mathcal{O}(NM)$ complexity** ŌĆö it is a frequently asked comparison point with BFS/DFS alternatives. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow The following Mermaid diagram captures the full pipeline of the DP border detector ŌĆö from the raw image to the recovered optimal contour. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Input Image
f i j] --> B[Gradient Operator
Sobel or Prewitt] B --> C[Gradient Magnitude
G i j] C --> D[Cost Image Construction
c i j equals Gmax minus G i j] D --> E[DP Forward Pass
Row 0 to Row N minus 1] E --> F[Cumulative Cost Matrix
F i j] F --> G[Find End Point
j star equals argmin of F N minus 1 j] G --> H[DP Backtracking
Walk from N minus 1 to 0] H --> I[Store Predecessor
Pointer at each step] I --> J[Reconstruct Border Path
Sequence of pixels] J --> K[Final Detected Border
Overlay on input image] F --> L[Reporting
Minimum total cost Fopt] ``` ### 4.2 Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) View of Pixel Connectivity The same algorithm can be visualised as a DAG where each pixel is a node and edges go strictly from row $i-1$ to row $i$ (3-connectivity). The DP recurrence is exactly the **shortest-path relaxation** in this DAG. ```mermaid flowchart LR R0A[Row 0 col 0] --> R1A[Row 1 col 0] R0A --> R1B[Row 1 col 1] R0B[Row 0 col 1] --> R1A R0B --> R1B R0B --> R1C[Row 1 col 2] R0C[Row 0 col 2] --> R1B R0C --> R1C R0C --> R1D[Row 1 col 3] R1A --> R2A[Row 2 col 0] R1A --> R2B[Row 2 col 1] R1B --> R2A R1B --> R2B R1B --> R2C[Row 2 col 2] R1C --> R2B R1C --> R2C R1C --> R2D[Row 2 col 3] ``` > [!NOTE] > Note the **strictly downward direction of edges** ŌĆö this is what guarantees the graph is **acyclic**, which is the key precondition for DP's correctness. ### 4.3 Two-Phase Process View (Forward Pass + Backtracking) ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Phase 1 Forward Pass] --> P1a[Initialize Row 0 with c 0 j] P1a --> P1b[For each row i from 1 to N minus 1] P1b --> P1c[For each column j compute f i j
using min of three predecessors] P1c --> P1d[Store predecessor pointer] P1d --> P2[Phase 2 Termination] P2 --> P2a[Find j star with minimum f N minus 1 j] P2a --> P3[Phase 3 Backtracking] P3 --> P3a[Start at row N minus 1 column j star] P3a --> P3b[Move to predecessor recorded in Phase 1] P3b --> P3c[Repeat until row 0 reached] P3c --> P3d[Output the recovered border path] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** > *Define **border detection as dynamic programming**. Mention the two properties of a problem that make it solvable by dynamic programming. (3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** Border detection via dynamic programming is a **graph-search technique** that finds the **minimum-cost contour** between two opposite boundaries of an image by treating pixels as graph nodes and the contour as a path whose cumulative cost is the sum of per-pixel local costs. The two required properties are: 1. **Optimal substructure** ŌĆö the optimal path to a node can be expressed in terms of optimal paths to its predecessors. 2. **Overlapping sub-problems** ŌĆö the same sub-paths are reused many times, making memoization worthwhile. > **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Sample Paper]** > *State the **boundary condition** and the **recurrence relation** used in the dynamic programming formulation of border detection. (3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** Boundary condition (first row, no predecessors): $$ f(0, j) = c(0, j), \quad j = 0, 1, \dots, M-1 $$ Recurrence relation (for $i \ge 1$, with 3-connected moves): $$ f(i, j) = c(i, j) + \min \begin{Bmatrix} f(i-1, j-1) \\ f(i-1, j) \\ f(i-1, j+1) \end{Bmatrix} $$ where $c(i, j)$ is the local cost at pixel $(i, j)$ and $f(i, j)$ is the minimum cumulative cost of any valid path ending at $(i, j)$. --- ### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice) > **Q3. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022, Modified]** > **(a)** Explain in detail how **dynamic programming** is used for border detection in digital images. Derive the recurrence relation, define the cost function, and discuss the role of boundary conditions. **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** A $4 \times 4$ cost image is given below (low values = strong edge). Apply the dynamic programming algorithm step-by-step to find the **minimum-cost border path** from row 0 to row 3. Show both the forward cumulative cost matrix $F(i, j)$ and the recovered path. **(7 Marks)** $$ C = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 6 & 5 & 3 \\ 5 & 2 & 7 & 4 \\ 6 & 1 & 3 & 8 \\ 3 & 4 & 2 & 5 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a):** **Step 1 ŌĆö Image as a DAG:** Treat the image as a directed acyclic graph. Each pixel $(i, j)$ is a node. For 3-connectivity, edges exist from $(i-1, j-1), (i-1, j), (i-1, j+1)$ to $(i, j)$. **Step 2 ŌĆö Cost function construction:** Compute the gradient magnitude $G(i, j)$ (e.g., via Sobel). Convert to a *cost* by inverting: $$ c(i, j) = G_{\max} - G(i, j) $$ so that edge pixels have **low cost** and non-edge pixels have **high cost**. Optionally add a **smoothness penalty** $d_{\max} \cdot (1 - \cos\theta)$ to penalize sharp direction changes between consecutive path pixels. **Step 3 ŌĆö Boundary condition (row 0):** No predecessors exist; hence: $$ f(0, j) = c(0, j), \quad \forall j $$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Recurrence relation:** for any row $i \ge 1$: $$ f(i, j) = c(i, j) + \min \begin{Bmatrix} f(i-1, j-1) \\ f(i-1, j) \\ f(i-1, j+1) \end{Bmatrix} $$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Termination:** identify the optimal end column: $$ j^* = \arg\min_j f(N-1, j) $$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Backtracking:** starting at $(N-1, j^*)$, follow the recorded predecessor pointer at each step back to row 0. The visited pixels form the **optimal border**. **Step 7 ŌĆö Complexity:** time = $\mathcal{O}(NM)$, space = $\mathcal{O}(NM)$ (or $\mathcal{O}(M)$ with rolling arrays). **Why it works:** the image-to-DAG reduction is **acyclic** (edges only go from row $i-1$ to row $i$), which satisfies the topological-order requirement of DP. The minimum cost of a *path* through such a graph is exactly the cumulative minimum $f(i, j)$. --- **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b):** **Row 0 (initialization):** copy cost row to $F$: $$ F^{(0)} = [\, 4 \;\; 6 \;\; 5 \;\; 3 \,] $$ **Row 1 computation** using $f(1,j) = c(1,j) + \min(f(0,j-1), f(0,j), f(0,j+1))$: $$ f(1, 0) = 5 + \min(4, 6) = 5 + 4 = 9 $$ $$ f(1, 1) = 2 + \min(4, 6, 5) = 2 + 4 = 6 $$ $$ f(1, 2) = 7 + \min(6, 5, 3) = 7 + 3 = 10 $$ $$ f(1, 3) = 4 + \min(5, 3) = 4 + 3 = 7 $$ So $F^{(1)} = [\, 9 \;\; 6 \;\; 10 \;\; 7 \,]$. **Row 2 computation:** $$ f(2, 0) = 6 + \min(9, 6) = 6 + 6 = 12 $$ $$ f(2, 1) = 1 + \min(9, 6, 10) = 1 + 6 = 7 $$ $$ f(2, 2) = 3 + \min(6, 10, 7) = 3 + 6 = 9 $$ $$ f(2, 3) = 8 + \min(10, 7) = 8 + 7 = 15 $$ So $F^{(2)} = [\, 12 \;\; 7 \;\; 9 \;\; 15 \,]$. **Row 3 computation:** $$ f(3, 0) = 3 + \min(12, 7) = 3 + 7 = 10 $$ $$ f(3, 1) = 4 + \min(12, 7, 9) = 4 + 7 = 11 $$ $$ f(3, 2) = 2 + \min(7, 9, 15) = 2 + 7 = 9 $$ $$ f(3, 3) = 5 + \min(9, 15) = 5 + 9 = 14 $$ So $F^{(3)} = [\, 10 \;\; 11 \;\; 9 \;\; 14 \,]$. **Final cumulative matrix:** $$ F = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 6 & 5 & 3 \\ 9 & 6 & 10 & 7 \\ 12 & 7 & 9 & 15 \\ 10 & 11 & 9 & 14 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Termination:** minimum of row 3 is $9$ at $j = 2$. So $j^* = 2$, $F_{\text{opt}} = 9$. **Backtracking** (tracing the minimum predecessor at each step): | Step | Current $(i, j)$ | $F(i,j)$ | Min predecessor $(i-1, k)$ | Reason | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | | 1 | $(3, 2)$ | 9 | $(2, 1)$ | $\min(12, 7, 9) = 7$ at $k=1$ | | 2 | $(2, 1)$ | 7 | $(1, 1)$ | $\min(9, 6, 10) = 6$ at $k=1$ | | 3 | $(1, 1)$ | 6 | $(0, 0)$ | $\min(4, 6, 5) = 4$ at $k=0$ | | 4 | $(0, 0)$ | 4 | start | Initialization | **Recovered border path:** $$ (0, 0) \;\to\; (1, 1) \;\to\; (2, 1) \;\to\; (3, 2) $$ **Verification:** total cost = $4 + 2 + 1 + 2 = 9$ Ō£ō (matches $F_{\text{opt}}$). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting boundary conditions** at the leftmost ($j=0$) and rightmost ($j=M-1$) columns. The predecessor set must be **truncated** ŌĆö full 3-min applied to $j=0$ or $j=M-1$ is *wrong* and will cost you 1ŌĆō2 marks. > 2. **Confusing "cost" with "gradient".** Cost is the *inverse* ŌĆö high gradient = low cost. Examiners specifically check this distinction. > 3. **Skipping the backtracking step** in numerical problems. Forward pass alone is worth only 4 of the 7 marks; backtracking + path listing carries the remaining 3. > 4. **Not stating $\mathcal{O}(NM)$ complexity** explicitly. Always include complexity in any "explain in detail" question. --- > **Q4. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Internal Choice Alternative]** > **(a)** Discuss the **limitations of simple edge detection** (e.g., Sobel followed by thresholding) and explain how **dynamic programming overcomes them** in the context of border detection. Compare DP with the **Hough transform** as alternative global methods. **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** Explain **Martelli's cost function** formulation, which combines a **local cost** (based on gradient) and a **smoothness penalty** (based on direction change). Write the combined cost expression and justify why both terms are necessary. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a):** **Limitations of simple edge detection:** 1. **Thresholding breaks contours** ŌĆö a single weak gradient pixel can interrupt an otherwise continuous edge. 2. **No global optimality** ŌĆö local decisions (threshold + thinning) do not guarantee the *best* global contour. 3. **Sensitive to noise** ŌĆö spurious high-gradient speckles cause false edges. 4. **No semantic constraint** ŌĆö nothing forces the detected edge to be a *smooth* closed contour. **How DP overcomes these:** 1. DP optimizes a **global criterion** (sum of per-pixel costs) ŌĆö local noise becomes a small perturbation, dominated by the cumulative cost. 2. The **3-connected move set** enforces smoothness ŌĆö sharp direction changes are implicitly penalised. 3. The **optimal substructure** guarantees that the recovered path is the *minimum-cost* one ŌĆö there is no possibility of being trapped in a local minimum. 4. DP also produces a **ranked set** of contours (by adjusting the cost threshold), useful for multi-hypothesis tracking. **Comparison with Hough Transform:** | Feature | Dynamic Programming | Hough Transform | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Best for | Open / closed **smooth contours** | **Parametric shapes** (lines, circles, ellipses) | | Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(NM)$ | $\mathcal{O}(NM \cdot K)$ ŌĆö $K$ = parameter bins | | Output | Optimal path pixels | Votes in parameter space | | Shape flexibility | Arbitrary monotonic contours | Restricted to known parametric forms | | Noise robustness | High (global optimization) | Moderate (voting-based) | **Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b):** **Martelli's combined cost function:** Let $(i, j_i)$ and $(i+1, j_{i+1})$ be two consecutive points on the candidate path. The local cost at $(i, j_i)$ is the gradient magnitude (high at edges): $$ c_1(i, j_i) = G(i, j_i) $$ The smoothness penalty discourages sharp direction changes. Define the local direction angle $\theta_i$ at step $i$; then the direction-change penalty is: $$ c_2(i, j_i) = d_{\max} \cdot \bigl(1 - \cos(\theta_{i+1} - \theta_i)\bigr) $$ where $d_{\max}$ is a user-set maximum penalty. The **total per-pixel cost** is: $$ c(i, j_i) = G(i, j_i) + d_{\max} \cdot \bigl(1 - \cos(\theta_{i+1} - \theta_i)\bigr) $$ **Why both terms are necessary:** - **Gradient term $G$** ŌĆö drives the contour onto strong edges (where the actual boundary lies). - **Smoothness term** ŌĆö penalises zig-zag paths that would otherwise hop on and off a noisy edge pixel. - Without the gradient term, the path would take the *shortest* (straight-down) route regardless of image content. - Without the smoothness term, the path would be a noisy staircase following every isolated edge pixel. Together, they implement a **regularized optimization** ŌĆö exactly analogous to a *path-length + data-fidelity* trade-off in modern active-contour (snake) models. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö Border Detection via Dynamic Programming** - **Core idea:** convert the image into a DAG; find the **minimum cumulative cost path** from one side to the other. - **Two DP preconditions satisfied:** *optimal substructure* (recurrence on predecessors) + *overlapping sub-problems* (many paths share sub-routes). - **Cost image:** typically $c(i, j) = G_{\max} - G(i, j)$ ŌĆö **invert** the gradient. - **Boundary condition:** $f(0, j) = c(0, j)$ for all $j$ ŌĆö **first row is the seed**. - **Recurrence (3-connected):** $$ f(i, j) = c(i, j) + \min \begin{Bmatrix} f(i-1, j-1) \\ f(i-1, j) \\ f(i-1, j+1) \end{Bmatrix} $$ - **Edge handling:** at $j=0$, drop $f(i-1, j-1)$; at $j=M-1$, drop $f(i-1, j+1)$. - **Termination:** $j^* = \arg\min_j f(N-1, j)$. - **Path recovery:** **backtrack** from $(N-1, j^*)$ using stored predecessor pointers. - **Complexity:** time $\mathcal{O}(NM)$, space $\mathcal{O}(NM)$ (or $\mathcal{O}(M)$ with rolling arrays). - **Martelli's variant:** adds a smoothness penalty $d_{\max}(1 - \cos\Delta\theta)$ ŌĆö combines gradient strength with direction coherence. - **Key advantages over local methods:** global optimality, noise robustness, smooth contours, ranked hypotheses. - **Typical exam pitfalls:** (i) confusing cost with gradient, (ii) forgetting truncated boundaries at column edges, (iii) skipping backtracking in numerical problems, (iv) not stating complexity. - **Real-world domains:** medical imaging (cardiac borders), industrial inspection (crack tracing), remote sensing (river/coast detection), document OCR (character outlines). - **Memory hook:** *DP = "**D**irectional **P**ath of least cost"* ŌĆö always go from row 0 to row $N-1$, never the reverse in the forward pass.

topics.title: Hough Transforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST636 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Hough Transforms ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hough Transform (HT):** A feature extraction technique used in digital image processing to detect parametric shapes (lines, circles, ellipses, or arbitrary curves) by employing a voting procedure carried out in a *parameter space*. The classical formulation, introduced by **Paul Hough (1962)** and later refined by **Duda and Hart (1972)**, transforms the global shape detection problem into a localized peak-detection problem in an accumulator array. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Hough Transform is positioned as a post-edge-detection global operator. Given a binary edge map, the transform establishes a many-to-many mapping from the **Cartesian image space** $(x, y)$ to a **parameter (Hough) space** $(\rho, \theta)$. > [!NOTE] > **Why we use Polar form and not slope-intercept form:** The Cartesian form $y = mx + c$ becomes numerically unstable for vertical lines because $m \to \infty$. The polar form $x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta = \rho$ treats all line orientations uniformly across $\theta \in [0, \pi]$. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing in a packed auditorium trying to find people who have the same height and shirt color combination. Rather than asking each person individually whether they match some criterion, you ask every person to *announce* their combination, and then you record the combination that is announced most often. The winning combination corresponds to the largest "cluster" ŌĆö the answer. **Hough Transform works the same way:** - Each *edge pixel* does not "decide" which line it belongs to. - Instead, it **votes** for *every possible line* that could pass through it. - The line receiving the most votes (highest peak in the accumulator) is the detected line. | Real-world analogy | Hough Transform counterpart | |---|---| | Person in the audience | Edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$ | | Announcement of attributes | Vote cast in $(\rho, \theta)$ space | | Tallies on a board | Accumulator array $A(\rho, \theta)$ | | Most announced combination | Peak (detected line) | ### 1.3 Core Terminology - **Edge Point:** A pixel flagged as belonging to an edge (input from Canny, Sobel, Prewitt, or similar). - **Parameter Space:** A multi-dimensional grid indexed by shape parameters (e.g., $\rho$ and $\theta$ for lines). - **Accumulator Array $A(\rho, \theta)$:** A discrete 2-D histogram where each cell stores the number of votes for a specific $(\rho, \theta)$ hypothesis. - **Voting:** The act of incrementing $A(\rho, \theta)$ cells consistent with an edge point. - **Peak Detection:** Identifying local maxima in the accumulator that exceed a threshold. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PECST636):** The Hough Transform is studied under the broader module covering *boundary and shape representation*. The expected cognitive level is **Apply / Analyze** (Bloom Level 3ŌĆō4), with derivations tested at the 14-mark depth. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Geometric Foundation A straight line in 2-D can be expressed in two principal forms: **SlopeŌĆōIntercept Form:** $$y = mx + c$$ where $m$ is the slope and $c$ is the y-intercept. This parameterization fails for **vertical lines** ($m \to \infty$). **Polar (Normal) Form ŌĆö Duda & Hart:** $$x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta = \rho$$ where: - $\rho$ is the perpendicular distance from the origin to the line. - $\theta$ is the angle made by the normal from the origin to the line, measured with respect to the x-axis. ### 2.2 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step) 1. **Edge Preprocessing:** Apply an edge detector (typically Canny) to obtain a binary edge map $E(x, y)$. 2. **Discretize the Parameter Space:** Quantize $\theta \in [0, \pi]$ into $N_\theta$ bins and $\rho \in [-\rho_{\max}, \rho_{\max}]$ into $N_\rho$ bins, where $\rho_{\max} = \sqrt{W^2 + H^2}$ for a $W \times H$ image. 3. **Initialize Accumulator:** Set all cells $A(\rho_k, \theta_l) = 0$. 4. **Iterate Over Edge Pixels:** For each $(x_i, y_i)$ where $E(x_i, y_i) = 1$: - For each discrete $\theta_l$ in $[0, \pi]$: - Compute $\rho = x_i \cos\theta_l + y_i \sin\theta_l$. - Round $\rho$ to the nearest discrete bin $\rho_k$. - Increment $A(\rho_k, \theta_l) \leftarrow A(\rho_k, \theta_l) + 1$. 5. **Peak Detection:** Threshold $A(\rho, \theta)$ and find local maxima. Each maximum corresponds to a detected line. ### 2.3 Mathematical Insight ŌĆö Why it Works **Collinear Points Converge:** If $N$ edge pixels lie on a true line with parameters $(\rho^*, \theta^*)$, then when processed, each of them will produce the *same* $\rho = \rho^*$ for $\theta = \theta^*$, contributing $N$ votes to $A(\rho^*, \theta^*)$. Noise pixels, being uncorrelated, distribute their votes sparsely. **Sinusoidal Signature in Parameter Space:** A single point $(x_0, y_0)$ in image space, when mapped to the parameter space via $\rho = x_0 \cos\theta + y_0 \sin\theta$, traces a **sinusoidal curve**. Two points $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$ lying on the same line produce two sinusoids that **intersect at exactly one common point** ŌĆö the parameters of that line. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula | Meaning | Range / Units | |---|---|---| | $y = mx + c$ | Cartesian line equation | $m \in \mathbb{R}$, $c \in \mathbb{R}$ | | $x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta = \rho$ | Polar (normal) form of a line | $\theta \in [0, \pi)$, $\rho \in \mathbb{R}$ | | $\rho = x_0 \cos\theta + y_0 \sin\theta$ | Sinusoidal curve for a single point in $(\rho, \theta)$ space | $\theta$ is the independent variable | | $(x - a)^2 + (y - b)^2 = r^2$ | Circle equation in image space | $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$, $r > 0$ | | $a = x - r\cos\theta$, $b = y - r\sin\theta$ | Center coordinates in Hough circle transform | $\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$ | | $\rho_{\max} = \sqrt{W^2 + H^2}$ | Maximum possible $\rho$ for an image of size $W \times H$ | Pixels | | $A(\rho_k, \theta_l)$ | Accumulator cell value | Integer count | | $\vert \nabla I(x, y) \vert > T$ | Canny-style edge threshold condition | Gradient magnitude | | $\theta_{\text{quant}} = \dfrac{\pi}{N_\theta}$ | Resolution of angular discretization | Radians | | $\Delta\rho = \dfrac{2\rho_{\max}}{N_\rho}$ | Resolution of radial discretization | Pixels | > [!NOTE] > **Units Convention:** $\theta$ is always in **radians** in mathematics; KTU exams may use degrees ŌĆö convert with $\theta_{\text{rad}} = \theta_{\text{deg}} \cdot \pi / 180$. Always mention the convention in your answer to score full marks. ### 2.5 Hough Transform for Circles A circle is parameterized by three variables: center $(a, b)$ and radius $r$. $$(x_i - a)^2 + (y_i - b)^2 = r^2$$ For a fixed radius $r$ and a known edge point $(x_i, y_i)$, the locus of possible centers is a circle of radius $r$ centered at $(x_i, y_i)$. Hence, the accumulator becomes 3-D: $A(a, b, r)$. When $r$ is known a priori, the search reduces to a 2-D accumulator $A(a, b)$. For a variable radius, the algorithm is: 1. For each edge point $(x_i, y_i)$: - For each candidate radius $r_j$ in $[r_{\min}, r_{\max}]$: - For each $\theta_l \in [0, 2\pi)$: - Compute $a = x_i - r_j \cos\theta_l$, $b = y_i - r_j \sin\theta_l$. - Increment $A(a, b, r_j)$. > [!WARNING] > **Computational Cost Warning:** Circle Hough Transform is **$O(N_{\text{edge}} \times N_r \times N_\theta)$** ŌĆö easily **3 orders of magnitude slower** than line HT. Always use gradient-direction information (Ballard's method) to restrict $\theta$ to a narrow band around the edge normal. ### 2.6 Generalized Hough Transform (GHT) ŌĆö Ballard (1981) When the target shape has **no analytical equation**, the Generalized Hough Transform uses an *R-table* (Reference table): 1. Choose a reference point $(x_r, y_r)$ inside the shape template. 2. For each boundary point $(x_i, y_i)$ of the template, compute: - The vector from reference to boundary: $\mathbf{r}_i = (x_i - x_r, y_i - y_r)$. - The gradient angle $\phi_i$ of the boundary at $(x_i, y_i)$. 3. Build the R-table indexed by $\phi$: $R(\phi) = \{ (r_1, \alpha_1), (r_2, \alpha_2), \ldots \}$ where $r_i = \vert \mathbf{r}_i \vert$ and $\alpha_i = \arctan(y_i - y_r, x_i - x_r)$. 4. For each edge pixel in the input image with gradient angle $\phi$: - Look up all entries in $R(\phi)$. - Vote for the corresponding reference point in the accumulator. 5. Peaks in the accumulator correspond to detected instances of the shape. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Lane Detection** in autonomous vehicles and ADAS. - **Railway Track Monitoring** in intelligent transportation systems. - **Medical Imaging:** Detection of circular cells, ribs, and anatomical landmarks in X-rays and CT scans. - **Industrial Quality Control:** PCB track inspection, bottle-cap circularity checks. - **Document Analysis:** Table-line and underline detection in OCR pipelines. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Polar Form of a Line from Normal Vector **Given:** A line $\ell$ in the 2-D plane and a unit normal vector $\hat{n} = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ making angle $\theta$ with the positive x-axis. **Let** $\rho$ be the signed perpendicular distance from the origin to the line. **Step 1 ŌĆö Position Vector of a Generic Point on the Line:** Any point $P = (x, y)$ on the line satisfies: $$\vec{OP} = \rho \hat{n} + s \hat{t}$$ where $s$ is a scalar along the line's tangent direction $\hat{t}$. **Step 2 ŌĆö Dot Product with the Normal:** Taking the dot product of both sides with $\hat{n}$: $$\vec{OP} \cdot \hat{n} = \rho (\hat{n} \cdot \hat{n}) + s (\hat{t} \cdot \hat{n})$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Simplify Using Orthogonality:** Since $\hat{n} \cdot \hat{n} = 1$ and $\hat{t} \cdot \hat{n} = 0$ (tangent is perpendicular to normal): $$\vec{OP} \cdot \hat{n} = \rho$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Expand the Dot Product:** $$(x, y) \cdot (\cos\theta, \sin\theta) = \rho$$ $$x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta = \rho$$ > This is the **polar (normal) parameterization of a line**, with the unique property that $\rho \geq 0$ for $\theta \in [0, \pi)$. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: A Single Point Maps to a Sinusoid in $(\rho, \theta)$ Space **Given:** A fixed image-space point $P_0 = (x_0, y_0)$. **Step 1 ŌĆö Apply the Polar Equation:** For every possible line passing through $P_0$, there exists a $(\rho, \theta)$ pair satisfying: $$\rho = x_0 \cos\theta + y_0 \sin\theta$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Rewrite Using a Trigonometric Identity:** Using $a\cos\theta + b\sin\theta = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2} \cos(\theta - \phi)$ with $\phi = \arctan(b/a)$: $$\rho = \sqrt{x_0^2 + y_0^2} \cos(\theta - \phi)$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Identify the Curve:** This is a **sinusoid** with: - **Amplitude:** $R = \sqrt{x_0^2 + y_0^2}$ (distance from origin to $P_0$). - **Phase shift:** $\phi = \arctan(y_0 / x_0)$ (angle to $P_0$). > **Interpretation:** Every image-space point is represented as one sinusoid in Hough space. The amplitude equals the radial distance of the point from the origin, and the phase equals the polar angle of the point. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Two Collinear Points Intersect at the Line's Parameters **Given:** Two image-space points $P_1 = (x_1, y_1)$ and $P_2 = (x_2, y_2)$ lying on the same line $\ell^*$. **Step 1 ŌĆö Write the Sinusoid for $P_1$:** $$\rho = x_1 \cos\theta + y_1 \sin\theta$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Write the Sinusoid for $P_2$:** $$\rho = x_2 \cos\theta + y_2 \sin\theta$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Equate for the True Line:** At the true parameters $(\rho^*, \theta^*)$ of the line $\ell^*$, both equations must hold: $$x_1 \cos\theta^* + y_1 \sin\theta^* = \rho^*$$ $$x_2 \cos\theta^* + y_2 \sin\theta^* = \rho^*$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Solve for $\theta^*$ (if needed):** Subtracting the two equations: $$(x_1 - x_2) \cos\theta^* + (y_1 - y_2) \sin\theta^* = 0$$ $$\tan\theta^* = -\dfrac{x_1 - x_2}{y_1 - y_2}$$ > **Conclusion:** Two collinear image points map to two sinusoids that intersect at **exactly one** point in $(\rho, \theta)$ space ŌĆö the line's parameters. By extension, $N$ collinear points all intersect at the same $(\rho^*, \theta^*)$, producing a tall peak in the accumulator. --- ### 3.4 Derivation: Gradient-Direction Optimization for Circle HT (Ballard's Trick) **Given:** Edge point $P = (x, y)$ with gradient direction $\psi = \arctan(\partial I / \partial y, \partial I / \partial x)$. **Key Observation:** The center of any circle passing through $P$ must lie along the inward-pointing gradient direction (perpendicular to the edge tangent). **Step 1 ŌĆö Express the Center:** $$a = x - r \cos\psi, \quad b = y - r \sin\psi$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Standard Hough Circle Cost:** For each $(x, y)$ and each $\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$, evaluate all $(a, b)$: $$\text{Cost}_{\text{standard}} = O(N_{\text{edge}} \times N_r \times N_\theta)$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Gradient-Constrained Cost:** Restrict $\theta = \psi \pm \Delta$ with a small tolerance $\Delta$ (typically $\pm 5^\circ$): $$\text{Cost}_{\text{optimized}} = O(N_{\text{edge}} \times N_r \times 1)$$ > **Speedup Factor:** $N_\theta / 1$, often a 100├ŚŌĆō1000├Ś improvement. --- ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** Given three edge points $P_1 = (2, 2)$, $P_2 = (3, 4)$, $P_3 = (4, 6)$, verify that they are collinear and determine their Hough parameters. **Step 1 ŌĆö Check Collinearity (slope method):** $$m_{12} = \dfrac{4 - 2}{3 - 2} = 2$$ $$m_{23} = \dfrac{6 - 4}{4 - 3} = 2$$ Slopes are equal $\Rightarrow$ collinear. **Step 2 ŌĆö Line Equation:** Using point-slope form with $(2, 2)$ and slope $2$: $$y - 2 = 2(x - 2) \Rightarrow y = 2x - 2$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Convert to Polar Form:** $y = 2x - 2$ can be rewritten as $2x - y = 2$. Compare with $x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta = \rho$: $$\cos\theta = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{5}}, \quad \sin\theta = -\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}}, \quad \rho = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{5}}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Solve for $\theta$ and $\rho$:** $$\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{-1/\sqrt{5}}{2/\sqrt{5}}\right) = \arctan(-0.5) \approx -26.57^\circ$$ $$\rho = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{5}} \approx 0.894 \text{ (in units of } \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}\text{)}$$ > **Final HT Parameters:** $\theta \approx -26.57^\circ$ (or equivalently $153.43^\circ$ in $[0, \pi)$), $\rho \approx 0.894$. All three points cast a vote into $A(0.894, 153.43^\circ)$, producing a peak of value **3** in the accumulator. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Hough Transform Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Image I x y] --> B[Grayscale Conversion] B --> C[Noise Smoothing Gaussian Filter] C --> D[Edge Detection Canny or Sobel] D --> E[Binary Edge Map E x y] E --> F[Initialize Accumulator A rho theta to 0] F --> G[Discretize theta in 0 to pi] G --> H[For Each Edge Pixel x_i y_i] H --> I[For Each theta_l] I --> J[Compute rho = x_i cos theta_l + y_i sin theta_l] J --> K[Quantize rho to nearest bin] K --> L[Increment A rho_k theta_l] L --> M{More theta values} M --> I M --> N{More edge pixels} N --> H N --> O[Threshold Accumulator] O --> P[Non Maximum Suppression] P --> Q[Detect Peaks in A rho theta] Q --> R[Map Back to Image Space] R --> S[Output Detected Lines] ``` ### 4.2 Conceptual Mapping Between Image Space and Hough Space ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph IS[IMAGE SPACE] P1[Point P1 x1 y1] P2[Point P2 x2 y2] P3[Point P3 x3 y3] end subgraph HS[HOUGH SPACE rho theta] S1[Sinusoid 1] S2[Sinusoid 2] S3[Sinusoid 3] X[Intersection Point rho star theta star] end P1 --> S1 P2 --> S2 P3 --> S3 S1 --> X S2 --> X S3 --> X ``` ### 4.3 Comparative Decision Flow: Line vs Circle vs Generalized HT ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Select Hough Variant] --> Q1{Is the target shape analytical with 2 parameters} Q1 -->|Yes Line| LINE[Line Hough Transform
2D Accumulator rho theta
Cost O N times N theta] Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is the target shape analytical with 3 parameters} Q2 -->|Yes Circle or Ellipse| CIRC[Circle Hough Transform
3D Accumulator a b r
Cost O N times N theta times N r] Q2 -->|No Arbitrary shape| GEN[Generalized Hough Transform
R Table Lookup
2D Accumulator x ref y ref] LINE --> OUT1[Detect Lines] CIRC --> OUT2[Detect Circles or Ellipses] GEN --> OUT3[Detect Arbitrary Shapes] ``` ### 4.4 Generalized Hough Transform R-Table Construction Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD T1[Input Shape Template] --> T2[Select Reference Point x_r y_r] T2 --> T3[For Each Boundary Point x_i y_i] T3 --> T4[Compute Gradient Angle phi_i] T4 --> T5[Compute Vector r_i from ref to boundary] T5 --> T6[Compute Magnitude r_i and Angle alpha_i] T6 --> T7[Store Entry in R Table indexed by phi_i] T7 --> T8{More boundary points} T8 --> T3 T8 --> T9[R Table Complete] Q1[Query Image Edge Pixel] --> Q2[Compute Gradient Angle phi] Q2 --> Q3[Lookup R phi] Q3 --> Q4[For Each Entry r alpha] Q4 --> Q5[Compute Candidate Ref Point x_r = x - r cos alpha
y_r = y - r sin alpha] Q5 --> Q6[Increment Accumulator A x_r y_r] Q6 --> Q7{More entries} Q7 --> Q4 Q7 --> Q8[Peak in Accumulator = Detected Shape Location] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question, Module 3]** **Q1. Define Hough Transform. List any two advantages of the polar (ŽüŌĆō╬Ė) parameterization over the slopeŌĆōintercept form.** `[3 Marks]` **Model Answer:** The Hough Transform is a feature extraction technique used to detect parametric shapes in an image by mapping edge points from the Cartesian image space $(x, y)$ into a parameter space $(\rho, \theta)$ and identifying peaks in an accumulator array. **Advantages of polar form:** 1. It handles vertical lines naturally (slope form has $m \to \infty$). 2. Both parameters $\rho$ and $\theta$ are bounded ($\rho \in [-\rho_{\max}, \rho_{\max}]$, $\theta \in [0, \pi]$), making the accumulator a fixed finite grid. --- **Q2. What is an accumulator array in the context of the Hough Transform? Why is peak detection performed on it?** `[3 Marks]` **Model Answer:** An **accumulator array** $A(\rho, \theta)$ is a discretized 2-D histogram in the parameter space, where each cell $A(\rho_k, \theta_l)$ stores the number of edge points that support the hypothesis that a line with parameters $(\rho_k, \theta_l)$ exists in the image. **Peak detection** is performed because the cell with the maximum count corresponds to the set of parameters that are *most consistently* supported by the edge points ŌĆö i.e., the most likely line in the image. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice Pattern) > **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question, Module 3, 14-Mark Module Choice Pattern]** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain the Hough Transform algorithm for line detection with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the accumulator array and the quantization of parameter space. `[7 Marks]` **Model Answer:** The Hough Transform for line detection follows these operational steps: 1. **Edge Map Generation:** The input image is preprocessed using an edge detector (typically Canny) to produce a binary edge map $E(x, y)$. 2. **Parameter Space Discretization:** The continuous $(\rho, \theta)$ space is discretized into a finite grid: - $\theta_l = l \cdot \Delta\theta$ for $l = 0, 1, \ldots, N_\theta - 1$, where $\Delta\theta = \pi / N_\theta$. - $\rho$ range $[-\rho_{\max}, \rho_{\max}]$ is divided into $N_\rho$ bins. 3. **Accumulator Initialization:** A 2-D array $A(\rho_k, \theta_l)$ of size $N_\rho \times N_\theta$ is created and initialized to zero. 4. **Voting Phase:** For each edge point $(x_i, y_i)$ and each $\theta_l$: - Compute $\rho = x_i \cos\theta_l + y_i \sin\theta_l$. - Find the nearest discrete bin $\rho_k$. - Increment $A(\rho_k, \theta_l) \leftarrow A(\rho_k, \theta_l) + 1$. 5. **Peak Detection:** Local maxima in $A$ exceeding a threshold $T$ are identified. Each peak $(\rho^*, \theta^*)$ defines a detected line. **Role of the Accumulator Array:** The accumulator acts as a *voting register*. Each edge point casts votes for *all* lines that could pass through it. Collinear points reinforce the same cell, producing tall peaks. Noise points distribute votes sparsely. **Quantization Significance:** Coarse quantization gives faster but less accurate detection; fine quantization gives higher accuracy at the cost of memory and time. The optimal choice balances computational budget with detection precision. **(b)** Derive the polar form of a line equation from first principles. Show that a single image-space point $(x_0, y_0)$ maps to a sinusoid in the $(\rho, \theta)$ space. `[7 Marks]` **Model Answer:** **Derivation of Polar Form:** Consider a line $\ell$ with unit normal $\hat{n} = (\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ at perpendicular distance $\rho$ from the origin. Any point $P = (x, y)$ on $\ell$ satisfies $\vec{OP} \cdot \hat{n} = \rho$. Expanding: $$x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta = \rho \quad \text{...(1)}$$ **Sinusoidal Mapping for a Single Point:** For a fixed $(x_0, y_0)$, as $\theta$ varies over $[0, \pi]$, equation (1) becomes: $$\rho(\theta) = x_0 \cos\theta + y_0 \sin\theta$$ Using the identity $a \cos\theta + b \sin\theta = R \cos(\theta - \phi)$ with $R = \sqrt{x_0^2 + y_0^2}$ and $\phi = \arctan(y_0, x_0)$: $$\rho(\theta) = \sqrt{x_0^2 + y_0^2} \cos(\theta - \phi)$$ This is a **cosinusoidal curve** of amplitude $\sqrt{x_0^2 + y_0^2}$ and phase $\phi$. Hence, every image-space point corresponds to one such sinusoid in the Hough space, and the intersection of sinusoids from collinear points locates the true line parameters. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain the Hough Transform for circle detection. Discuss its computational complexity and the Ballard's gradient-direction optimization. `[7 Marks]` **Model Answer:** A circle is defined by three parameters: center $(a, b)$ and radius $r$, with the equation: $$(x - a)^2 + (y - b)^2 = r^2$$ **Algorithm for a Fixed Radius $r$:** 1. Detect edges to obtain $(x_i, y_i)$. 2. For each edge point and each $\theta \in [0, 2\pi)$: - Compute $a = x_i - r \cos\theta$, $b = y_i - r \sin\theta$. - Increment $A(a, b)$. 3. Peaks in $A$ correspond to circle centers. **Algorithm for Variable Radius:** A 3-D accumulator $A(a, b, r)$ is used, iterating over $r \in [r_{\min}, r_{\max}]$. **Computational Complexity:** $O(N_{\text{edge}} \times N_r \times N_\theta)$ ŌĆö typically three orders of magnitude slower than line HT. **Ballard's Gradient Optimization:** For an edge point, the gradient angle $\psi$ approximates the direction of the radial vector to the circle's center. By restricting $\theta = \psi$ (instead of iterating all $\theta$), the cost reduces to $O(N_{\text{edge}} \times N_r)$, achieving a speedup of $N_\theta$ (often 100├ŚŌĆō1000├Ś). **(b)** Explain the Generalized Hough Transform (GHT) with the construction of the R-table. State two situations where GHT is preferred over standard HT. `[7 Marks]` **Model Answer:** The **Generalized Hough Transform (Ballard, 1981)** detects arbitrary, non-analytical shapes. **R-Table Construction:** 1. Choose a reference point $(x_r, y_r)$ inside the template shape. 2. For each boundary point $(x_i, y_i)$: - Compute gradient angle $\phi_i$ at $(x_i, y_i)$. - Compute the displacement vector $\mathbf{r}_i = (x_i - x_r, y_i - y_r)$. - Compute magnitude $r_i = \vert \mathbf{r}_i \vert$ and angle $\alpha_i = \arctan(y_i - y_r, x_i - x_r)$. 3. Store $(r_i, \alpha_i)$ pairs in $R(\phi_i)$ indexed by gradient angle. **Detection Phase:** For each edge pixel in the query image with gradient $\phi$: - Look up all $(r, \alpha)$ entries in $R(\phi)$. - Compute candidate reference point: $x_r = x - r\cos\alpha$, $y_r = y - r\sin\alpha$. - Vote in $A(x_r, y_r)$. Peaks in $A$ correspond to detected instances of the shape. **Two Situations Where GHT is Preferred:** 1. **Arbitrary non-analytical shapes** (e.g., industrial parts, logos) that have no simple parametric equation. 2. **Scale or rotation invariance requirements**, where multiple R-tables can be pre-computed for different orientations and scales. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Always mention preprocessing** (Canny/Sobel edge map). Answers beginning directly with the voting phase will lose 1ŌĆō2 marks. > 2. **State the bounds of parameters explicitly:** $\theta \in [0, \pi]$ and $\rho \in [-\rho_{\max}, \rho_{\max}]$. Missing bounds indicates conceptual looseness. > 3. **For circle HT, do not forget gradient optimization.** Examiners expect the Ballard speedup discussion for full marks on circle-based questions. > 4. **In numerical problems, retain units** (radians vs degrees) and explicitly state the discretization resolution used. > 5. **R-table questions:** Students often confuse the roles of $\phi$ (index) and $\alpha$ (stored angle). Memorize: *$\phi$ indexes the table; $\alpha$ is stored inside.* --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Hough Transform is a voting-based shape detection technique** that operates on a binary edge map. - **Polar form** $x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta = \rho$ is preferred over slope-intercept because it handles vertical lines uniformly. - **A single image point maps to a sinusoid** in $(\rho, \theta)$ space; collinear points cause sinusoids to **intersect at a common point** that corresponds to the line's parameters. - **Accumulator array** $A(\rho, \theta)$ records votes; the cell with the **maximum count** is the detected line. - **Parameter discretization** directly trades off **accuracy** vs **computational cost**; finer grids yield better accuracy at higher memory/time cost. - **Circle HT** has computational cost $O(N_{\text{edge}} \times N_r \times N_\theta)$ and benefits from the **Ballard gradient optimization** to reduce to $O(N_{\text{edge}} \times N_r)$. - **Generalized Hough Transform (GHT)** uses an **R-table** indexed by gradient angle to detect arbitrary, non-analytical shapes; it is the foundation of modern shape-detection pipelines. - **Computational Footprint Summary:** Line HT $\ll$ Circle HT $\ll$ GHT (in general, before optimization). - **Practical Tips for KTU:** Always draw a block diagram, mention edge preprocessing, specify parameter bounds, and conclude with a brief note on advantages/limitations. - **Limitations to Remember:** Sensitivity to noise (mitigated by edge thresholding), inability to detect line endpoints (returns infinite lines unless segmented post-processing), and high memory cost for high-resolution 3-D accumulators. - **Bloom Level Targeted:** Apply (algorithm tracing) and Analyze (derivation, comparison) ŌĆö prepare accordingly.

topics.title: Border Detection Using Border Location Information
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST636 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Border Detection Using Border Location Information # ­¤ōś SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Border (Edge) Detection Using Border Location Information** is a refined image segmentation technique in which the *approximate location* of an edge is first estimated using a gradient or second-derivative operator, and then the **precise sub-pixel position** of the edge is recovered by exploiting the **direction of the gradient vector** and the **shape of the intensity profile** across the edge. In formal mathematical terms, given a digital image $f(x,y)$, a gradient-based edge detector computes: $$g(x,y) = \vert \nabla f(x,y) \vert = \sqrt{g_x^2(x,y) + g_y^2(x,y)}$$ The direction of the gradient (the **edge-normal direction**) is given by: $$\alpha(x,y) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{g_y(x,y)}{g_x(x,y)}\right)$$ The "border location information" refers to using this $\alpha(x,y)$ to *interpolate* across neighbouring pixels along the normal direction, so that the edge is no longer tied to the integer pixel grid but can be located with **fractional (sub-pixel) accuracy**. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PECST636 Module 3):** > The intent is *not* just to compute "where pixels look different" but to *geometrically localise* the edge by interpreting the gradient direction as a vector pointing **perpendicular to the edge** and riding along the intensity ramp to its inflection point. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing on top of a long, straight sand-dune at night. The dune is the "edge" between a bright plateau and a dark valley. - **Gradient magnitude** = how *steep* the dune is. A very steep dune is a strong edge; a gentle slope is a weak edge. - **Gradient direction** = the compass direction of the **steepest climb up the dune's slope**. This is the direction *perpendicular* to the dune ridge. - **Border location** = if a friend asks "where exactly is the top of the dune?", you don't give them a pixel ŌĆö you walk along the steepest-slope direction and tell them *"the crest lies about 2.4 metres uphill in that exact compass direction."* A pixel-level edge detector would only say "somewhere around this region." A **border-location-aware detector** uses the gradient's direction and magnitude profile to pinpoint the edge with sub-pixel precision, much like a hiker tracing the contour of a hill by following its steepest path. --- ## 1.3 Physical & Algorithmic Constants | Quantity | Standard Value / Range | Meaning | |----------|----------------------|---------| | **Gradient Threshold $T$** | Typically $T \approx 20$ŌĆō$60$ on a 0ŌĆō255 scale | Minimum $g(x,y)$ to qualify as an edge | | **Sub-pixel Resolution** | $\Delta x, \Delta y \in [0, 1]$ pixel | Fractional offset recovered from interpolation | | **Edge-Normal Angle $\alpha$** | $0$ to $2\pi$ radians (or $0┬░$ to $360┬░$) | Direction in which intensity changes most rapidly | | **Inflection Point (2nd-Derivative Method)** | Zero-crossing of $\partial^2 f / \partial n^2 = 0$ | Sub-pixel edge in the LoG/Canny framework | > [!TIP] > The **edge normal** is the line passing *through* a candidate edge pixel **in the direction $\alpha(x,y)$**. Walking along this line, you cross from the dark side to the bright side of the edge ŌĆö that is exactly where the **true border** sits. --- ## 1.4 Why Not Just Threshold the Gradient? A naive operator marks every pixel whose gradient magnitude exceeds a threshold as an "edge." This gives a **thick, ragged band of pixels** straddling the true boundary. The border-location-information technique solves three problems: 1. **Thick edges** ŌåÆ The true edge is *inside* the band, not on every pixel of the band. 2. **Pixel-locked coordinates** ŌåÆ Real edges in natural images are continuous curves, not integer grids. 3. **Orientation bias** ŌåÆ A step edge running diagonally to the pixel grid samples fewer "dark-bright" transitions than an axis-aligned edge, so a pure magnitude threshold mis-reports the location. The remedy: travel along $\alpha(x,y)$ and read the **intensity profile** as a 1-D signal whose mid-point (or inflection point) *is* the edge. # ­¤ōś SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two-Stage Paradigm of Border Location Border detection using border-location information follows a strict two-stage pipeline: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stage 1 ŌĆö Localisation Hint (Coarse):** Compute $g(x,y)$ and $\alpha(x,y)$ to find candidate edge pixels. > **Stage 2 ŌĆö Sub-pixel Refinement (Fine):** Use the profile of $f$ along the line defined by $\alpha(x,y)$ to recover the edge's true position between pixels. ### Stage 1 ŌĆö Gradient Computation For a pixel at $(x,y)$ in the discrete image, gradient components are approximated by convolution: $$g_x = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x} \;\approx\; f(x+1, y) - f(x-1, y)$$ $$g_y = \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} \;\approx\; f(x, y+1) - f(x, y-1)$$ Sobel and Prewitt operators apply small pre-smoothing weights before differencing (e.g., $3\times 3$ kernels). This makes them less noise-sensitive than the raw central-difference mask. ### Stage 2 ŌĆö Sub-pixel Interpolation Along the Normal Once a candidate edge pixel is found, the algorithm: 1. Constructs the unit normal vector $$\hat{n} = (\cos\alpha,\ \sin\alpha)$$ 2. Samples the 1-D intensity profile along $\hat{n}$ centred on $(x,y)$: $$p(t) = f\bigl(x + t\cos\alpha,\ y + t\sin\alpha\bigr)$$ 3. Locates the point where $p(t)$ is **steepest** (max $\vert p'(t) \vert$) ŌĆö the **edge's true location** in the continuous domain. In practice, four samples (or higher-order interpolation) are taken at $t = -3/2, -1/2, +1/2, +3/2$ to obtain a sub-pixel offset. ## 2.2 Why the Edge Lies at the Inflection Point Consider a 1-D intensity profile $f(x)$ representing a step edge blurred by the imaging system's point-spread function. Its shape is approximately an **error function**: $$f(x) \approx \frac{1}{2}\!\left[1 + \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{x - x_0}{\sigma\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$$ The first derivative peaks at $x = x_0$ ŌĆö this is the **maximum gradient point**, which is the inflection point of the original step. The second derivative changes sign (zero-crossing) at $x = x_0$. **Both** coincide with the true edge. Therefore: $$\boxed{\;x_{\text{edge}} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \max\,\bigl\vert f'(x) \bigr\vert \;\Longleftrightarrow\; f''(x) = 0\;}$$ This is the geometric foundation of **Canny's edge detector** and the MarrŌĆōHildreth (LoG) zero-crossing method. ## 2.3 Edge-Normal Tracking (Linking) Once sub-pixel edge points are obtained, they are linked into continuous contours by **edge-normal tracking**: - From a confirmed edge pixel, walk one step in the direction $\alpha$. - The next pixel in that direction is the candidate neighbour. - If its gradient is above threshold and its orientation matches (within tolerance), accept it. - Repeat until no compatible neighbour exists. > [!NOTE] > This is the principle behind the **Hough transform's** accumulator-based linking and behind **contour-tracing** algorithms (Suzuki's border following, Pavlidis's algorithm, etc.). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Typical Use | |---|---------|-------------|-------------| | 1 | $g(x,y) = \sqrt{g_x^2 + g_y^2}$ | Gradient magnitude | Edge strength | | 2 | $M(x,y) \approx \vert g_x \vert + \vert g_y \vert$ | Approximate magnitude (cheaper) | Real-time systems | | 3 | $\alpha(x,y) = \arctan(g_y / g_x)$ | Gradient direction = edge-normal | Localisation vector | | 4 | $\hat{n} = (\cos\alpha,\ \sin\alpha)$ | Unit normal to edge | Sub-pixel interpolation | | 5 | $p(t) = f(x + t\cos\alpha,\ y + t\sin\alpha)$ | Intensity profile along normal | 1-D edge analysis | | 6 | $x_{\text{edge}} = \arg\max_t\,\bigl\vert p'(t) \bigr\vert$ | True edge location | Sub-pixel refinement | | 7 | $\nabla^2 f = \partial^2 f / \partial x^2 + \partial^2 f / \partial y^2$ | Laplacian (LoG kernel) | Zero-crossing edges | | 8 | $G_\sigma(x,y) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2+y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | Gaussian smoothing kernel | Pre-smoothing for LoG/Canny | | 9 | $g_{xx} + g_{yy} = 0$ | Inflection condition | Sub-pixel edge in 2nd-deriv method | | 10 | $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$ | Hough parameterization | Edge linking / line detection | > All thresholds and constants are **empirical** ŌĆö the **Canny recommendation** is $T_{\text{high}} / T_{\text{low}} \in [2, 3]$. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | Use of Border-Location Information | |--------------------|-------------------------------------| | **Medical Imaging (CT/MRI)** | Tumour boundary tracing with sub-millimetre precision | | **Autonomous Vehicles** | Lane detection with sub-pixel accuracy in road imagery | | **PCB / Semiconductor Inspection** | Detecting micron-scale defects along chip edges | | **Satellite Remote Sensing** | Coastline and crop-field boundary extraction | | **Industrial Metrology** | Caliper-style measurement of object dimensions from camera images | | **OCR & Document Analysis** | Locating character strokes between pixel rows | # ­¤ōś SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Sub-Pixel Edge Position from a Sampled Gradient Profile Suppose we have a candidate edge pixel $P$ at $(x_0, y_0)$. We sample the 1-D intensity profile $p(t)$ along the unit normal $\hat{n}$ at three fractional offsets $t = -1, 0, +1$. The intensity values are: $$p_{-1} = f(x_0 - \hat{n}_x,\, y_0 - \hat{n}_y)$$ $$p_{0} = f(x_0,\, y_0)$$ $$p_{+1} = f(x_0 + \hat{n}_x,\, y_0 + \hat{n}_y)$$ We fit a **quadratic model** to the gradient (not the intensity). The first derivative of the intensity, sampled at $t = -1, 0, +1$, is: $$p'_{-1} \;\approx\; \frac{p_0 - p_{-1}}{1}$$ $$p'_{0} \;\approx\; \frac{p_{+1} - p_{-1}}{2}$$ $$p'_{+1} \;\approx\; \frac{p_{+1} - p_0}{1}$$ Assume the gradient profile itself is a smooth function $G(t) = p'(t)$, and near the edge it is **unimodal**. We model it as a parabola: $$G(t) = A\,t^2 + B\,t + C$$ The three unknown coefficients are solved by substitution. Substituting $t = -1, 0, +1$: $$ \begin{aligned} G(-1) &= A - B + C \\ G(0) &= C \\ G(+1) &= A + B + C \end{aligned} $$ Solving this linear system: $$ \begin{aligned} A &= \frac{G(+1) + G(-1)}{2} - G(0) \\ B &= \frac{G(+1) - G(-1)}{2} \\ C &= G(0) \end{aligned} $$ The vertex of the parabola ŌĆö the **maximum gradient point**, which is the true sub-pixel edge location ŌĆö is at: $$t_{\text{edge}} = -\frac{B}{2A} = -\frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{G(+1) - G(-1)}{G(+1) + G(-1) - 2G(0)}$$ In discrete notation: $$\boxed{\;t_{\text{edge}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{G(-1) - G(+1)}{G(+1) + G(-1) - 2\,G(0)}\;}$$ > **Explanation of logic:** The parabola's peak is where the linear term $Bt$ balances the quadratic term $At^2$. This gives a closed-form sub-pixel offset between $-0.5$ and $+0.5$ pixels. The 2-D edge coordinate is then: $$(x_{\text{edge}},\, y_{\text{edge}}) \;=\; (x_0 + t_{\text{edge}}\cos\alpha,\; y_0 + t_{\text{edge}}\sin\alpha)$$ > [!TIP] > This is the same mathematical trick used inside OpenCV's `getSubPix()` and in Canny's non-maximum suppression refinement. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Relation Between Gradient Direction and Edge Orientation A step edge is, locally, a 1-D intensity transition. Let the edge make an angle $\theta$ with the $x$-axis. Parametrise a line perpendicular to the edge (i.e., the gradient direction) as: $$(x(s),\, y(s)) = (s\cos\theta + d,\; s\sin\theta + d)$$ where $d$ is a constant and $s$ is the running coordinate. The intensity along this normal is $f(s\cos\theta + d,\ s\sin\theta + d) = p(s)$. The total derivative along $s$ is: $$\frac{dp}{ds} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\cos\theta + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\sin\theta$$ This must be **maximal** for $\theta$ to be the edge-normal direction. Taking $\partial(dp/ds) / \partial \theta = 0$: $$ \begin{aligned} 0 &= -\frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\sin\theta + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\cos\theta \\ \Rightarrow \quad \tan\theta &= \frac{\partial f / \partial y}{\partial f / \partial x} = \frac{g_y}{g_x} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\;\alpha(x,y) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{g_y}{g_x}\right) \;=\; \theta_{\text{normal}}\;}$$ > **Conclusion:** The angle returned by the $\arctan(g_y/g_x)$ formula is precisely the **angle of the normal** to the edge, and the edge itself is rotated by $\pi/2$ from $\alpha$. This is the "border location information" the topic is named after. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** Given a $3\times 3$ image patch with intensity values: $$ \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 10 & 10 \\ 10 & 50 & 80 \\ 10 & 80 & 100 \end{bmatrix} $$ Find the gradient magnitude, gradient direction, and the sub-pixel edge location at the centre pixel $(1,1)$ using the quadratic-parabola method. **Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $g_x$ and $g_y$ via Sobel-like central differences:** $$ \begin{aligned} g_x &= f(2,1) - f(0,1) = 80 - 10 = 70 \\ g_y &= f(1,2) - f(1,0) = 80 - 10 = 70 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Magnitude and direction:** $$ \begin{aligned} g(1,1) &= \sqrt{70^2 + 70^2} = 70\sqrt{2} \approx 98.99 \\ \alpha(1,1) &= \arctan(70/70) = \arctan(1) = \frac{\pi}{4} = 45^\circ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Unit normal vector:** $$\hat{n} = \bigl(\cos 45^\circ,\ \sin 45^\circ\bigr) = \left(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt 2},\ \tfrac{1}{\sqrt 2}\right)$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Sample intensity along $\hat{n}$ at $t = -1, 0, +1$:** For a $3\times 3$ image with origin at $(0,0)$ top-left, centre is $(1,1)$. The samples fall at: - $t = -1$: $(1 - 1/\sqrt 2,\ 1 - 1/\sqrt 2) \approx (0.29,\ 0.29)$ ŌĆö bilinearly interpolated between $(0,0)=10$, $(1,0)=10$, $(0,1)=10$, $(1,1)=50$. Result $\approx 10 \cdot (1-0.29)^2 + 50 \cdot 0.71^2 + 40 \cdot (\text{cross terms}) \approx 27.05$. - $t = 0$: $(1, 1) = 50$. - $t = +1$: $(1 + 1/\sqrt 2,\ 1 + 1/\sqrt 2) \approx (1.71,\ 1.71)$ ŌĆö bilinearly interpolated between $(1,1)=50$, $(2,1)=80$, $(1,2)=80$, $(2,2)=100$. Result $\approx 90.05$. **Step 5 ŌĆö Sample the gradient magnitude profile at $t = -1, 0, +1$:** Repeating the gradient computation at the three interpolated points: $$ \begin{aligned} G(-1) &\approx 60.0 \\ G(0) &= 70\sqrt{2} \approx 98.99 \\ G(+1) &\approx 80.0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Apply the sub-pixel formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} t_{\text{edge}} &= \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{G(-1) - G(+1)}{G(+1) + G(-1) - 2\,G(0)} \\ &= \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{60.0 - 80.0}{80.0 + 60.0 - 2(98.99)} \\ &= \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{-20.0}{140.0 - 197.98} \\ &= \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{-20.0}{-57.98} \\ &= \frac{1}{2}\cdot 0.3449 \\ &\approx 0.1725 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 ŌĆö Sub-pixel edge coordinate:** $$ \begin{aligned} x_{\text{edge}} &= 1 + 0.1725 \cdot \cos 45^\circ \approx 1 + 0.122 \approx 1.122 \\ y_{\text{edge}} &= 1 + 0.1725 \cdot \sin 45^\circ \approx 1 + 0.122 \approx 1.122 \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** The true edge lies **about 0.17 pixel** to the "bright" side of the centre pixel ŌĆö i.e., the discrete pixel coordinate is *not* the true edge. The border-location-information method has pushed the edge to a more accurate continuous-domain position. --- ## 3.4 Analytic Comparison of Methods | Method | Computes | Edge Location | Sub-Pixel? | Noise Robustness | |--------|----------|---------------|------------|------------------| | Roberts Cross | $\sqrt{(f_{i,j}-f_{i+1,j+1})^2 + (f_{i+1,j}-f_{i,j+1})^2}$ | Pixel | No | Low | | Prewitt | $\max(\lvert g_x \rvert, \lvert g_y \rvert)$ or sum | Pixel | No | Medium | | Sobel | $g_x^2 + g_y^2$ (or $\lvert g_x \rvert + \lvert g_y \rvert$) | Pixel | No | Medium-High | | **Laplacian of Gaussian (LoG)** | $\nabla^2 G_\sigma * f$ | **Zero-crossing between pixels** | **Yes (Ōēł 0.5 px)** | High (if $\sigma$ large) | | **Canny** | NMS + hysteresis | **Sub-pixel via parabolic fit** | **Yes (Ōēł 0.1ŌĆō0.2 px)** | Very High | | **Hueckel / NevatiaŌĆōBabu** | Parametric edge model | **Sub-pixel** | **Yes (Ōēł 0.1 px)** | High | # ­¤ōś SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Image f of x,y] --> B[Noise Smoothing

The continuous edge lies *between* pixel 2 and pixel 3, but the discrete integer grid cannot represent 2.34. The gradient-profile parabolic fit recovers this **0.34-pixel offset** as the "border location information." ## 4.3 Edge-Normal Geometry Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph scene[Image Plane] edge[/True Edge

The gradient direction $\alpha$ is perpendicular to it. Walking along $\alpha$ from a confirmed edge pixel identifies the next two candidate neighbours ŌĆö one brighter, one darker ŌĆö to be tested for the linkage criterion. ## 4.4 Conceptual Comparison: Pixel-Level vs. Sub-Pixel Edge ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph pix[Pixel Level Edge] A1[Pixel marked edge] A2[Pixel marked edge] A3[Pixel marked edge] A4[Pixel marked edge] end subgraph sub[Sub-Pixel Edge] B1((Continuous Point
