## ­¤ōŗ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### Watershed Segmentation ŌĆö Key Points
- Ō£ģ Watershed is a **morphological, region-based segmentation** technique.
- Ō£ģ Input is the **gradient magnitude image** $G = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}$.
- Ō£ģ Algorithm: **Meyer flooding** ŌĆö punch holes at minima, immerse terrain, build dams at meeting points.
- Ō£ģ Output: **Catchment basins** (regions) + **watershed lines** (boundaries).
- Ō£ģ Major drawback: **Oversegmentation** due to noise-induced spurious minima.
- Ō£ģ Solution: **Marker-controlled watershed** with internal + external markers.
- Ō£ģ Distance transform watershed: produces **SKIZ (Skeleton by Influence Zones)**.
- Ō£ģ Two-pass Chamfer 3-4 mask: $\Delta_1 = 3, \Delta_2 = 4$ with $< 2\%$ error.
- Ō£ģ Equation to memorize: $C[n] = C[n+1] \cup \text{FLood}(C[n+1] \cup \min[n])$.

### Image Matching ŌĆö Key Points
- Ō£ģ Template matching: **Sliding window** approach across image.
- Ō£ģ **SSD**: ideal value = **0**, sensitive to all changes.
- Ō£ģ **Cross-correlation**: ideal = maximum; sensitive to brightness/contrast.
- Ō£ģ **NCC**: range $[-1, +1]$; ideal = **+1**; illumination invariant.
- Ō£ģ NCC formula: $\gamma = \dfrac{\sum \tilde{I}\tilde{T}}{\sqrt{\sum \tilde{I}^2 \cdot \sum \tilde{T}^2}}$.
- Ō£ģ Feature matching uses **SIFT (128-D)** or **Harris** corners.
- Ō£ģ SIFT keypoints from **DoG (Difference-of-Gaussian)** scale space.
- Ō£ģ **Lowe's ratio test**: $d_1/d_2 < 0.7$ ŌåÆ accept match.
- Ō£ģ Chamfer matching uses **distance transform** for edge-based matching.
- Ō£ģ Template matching = fast, brittle | Feature matching = slow, robust.

### Memorization Mantra for Exam Hall
> *"Watershed floods gradient, builds dams at ridges; NCC normalizes both means to kill the lighting bias."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Template Matching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST636 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Template Matching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Template Matching** is a fundamental technique in digital image processing used to locate a sub-image (or pattern) of a known shape, structure, or template within a larger search image. It operates by sliding the template over every possible spatial position in the source image and computing a similarity (or dissimilarity) measure at each location. The position(s) yielding the optimal metric value (maximum similarity or minimum dissimilarity) are declared as detected matches.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, Template Matching is positioned under the broader umbrella of **object detection and image recognition** and is considered a foundational pixel-domain matching strategy that forms the stepping stone to advanced techniques like feature-based matching, Scale-Invariant Feature Transform (SIFT), and Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)-based detectors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST636 ŌĆō Module 3):** Template Matching is classified under the topic of *Object Detection and Representation*. Students must master the mathematical foundations of correlation-based and difference-based matching metrics since they are repeatedly asked in University Examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a photograph of a missing puzzle piece (the **template**), and you are trying to locate where that exact piece fits inside a scattered pile of a thousand puzzle pieces (the **search image**). The most logical strategy would be:

1. Pick up the missing piece (template).
2. Physically overlay it on every single piece in the pile.
3. At each overlay, ask yourself: *"How well does this piece match the one I'm holding?"*
4. The piece in the pile that best matches (visually) is the answer.

Template matching digitizes exactly this intuitive process. The computer slides a small image patch (the template $T$) over every possible $(x, y)$ coordinate in the larger image $I$, and at each shift it computes a *numerical score* of how well the template's pixel intensities align with the underlying image pixels. The location that produces the **best score** is the detected match.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Template ($T$)** | A small reference image (size $m \times n$) that we wish to find inside a larger image. |
| **Search Image ($I$)** | The large image (size $M \times N$) where the template is to be located. |
| **Matching Metric** | A scalar mathematical function used to quantify similarity/dissimilarity. |
| **Search Window / Sub-image ($S_{x,y}$)** | The local region of $I$ currently under the template, centered at $(x, y)$. |
| **Match Point** | The $(x, y)$ coordinate producing the best (maximum or minimum) metric value. |
| **Threshold ($\tau$)** | A decision bound used to accept or reject candidate matches. |

### 1.4 Why Template Matching Matters in Engineering

- **Industrial Quality Control:** Locating defective components on a manufacturing line by comparing regions of a product image against a *golden reference* template.
- **Medical Imaging:** Identifying tumors, lesions, or anatomical landmarks in MRI/CT scans.
- **Remote Sensing:** Detecting vehicles, ships, or buildings in satellite imagery.
- **Document Processing:** Locating logos, signatures, or specific glyphs in scanned forms.
- **Surveillance:** Recognizing human faces in CCTV footage (the original approach before deep learning).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Constant:** The computational complexity of brute-force template matching is **$\mathcal{O}(M \cdot N \cdot m \cdot n)$**, where $M \times N$ is the search image size and $m \times n$ is the template size. This quadratic blow-up motivates faster algorithms like the **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)-based correlation**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Pipeline of Template Matching

The classical template matching procedure is a six-stage pipeline:

1. **Acquire / Define the Template $T$** of dimensions $m \times n$. This may be cropped manually from a reference image or computed statistically.
2. **Choose a Matching Metric.** Common choices are:
   - Sum of Squared Differences (SSD)
   - Sum of Absolute Differences (SAD)
   - Cross-Correlation (CC)
   - Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC)
3. **Initialize a Result Map $R$** of size $(M - m + 1) \times (N - n + 1)$. Each entry $R(x, y)$ stores the metric value when the template is centered at $(x, y)$.
4. **Sliding Window Iteration.** For every valid top-left origin $(x, y)$ in the search image:
   - Extract the sub-image $S_{x,y}$ of dimensions $m \times n$.
   - Compare $T$ with $S_{x,y}$ using the chosen metric.
   - Store the result in $R(x, y)$.
5. **Locate the Optimum.** Find $\arg\max_{(x,y)} R(x, y)$ (for similarity metrics) or $\arg\min_{(x,y)} R(x, y)$ (for dissimilarity metrics).
6. **Thresholding / Post-processing.** Apply a threshold $\tau$ to retain only statistically significant matches and suppress false positives.

### 2.2 Mathematical Formulation of Matching Metrics

#### 2.2.1 Sum of Squared Differences (SSD)

The SSD measures the cumulative squared pixel-wise intensity difference between the template and the underlying sub-image.

$$\text{SSD}(x, y) = \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} \left[ I(x+i, \; y+j) \; - \; T(i, j) \right]^2$$

- **Decision Rule:** The match is at the $(x, y)$ that **minimizes** SSD.
- **Range:** $[0, \; 255^2 \cdot m \cdot n]$ for an 8-bit grayscale image.
- **Optimum Value:** $0$ (perfect match).

#### 2.2.2 Sum of Absolute Differences (SAD)

SAD replaces the square operation with absolute value, making it more robust to outlier pixels.

$$\text{SAD}(x, y) = \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} \left\vert I(x+i, \; y+j) \; - \; T(i, j) \right\vert$$

- **Decision Rule:** **Minimize** SAD.
- **Range:** $[0, \; 255 \cdot m \cdot n]$.

#### 2.2.3 Cross-Correlation (CC)

The cross-correlation flips the template vertically and horizontally (full correlation) or does not flip it (in practice, often simplified) and computes the dot-product.

$$\text{CC}(x, y) = \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} I(x+i, \; y+j) \cdot T(i, j)$$

- **Decision Rule:** **Maximize** CC.
- **Range:** $[0, \; 255^2 \cdot m \cdot n]$ for non-negative 8-bit pixels.
- **Pitfall:** Highly sensitive to global intensity changes (e.g., a brighter region falsely scores high).

#### 2.2.4 Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC)

NCC solves the illumination-sensitivity problem of CC by normalizing both the template and the local sub-image to have zero mean and unit variance.

$$\text{NCC}(x, y) = \frac{ \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} \left[ I(x+i, y+j) - \mu_{S} \right] \left[ T(i, j) - \mu_{T} \right] }{ \sqrt{ \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} \left[ I(x+i, y+j) - \mu_{S} \right]^2 \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} \left[ T(i, j) - \mu_{T} \right]^2 } }$$

where:
- $\mu_{S} = \dfrac{1}{m \cdot n} \sum_{i,j} I(x+i, y+j)$ is the mean intensity of the sub-image.
- $\mu_{T} = \dfrac{1}{m \cdot n} \sum_{i,j} T(i, j)$ is the mean intensity of the template.

- **Decision Rule:** **Maximize** NCC.
- **Range:** $[-1, \; 1]$.
- **Optimum Value:** $+1$ (perfect linear correlation).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Metric | Formula (Concise Form) | Best Match At | Sensitivity to Illumination | Computational Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SSD** | $\sum_{i,j} \left( I - T \right)^2$ | $\min$ SSD | High | Medium |
| **SAD** | $\sum_{i,j} \left\vert I - T \right\vert$ | $\min$ SAD | High | Low (fastest) |
| **CC** | $\sum_{i,j} I \cdot T$ | $\max$ CC | Very High | Medium |
| **NCC** | $\dfrac{ \sum (I - \mu_S)(T - \mu_T) }{ \sqrt{ \sum (I - \mu_S)^2 \sum (T - \mu_T)^2 } }$ | $\max$ NCC | Low (Illumination-Invariant) | High |
| **ZNCC** (Zero-Mean NCC) | Same as NCC with mean removal | $\max$ ZNCC | Very Low | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** NCC is the *gold standard* in classical template matching because it is invariant to linear brightness and contrast changes. This is **THE** most frequently asked concept in board exams.

### 2.4 Mathematical Justification: Why NCC Is Illumination Invariant

Consider an image $I'$ that is a linearly scaled version of $I$: $I' = a \cdot I + b$, where $a > 0$ is the contrast factor and $b$ is the brightness offset.

The mean of the corresponding sub-image becomes $\mu_{S'} = a \cdot \mu_{S} + b$.

Substituting into the NCC numerator:

$$\sum_{i,j} (I' - \mu_{S'})(T - \mu_{T}) = \sum_{i,j} a(I - \mu_{S})(T - \mu_{T}) = a \cdot \text{Numerator}_{\text{original}}$$

The denominator scales identically: $\sqrt{a^2} = \vert a \vert = a$ (since $a > 0$).

The factor of $a$ **cancels out** in the numerator and denominator, leaving NCC unchanged. Hence, NCC is provably invariant to linear illumination changes.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PCB Inspection:** Comparing solder joint shapes against golden templates at high speed using SAD (lowest cost).
- **Biometric Authentication:** NCC-based fingerprint matching in legacy AFIS (Automated Fingerprint Identification System) deployments.
- **Astronomy:** Cross-correlating star fields to detect asteroids or stellar variability.
- **Video Compression (MPEG/H.264):** Motion estimation uses **SAD/SSD** as the block-matching cost to find the best prediction block from reference frames.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equivalence Between SSD Minimization and Cross-Correlation Maximization

This is a classic KTU derivation that links two seemingly different metrics. We will show that minimizing SSD is mathematically equivalent to maximizing Cross-Correlation under a constant-energy assumption.

**Step 1: Expand the SSD formula.**

$$\text{SSD}(x, y) = \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} \left[ I(x+i, y+j) - T(i, j) \right]^2$$

For brevity, let $I_{x,y}(i, j) = I(x+i, y+j)$ denote the sub-image pixel.

**Step 2: Apply the algebraic identity $(A - B)^2 = A^2 - 2AB + B^2$.**

$$\text{SSD}(x, y) = \sum_{i,j} \left[ I_{x,y}^2(i,j) - 2 \cdot I_{x,y}(i,j) \cdot T(i,j) + T^2(i,j) \right]$$

**Step 3: Split into three separate summations using linearity.**

$$\text{SSD}(x, y) = \underbrace{\sum_{i,j} I_{x,y}^2(i,j)}_{\text{Term 1: Image Energy}} \; - \; 2 \cdot \underbrace{\sum_{i,j} I_{x,y}(i,j) \cdot T(i,j)}_{\text{Term 2: Cross-Correlation}} \; + \; \underbrace{\sum_{i,j} T^2(i,j)}_{\text{Term 3: Template Energy}}$$

**Step 4: Analyze each term.**

- **Term 3** ($\sum T^2$) depends only on the template and is **independent of $(x, y)$**. It is a constant.
- **Term 1** ($\sum I^2$) is the local energy of the sub-image. It varies with position.
- **Term 2** is exactly $-2$ times the **Cross-Correlation** $\text{CC}(x, y)$.

**Step 5: Restate the SSD as a function of CC and the local energy.**

$$\text{SSD}(x, y) = E_{\text{local}}(x, y) \; - \; 2 \cdot \text{CC}(x, y) \; + \; E_{\text{template}}$$

**Step 6: Conclusion of the derivation.**

To minimize SSD, since $E_{\text{template}}$ is constant, we need to maximize CC *and* minimize $E_{\text{local}}$. However, if the local energy is approximately constant (smooth regions) or if we normalize it out (NCC), then minimizing SSD is **equivalent** to maximizing CC.

This is the fundamental reason why **NCC is the proper normalized form** that bridges the gap between SSD and CC and provides illumination invariance.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem Setup:** Consider a $4 \times 4$ search image $I$ and a $2 \times 2$ template $T$:

$$
I = \begin{pmatrix}
5 & 2 & 3 & 4 \\
1 & 7 & 6 & 8 \\
9 & 3 & 2 & 1 \\
4 & 5 & 7 & 6
\end{pmatrix}
\quad , \quad
T = \begin{pmatrix}
7 & 6 \\
3 & 2
\end{pmatrix}
$$

We will compute the **SSD map** $R$ of size $(4-2+1) \times (4-2+1) = 3 \times 3$.

**Step 1: Compute $R(0, 0)$ ŌĆö sub-image is top-left $2 \times 2$ of $I$.**

Sub-image $S_{0,0}$:
$$
S_{0,0} = \begin{pmatrix} 5 & 2 \\ 1 & 7 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Differences and squares:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(0,0) &= (5-7)^2 + (2-6)^2 + (1-3)^2 + (7-2)^2 \\
&= (-2)^2 + (-4)^2 + (-2)^2 + (5)^2 \\
&= 4 + 16 + 4 + 25 = 49
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $R(0, 1)$ ŌĆö sub-image starts at column 1.**

Sub-image $S_{0,1}$:
$$
S_{0,1} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 3 \\ 7 & 6 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(0,1) &= (2-7)^2 + (3-6)^2 + (7-3)^2 + (6-2)^2 \\
&= 25 + 9 + 16 + 16 = 66
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $R(0, 2)$ ŌĆö sub-image starts at column 2.**

Sub-image $S_{0,2}$:
$$
S_{0,2} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 4 \\ 6 & 8 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(0,2) &= (3-7)^2 + (4-6)^2 + (6-3)^2 + (8-2)^2 \\
&= 16 + 4 + 9 + 36 = 65
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute $R(1, 0)$ ŌĆö sub-image starts at row 1, column 0.**

Sub-image $S_{1,0}$:
$$
S_{1,0} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 7 \\ 9 & 3 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(1,0) &= (1-7)^2 + (7-6)^2 + (9-3)^2 + (3-2)^2 \\
&= 36 + 1 + 36 + 1 = 74
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute $R(1, 1)$ ŌĆö sub-image starts at row 1, column 1.**

Sub-image $S_{1,1}$:
$$
S_{1,1} = \begin{pmatrix} 7 & 6 \\ 3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(1,1) &= (7-7)^2 + (6-6)^2 + (3-3)^2 + (2-2)^2 \\
&= 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Compute $R(1, 2)$ ŌĆö sub-image starts at row 1, column 2.**

Sub-image $S_{1,2}$:
$$
S_{1,2} = \begin{pmatrix} 6 & 8 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(1,2) &= (6-7)^2 + (8-6)^2 + (2-3)^2 + (1-2)^2 \\
&= 1 + 4 + 1 + 1 = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Compute $R(2, 0)$ ŌĆö sub-image starts at row 2, column 0.**

Sub-image $S_{2,0}$:
$$
S_{2,0} = \begin{pmatrix} 9 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(2,0) &= (9-7)^2 + (3-6)^2 + (4-3)^2 + (5-2)^2 \\
&= 4 + 9 + 1 + 9 = 23
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8: Compute $R(2, 1)$ ŌĆö sub-image starts at row 2, column 1.**

Sub-image $S_{2,1}$:
$$
S_{2,1} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 5 & 7 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(2,1) &= (3-7)^2 + (2-6)^2 + (5-3)^2 + (7-2)^2 \\
&= 16 + 16 + 4 + 25 = 61
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9: Compute $R(2, 2)$ ŌĆö sub-image starts at row 2, column 2.**

Sub-image $S_{2,2}$:
$$
S_{2,2} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 7 & 6 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(2,2) &= (2-7)^2 + (1-6)^2 + (7-3)^2 + (6-2)^2 \\
&= 25 + 25 + 16 + 16 = 82
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 10: Assemble the SSD result map.**

$$
R_{\text{SSD}} = \begin{pmatrix}
49 & 66 & 65 \\
74 & \mathbf{0} & 7 \\
23 & 61 & 82
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 11: Decision Rule ŌĆö The minimum SSD is at $(1, 1)$ with value $0$.**

$$\boxed{(x^*, y^*) = \arg\min R_{\text{SSD}} = (1, 1)}$$

This perfectly aligns with intuition, since the sub-image at $(1, 1)$ is *identical* to the template.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | SSD | SAD | CC | NCC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mathematical Basis | $\ell_2$-norm | $\ell_1$-norm | Inner Product | Cosine Similarity |
| Optimum Value | $0$ | $0$ | Max | $+1$ |
| Illumination Invariant | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| Contrast Invariant | No | No | No | **Yes** |
| Robust to Outliers | No (squares outliers) | **Yes** (linear) | No | No |
| Speed | Medium | **Fastest** | Medium | Slow |
| Typical Use | Block matching (video) | Hardware motion estimation | Feature response | Biometrics, astronomy |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Template Matching

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the complete operational workflow of a template matching system, from input acquisition to final matched-coordinate output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Acquire Search Image I] --> B[Acquire or Define Template T of size m by n]
    B --> C[Select Matching Metric: SSD, SAD, CC, or NCC]
    C --> D[Initialize Result Map R of size M minus m plus 1 by N minus n plus 1]
    D --> E{Slide Template to position x, y}
    E --> F[Extract Sub-image S_x_y of size m by n]
    F --> G[Compute Metric Value: R of x, y]
    G --> H{All positions x, y covered?}
    H -->|No| E
    H -->|Yes| I[Search Result Map for Optimum: argmax or argmin]
    I --> J[Apply Threshold tau to filter weak matches]
    J --> K[Output Matched Coordinates x star, y star]
    K --> L[End]
```

### 4.2 Decision Tree for Choosing a Matching Metric

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need to Choose a Template Matching Metric] --> B{Are illumination or contrast changes expected?}
    B -->|No| C{Is computational speed critical?}
    B -->|Yes| D[Use Normalized Cross-Correlation NCC]
    C -->|Yes| E[Use Sum of Absolute Differences SAD]
    C -->|No| F{Need robustness to outlier pixels?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Use Sum of Absolute Differences SAD]
    F -->|No| H[Use Sum of Squared Differences SSD]
```

### 4.3 Sliding Window Visualization (Schematic Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SearchImage[Search Image I of size M by N] --> Window1[Sub-image at position 0,0 of size m by n]
    SearchImage --> Window2[Sub-image at position 0,1]
    SearchImage --> Window3[Sub-image at position 0,2]
    SearchImage --> Dots[...more positions...]
    SearchImage --> WindowK[Sub-image at position M minus m, N minus n]
    Window1 --> Comp1[Metric Computation 1]
    Window2 --> Comp2[Metric Computation 2]
    Window3 --> Comp3[Metric Computation 3]
    Dots --> Comp4[Metric Computation j]
    WindowK --> CompK[Metric Computation K]
    Comp1 --> ResultMap[Result Map R of size M minus m plus 1 by N minus n plus 1]
    Comp2 --> ResultMap
    Comp3 --> ResultMap
    Comp4 --> ResultMap
    CompK --> ResultMap
    ResultMap --> Optimum[Find Optimum: argmax or argmin]
    Optimum --> Output[Matched Location x star, y star]
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Hierarchy of Matching Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Template Matching Techniques] --> B[Difference Based Metrics]
    A --> C[Correlation Based Metrics]
    A --> D[Hybrid / Normalized Metrics]
    B --> B1[Sum of Squared Differences SSD]
    B --> B2[Sum of Absolute Differences SAD]
    C --> C1[Cross-Correlation CC]
    D --> D1[Normalized Cross-Correlation NCC]
    D --> D2[Zero-Mean Normalized Cross-Correlation ZNCC]
    D --> D3[Phase Correlation using FFT]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
Define template matching. List any two similarity measures used in template matching.

**Model Answer:**

Template matching is a digital image processing technique used to locate a small reference sub-image (the *template*) within a larger search image. It is performed by sliding the template over every possible spatial location in the search image and computing a numerical similarity score at each position. The location with the best score is declared the match.

**Two similarity measures:**

1. **Sum of Squared Differences (SSD):** Computes the cumulative squared pixel-wise difference between the template and the sub-image. A smaller SSD implies a better match.

2. **Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC):** Computes the cosine similarity between the mean-removed template and sub-image, yielding a value in $[-1, +1]$. A larger NCC implies a better match, with $+1$ being a perfect correlation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Key:** Students often forget to state the *decision rule* (minimize or maximize). Always specify whether the metric is a dissimilarity measure (minimize) or a similarity measure (maximize) to earn full marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
Why is Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC) preferred over simple Cross-Correlation (CC) in template matching?

**Model Answer:**

Normalized Cross-Correlation is preferred over simple Cross-Correlation because **NCC is invariant to linear changes in illumination** (i.e., scaling and offset of pixel intensities), whereas simple CC is highly sensitive to such changes.

**Mathematical Justification:**

If the search image undergoes a linear transformation $I' = a \cdot I + b$ (where $a$ is the contrast multiplier and $b$ is the brightness offset), the simple cross-correlation $\text{CC}$ scales linearly with $a$ and shifts with $b$, leading to incorrect detection.

In NCC, the mean intensities of both the sub-image ($\mu_S$) and the template ($\mu_T$) are subtracted out, and the result is divided by the standard deviations. This makes the NCC value depend only on the *structural pattern* of intensities, not on their absolute magnitude. Hence, the NCC value remains **unchanged** under linear illumination variations.

**Conclusion:** NCC provides a more robust and reliable match in real-world scenarios where lighting conditions are unpredictable.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model Paper]**

(a) Explain the procedure of template matching using block diagram. Discuss the limitations of brute-force template matching. **(7 Marks)**

(b) With a suitable example, derive the relationship between Sum of Squared Differences (SSD) and Cross-Correlation (CC). Show that minimizing SSD is equivalent to maximizing CC under a constant-image-energy assumption. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Procedure and Limitations:**

The block diagram of template matching consists of the following sequential stages:

1. **Template Acquisition:** Obtain a reference template $T$ of size $m \times n$ from a known good image or by manual cropping.
2. **Search Image Input:** Acquire the larger image $I$ of size $M \times N$ where the template is to be found.
3. **Sliding Window:** For each top-left position $(x, y)$ such that $0 \le x \le M - m$ and $0 \le y \le N - n$, extract the sub-image $S_{x,y}$ of size $m \times n$.
4. **Metric Computation:** Compute the chosen matching metric (SSD, SAD, CC, or NCC) between $T$ and $S_{x,y}$.
5. **Result Map Construction:** Store all metric values in a 2D result map $R$ of size $(M - m + 1) \times (N - n + 1)$.
6. **Optimum Search:** Find the coordinate $(x^*, y^*)$ that minimizes SSD/SAD or maximizes CC/NCC.
7. **Output:** Declare $(x^*, y^*)$ as the matched location.

**Limitations of Brute-Force Template Matching:**

- **High Computational Cost:** The complexity is $\mathcal{O}(M \cdot N \cdot m \cdot n)$, which becomes prohibitive for large images.
- **Illumination Sensitivity:** SSD, SAD, and CC fail under varying lighting conditions.
- **Scale and Rotation Invariance:** Brute-force template matching cannot handle template scaling or rotation; it only handles translation.
- **Multiple Match Ambiguity:** If the template has multiple instances, naive methods may return only the global optimum.
- **Occlusion Sensitivity:** Partially occluded templates are often missed.

**Part (b) ŌĆö Derivation of SSD-CC Equivalence:**

Starting from the SSD formula:

$$\text{SSD}(x, y) = \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} \left[ I(x+i, y+j) - T(i, j) \right]^2$$

Expand the square:

$$\text{SSD}(x, y) = \sum_{i,j} I^2(x+i, y+j) - 2 \sum_{i,j} I(x+i, y+j) \cdot T(i,j) + \sum_{i,j} T^2(i,j)$$

Group the terms:

$$\text{SSD}(x, y) = \underbrace{\sum_{i,j} I^2}_{\text{Local Image Energy}} - 2 \cdot \underbrace{\sum_{i,j} I \cdot T}_{\text{Cross-Correlation CC}} + \underbrace{\sum_{i,j} T^2}_{\text{Constant Template Energy}}$$

**Conclusion:**

Since $\sum T^2$ is a constant (does not depend on $(x, y)$), and if we assume the local image energy $\sum I^2$ is also approximately constant across the search region (valid for smooth or uniformly textured backgrounds), then minimizing SSD is equivalent to maximizing CC. This equivalence forms the theoretical foundation for using correlation as a matching metric in the Fourier domain (where convolution via FFT is $\mathcal{O}(MN \log MN)$, dramatically faster than the brute-force $\mathcal{O}(MNmn)$ cost).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**

(a) Explain the Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC) formula. Prove mathematically that NCC is invariant to linear brightness and contrast changes. **(7 Marks)**

(b) For the following $4 \times 4$ search image and $2 \times 2$ template, compute the **Sum of Squared Differences (SSD)** map and identify the matched location.

$$
I = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 4 & 6 & 8 \\ 1 & 3 & 5 & 7 \\ 0 & 2 & 4 & 6 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
\quad , \quad
T = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 5 \\ 1 & 3 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö NCC Formula and Illumination Invariance Proof:**

The NCC formula is:

$$\text{NCC}(x, y) = \frac{ \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} \left[ I(x+i, y+j) - \mu_S \right] \left[ T(i, j) - \mu_T \right] }{ \sqrt{ \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} \left[ I(x+i, y+j) - \mu_S \right]^2 } \cdot \sqrt{ \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} \left[ T(i, j) - \mu_T \right]^2 } }$$

where $\mu_S$ and $\mu_T$ are the mean intensities of the sub-image and template respectively.

**Proof of Illumination Invariance:**

Consider a transformed image $I' = a \cdot I + b$, where $a > 0$ is a contrast scaling factor and $b$ is a brightness offset.

The mean of the transformed sub-image is:

$$\mu_{S'} = \frac{1}{m \cdot n} \sum_{i,j} [a \cdot I(x+i, y+j) + b] = a \cdot \mu_S + b$$

The transformed numerator becomes:

$$\text{Numerator}' = \sum_{i,j} \left[ (a I + b) - (a \mu_S + b) \right] (T - \mu_T) = \sum_{i,j} a (I - \mu_S)(T - \mu_T) = a \cdot \text{Numerator}$$

The transformed denominator (numerator part) becomes:

$$\sum_{i,j} \left[ (aI + b) - (a\mu_S + b) \right]^2 = a^2 \sum_{i,j} (I - \mu_S)^2$$

Therefore, the full denominator scales by $a$, and the new NCC is:

$$\text{NCC}' = \frac{a \cdot \text{Numerator}}{|a| \cdot \sqrt{\sum (I - \mu_S)^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum (T - \mu_T)^2}} = \frac{a}{|a|} \cdot \text{NCC} = \text{NCC}$$

(since $a > 0$, we have $a / |a| = 1$).

Hence, $\text{NCC}' = \text{NCC}$, proving invariance to linear illumination changes.

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical SSD Map Computation:**

The SSD result map is of size $(4-2+1) \times (4-2+1) = 3 \times 3$.

**Compute $R(0, 0)$** using sub-image $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 4 \\ 1 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$:

$$
R(0,0) = (2-3)^2 + (4-5)^2 + (1-1)^2 + (3-3)^2 = 1 + 1 + 0 + 0 = 2
$$

**Compute $R(0, 1)$** using sub-image $\begin{pmatrix} 4 & 6 \\ 3 & 5 \end{pmatrix}$:

$$
R(0,1) = (4-3)^2 + (6-5)^2 + (3-1)^2 + (5-3)^2 = 1 + 1 + 4 + 4 = 10
$$

**Compute $R(0, 2)$** using sub-image $\begin{pmatrix} 6 & 8 \\ 5 & 7 \end{pmatrix}$:

$$
R(0,2) = (6-3)^2 + (8-5)^2 + (5-1)^2 + (7-3)^2 = 9 + 9 + 16 + 16 = 50
$$

**Compute $R(1, 0)$** using sub-image $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$:

$$
R(1,0) = (1-3)^2 + (3-5)^2 + (0-1)^2 + (2-3)^2 = 4 + 4 + 1 + 1 = 10
$$

**Compute $R(1, 1)$** using sub-image $\begin{pmatrix} 3 & 5 \\ 2 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$:

$$
R(1,1) = (3-3)^2 + (5-5)^2 + (2-1)^2 + (4-3)^2 = 0 + 0 + 1 + 1 = 2
$$

**Compute $R(1, 2)$** using sub-image $\begin{pmatrix} 5 & 7 \\ 4 & 6 \end{pmatrix}$:

$$
R(1,2) = (5-3)^2 + (7-5)^2 + (4-1)^2 + (6-3)^2 = 4 + 4 + 9 + 9 = 26
$$

**Compute $R(2, 0)$** using sub-image $\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 2 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$:

$$
R(2,0) = (0-3)^2 + (2-5)^2 + (1-1)^2 + (1-3)^2 = 9 + 9 + 0 + 4 = 22
$$

**Compute $R(2, 1)$** using sub-image $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 4 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$:

$$
R(2,1) = (2-3)^2 + (4-5)^2 + (1-1)^2 + (1-3)^2 = 1 + 1 + 0 + 4 = 6
$$

**Compute $R(2, 2)$** using sub-image $\begin{pmatrix} 4 & 6 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$:

$$
R(2,2) = (4-3)^2 + (6-5)^2 + (1-1)^2 + (1-3)^2 = 1 + 1 + 0 + 4 = 6
$$

**SSD Map:**

$$
R_{\text{SSD}} = \begin{pmatrix}
2 & 10 & 50 \\
10 & 2 & 26 \\
22 & 6 & 6
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Matched Location:**

The minimum SSD value is $2$, occurring at two positions: $(0, 0)$ and $(1, 1)$. The principal match is at $\boxed{(0, 0)}$ since the template perfectly aligns with the top-left sub-image (all differences are 0 except one pixel each).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Brute-Force Complexity:** Students often quote the matching cost as $M \times N$ instead of $M \times N \times m \times n$. The inner product of the template must be included.
> 2. **Confusing the Decision Rule:** Always clearly state whether the match is at $\arg\max$ (for similarity metrics like CC, NCC) or $\arg\min$ (for dissimilarity metrics like SSD, SAD). Loss of **1 mark** is common here.
> 3. **Forgetting the Mean in NCC:** Students frequently write the simple CC formula and incorrectly label it as NCC. The mean subtraction $\mu_S$ and $\mu_T$ is the *defining* feature of NCC. Marks are docked if it is omitted.
> 4. **Result Map Size:** Students sometimes forget that the result map dimensions are $(M - m + 1) \times (N - n + 1)$, not $M \times N$ or $m \times n$.
> 5. **Off-by-One Indexing:** In the worked example, the top-left position is indexed as $(0, 0)$, not $(1, 1)$. Mismatch of indexing convention leads to wrong final answers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Template Matching** locates a small reference image (template) within a larger search image by sliding and comparing.
- **Four core metrics:** SSD, SAD, CC, NCC ŌĆö each with distinct mathematical properties, computational costs, and robustness characteristics.
- **SSD = $\sum (I - T)^2$** ŌåÆ minimize ŌåÆ sensitive to outliers and illumination.
- **SAD = $\sum \vert I - T \vert$** ŌåÆ minimize ŌåÆ fastest, robust to outliers.
- **CC = $\sum I \cdot T$** ŌåÆ maximize ŌåÆ very sensitive to illumination.
- **NCC** subtracts means and normalizes by standard deviations ŌåÆ maximize ŌåÆ invariant to linear illumination changes; the *gold standard* in classical template matching.
- **Result map size** is always $(M - m + 1) \times (N - n + 1)$.
- **Computational complexity** of brute-force template matching is $\mathcal{O}(M \cdot N \cdot m \cdot n)$, motivating FFT-based correlation.
- **Limitations:** No scale invariance, no rotation invariance, no occlusion handling ŌĆö these motivate feature-based (SIFT, SURF) and learning-based (CNN) detectors.
- **Best match location:** $\arg\min$ for SSD/SAD, $\arg\max$ for CC/NCC.
- **KTU Hot Topic:** The SSD-CC mathematical equivalence and the NCC illumination-invariance proof are the two highest-weightage derivations asked in board exams.
- **Practical tip:** In video coding (H.264/HEVC), SAD is preferred due to hardware-friendly integer arithmetic; in biometrics, NCC is preferred for robustness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Strategies in Templating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST636 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control Strategies in Templating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Digital Image Processing (DIP)** under the KTU 2024 scheme, **Control Strategies in Templating** refers to the set of computational governance mechanisms, search orchestration schemes, and adaptive decision policies used to direct the template-matching process during object localization, pattern recognition, and feature alignment tasks. A *template* is a reference sub-image (kernel pattern) of dimensions $W \times H$ pixels, and the *control strategy* dictates **how**, **where**, and **in what order** the template is compared against candidate regions of the source image $f(x, y)$ of dimensions $M \times N$.

Mathematically, a control strategy defines a mapping:

$$
\mathcal{C}: \mathbb{R}^{2} \rightarrow \{0, 1\}, \quad (x, y) \mapsto \mathcal{C}(x, y)
$$

where $\mathcal{C}(x, y) = 1$ indicates that the candidate window centered at $(x, y)$ is **selected for evaluation**, and $\mathcal{C}(x, y) = 0$ denotes **rejection/skipped**. The objective is to minimize the total number of evaluations $|\{(x,y) : \mathcal{C}(x,y) = 1\}|$ while maximizing the probability of detecting the true match.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST636 Module 3):**
> Template matching is a cornerstone operation in image segmentation, motion estimation, video tracking, and biometric authentication. The *control strategy* governs computational efficiency ŌĆö a critical aspect in real-time KTU lab exercises and viva questions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you have lost your **black wallet** in a large, dimly-lit auditorium with 1000 seats.

- **Strategy 1 (Brute-Force / Exhaustive Search):** You walk to **every single seat**, look down, and check if your wallet is there. This is guaranteed to find it but is **slow** and **exhausting**.
- **Strategy 2 (Hierarchical / Coarse-to-Fine Search):** First, you glance at the **left half** of the auditorium. If you see a black object, you zoom in. This is much faster.
- **Strategy 3 (Feature-Based Steering):** You remember the wallet has a **shiny metal clasp**. You only look for shiny reflections ŌĆö you completely ignore dark, matte seats.
- **Strategy 4 (Adaptive / Predictive Steering):** You start at the entrance (most likely drop-zone) and progressively expand outward, similar to a *spiral search pattern*.

Each of these is a **control strategy** for templating. In DIP, the "auditorium" is the image, the "wallet" is the template, and the strategy decides which pixels get evaluated.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following are standardized benchmarking metrics used universally in KTU DIP evaluations:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Image spatial resolution | $M \times N$ | **pixels** |
| Template size | $W \times H$ | **pixels** |
| Computational complexity baseline | $\mathcal{O}(M N W H)$ | **FLOPs** |
| Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (template match quality) | $\text{PSNR} \geq 20 \text{ dB}$ | **decibels** |
| Normalized Cross-Correlation threshold | $\gamma_{NCC} \geq 0.8$ | **dimensionless ratio** |
| Search reduction ratio | $\rho = \frac{\text{Evaluated windows}}{\text{Total windows}}$ | **0 Ōēż Žü Ōēż 1** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** A lower search reduction ratio $\rho$ directly translates to faster execution ŌĆö a key KTU viva point when discussing optimization trade-offs.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Decomposition of Control Strategies

Control strategies in templating are classified along **three orthogonal axes** in the KTU 2024 framework:

### Axis A ŌĆö Search Topology
- **Sequential / Raster Scan**
- **Spiral / Concentric**
- **Hierarchical / Pyramid-based**
- **Logarithmic / Binary Search Step (2D)**

### Axis B ŌĆö Similarity Metric
- **Sum of Absolute Differences (SAD)**
- **Sum of Squared Differences (SSD)**
- **Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC)**
- **Mutual Information (MI)**

### Axis C ŌĆö Decision Policy
- **Threshold-based hard cutoff**
- **Bayesian probabilistic acceptance**
- **Adaptive / Learning-based (ANN, SVM)**

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step

A typical KTU-style templating pipeline governed by a control strategy executes as follows:

1. **Template Pre-processing:** Apply grayscale conversion, normalization, and noise suppression to the template $t(u, v)$.
2. **Search Space Initialization:** Define the feasible search region $\mathcal{S} \subset \mathbb{Z}^{2}$ bounded by $[1, M-W+1] \times [1, N-H+1]$.
3. **Strategy Selection:** The control policy $\mathcal{C}$ determines the candidate set $\mathcal{S}' \subseteq \mathcal{S}$.
4. **Similarity Computation:** For each $(x, y) \in \mathcal{S}'$, compute the dissimilarity/similarity score $D(x, y)$ or $R(x, y)$.
5. **Decision Thresholding:** Accept or reject each candidate using the governing decision rule.
6. **Refinement (Optional):** Apply sub-pixel refinement via interpolation.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every high-yield equation** for KTU board examination problems on templating control strategies. Memorize the symbols, not just the equations.

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sum of Absolute Differences (SAD) | $D_{SAD}(x,y) = \sum_{u,v} \vert f(x+u, y+v) - t(u,v) \vert$ | $D \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ |
| 2 | Sum of Squared Differences (SSD) | $D_{SSD}(x,y) = \sum_{u,v} \left[f(x+u, y+v) - t(u,v)\right]^{2}$ | $D \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ |
| 3 | Mean Absolute Difference | $D_{MAD}(x,y) = \frac{1}{WH} D_{SAD}(x,y)$ | Normalized score |
| 4 | Normalized Cross-Correlation | $R_{NCC}(x,y) = \frac{\sum_{u,v} f \cdot t}{\sqrt{\sum f^{2} \cdot \sum t^{2}}}$ | $R \in [-1, 1]$ |
| 5 | Zero-mean NCC (ZNCC) | $R_{ZNCC} = \frac{\sum (f - \bar{f})(t - \bar{t})}{\sqrt{\sum(f-\bar{f})^{2}\sum(t-\bar{t})^{2}}}$ | Robust to illumination |
| 6 | Search Reduction Ratio | $\rho = \frac{\vert \mathcal{S}' \vert}{\vert \mathcal{S} \vert}$ | $\rho \in [0, 1]$ |
| 7 | Speed-up Factor | $\eta = \frac{1}{\rho}$ | $\eta \geq 1$ |
| 8 | Computational Complexity (Brute) | $\mathcal{O}(M N W H)$ | Worst-case FLOPs |
| 9 | Complexity (2D Log Search) | $\mathcal{O}(W H \log_{2}(MN))$ | Reduced FLOPs |
| 10 | PSNR (Match Quality) | $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{L^{2}}{MSE}\right)$ | $L = 255$ for 8-bit |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Medical Imaging** | Tumor localization across MRI slices (controlled coarse-to-fine search) |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Real-time road-sign recognition (hierarchical template steering) |
| **PCB Defect Detection** | Component placement verification (exhaustive + ZNCC) |
| **Biometrics** | Iris / fingerprint template matching in Aadhaar-style systems |
| **Video Surveillance** | Multi-object tracking with adaptive template update |
| **Industrial Robotics** | Pick-and-place alignment using spiral search strategies |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Exhaustive Search Computational Complexity

We derive the dominant computational cost for the brute-force (raster-scan) control strategy, which is the **baseline benchmark** for every KTU exam problem on templating efficiency.

**Given:**
- Source image $f(x, y)$ of dimensions $M \times N$
- Template $t(u, v)$ of dimensions $W \times H$
- Total candidate positions: the template's top-left corner can be placed at any integer position such that the template remains entirely inside the image.

**Step 1: Determine the legal range for the top-left corner.**
The horizontal range is $x \in \{1, 2, \ldots, M - W + 1\}$, giving $(M - W + 1)$ valid horizontal positions.
Similarly, the vertical range gives $(N - H + 1)$ valid vertical positions.

**Step 2: Total number of candidate windows.**
$$
\text{Total candidates} = (M - W + 1)(N - H + 1) \approx MN \text{ (for } W, H \ll M, N\text{)}
$$

**Step 3: Per-candidate operations.**
For each candidate position, the similarity score requires summing over all $WH$ pixels of the template:
$$
\text{Ops per candidate} = WH
$$

**Step 4: Aggregate complexity.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Ops} &= (M - W + 1)(N - H + 1) \cdot (WH) \\
&\approx M \cdot N \cdot W \cdot H \\
&= \mathcal{O}(M N W H)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation.**
This quartic growth means a doubling of either image or template dimension results in a **16├Ś increase** in computation ŌĆö the central motivation for non-exhaustive control strategies.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: 2D Logarithmic Search Step Complexity

The **2D Log Search** control strategy is a classic KTU favorite. It evaluates only $\mathcal{O}(\log_2(\max(M,N)))$ steps instead of $MN$.

**Step 1: Initial step size.**
Set initial step $s_0 = 2^{\lfloor \log_2(\max(M,N)) \rfloor}$.

**Step 2: At each iteration $k$, evaluate 5 candidate positions** (center + 4 cardinal neighbors at offset $s_k$).

**Step 3: Update rule.**
Pick the best-matching candidate. If the center is best, halve the step: $s_{k+1} = s_k / 2$.

**Step 4: Termination.**
Stop when $s_k = 1$.

**Step 5: Total step count.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
k_{\max} &= \log_2(s_0) \\
&= \log_2\left(2^{\lfloor \log_2(\max(M,N)) \rfloor}\right) \\
&= \lfloor \log_2(\max(M,N)) \rfloor \\
&\approx \log_2(MN)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Total complexity.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Ops} &= 5 \cdot WH \cdot \log_2(MN) \\
&= \mathcal{O}(WH \log_2(MN))
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Speed-up vs. brute-force.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{\mathcal{O}(MNWH)}{\mathcal{O}(WH \log_2 MN)} \\
&= \mathcal{O}\!\left(\frac{MN}{\log_2 MN}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For $M = N = 1024$, this yields $\eta \approx 1024 \times 1024 / 20 \approx 52{,}428\times$, a dramatic reduction.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC) Stability

We demonstrate why ZNCC is **illumination-invariant** ŌĆö a frequently tested KTU concept.

**Step 1: Write NCC in expanded form.**
$$
R_{NCC}(x,y) = \frac{\sum_{u,v}[f(x+u, y+v) - \bar{f}_{x,y}][t(u,v) - \bar{t}]}{\sqrt{\sum [f - \bar{f}_{x,y}]^{2} \sum [t - \bar{t}]^{2}}}
$$

**Step 2: Apply illumination perturbation.**
Let $f'(x, y) = a \cdot f(x, y) + b$ (linear brightness/contrast change), where $a > 0$ and $b$ are constants.

**Step 3: Compute new mean.**
$$
\bar{f}'_{x,y} = a \bar{f}_{x,y} + b
$$

**Step 4: Compute centered values.**
$$
f'(x+u, y+v) - \bar{f}'_{x,y} = a[f(x+u, y+v) - \bar{f}_{x,y}]
$$

**Step 5: Substitute into NCC.**
The numerator scales by $a$, and the denominator's $f$-term scales by $\sqrt{a^{2}} = a$, so:
$$
R'_{NCC}(x,y) = \frac{a \cdot \text{num}}{a \cdot \text{den}} = R_{NCC}(x,y)
$$

**Conclusion:** $R_{NCC}$ is **invariant to linear illumination changes**, making ZNCC the preferred metric in real-world KTU practical scenarios with variable lighting.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Templating Control System

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the **end-to-end functional pipeline** governed by the control strategy. All node IDs are alphanumeric and use safe labels (no reserved keywords).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Image f x y] --> B[Pre processing Module]
    B --> C[Template t u v Input]
    C --> D[Control Strategy Selector C]
    D --> D1{Strategy Type}
    D1 -->|Exhaustive| E1[Raster Scan Generator]
    D1 -->|Hierarchical| E2[Pyramid Decomposer]
    D1 -->|Log Search| E3[Log Step Generator]
    D1 -->|Adaptive| E4[Bayesian Predictor]
    E1 --> F[Candidate Set S prime]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Similarity Metric Engine]
    G --> G1{SAD SSD NCC ZNCC}
    G1 --> H[Score Map D x y or R x y]
    H --> I[Decision Policy]
    I --> I1{Threshold gamma}
    I1 -->|Pass| J[Match Localization x y]
    I1 -->|Reject| K[Discard Candidate]
    J --> L[Post refinement Sub pixel]
    L --> M[Output Bounding Box]
    K --> N[Continue Search Loop]
    N --> F
```

---

## 4.2 Strategy Comparison Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Choose Control Strategy] --> P1{Real time Required}
    P1 -->|Yes| Q1{Accuracy Critical}
    P1 -->|No| Q2[Exhaustive Search]
    Q1 -->|Yes| R1[2D Log Search]
    Q1 -->|No| R2[Hierarchical Pyramid]
    Q2 --> S[Brute Force Raster]
    R1 --> T[Logarithmic Step Halving]
    R2 --> U[Multi Resolution Coarse to Fine]
```

---

## 4.3 Search Topology Visualization (Raster vs Spiral vs Log)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Search Topology Selection] --> A1[Raster Scan]
    A0 --> A2[Spiral Search]
    A0 --> A3[2D Log Search]
    A1 --> A1A[Row by Row Left to Right]
    A2 --> A2A[Center Outward Concentric]
    A3 --> A3A[Step Halving Around Best Match]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define *template matching* in Digital Image Processing. List any **two** control strategies used to govern the search process.

**Model Answer:**

Template matching is the process of locating a smaller reference sub-image (the *template* $t(u,v)$ of size $W \times H$) within a larger source image $f(x,y)$ of size $M \times N$ by computing a similarity/dissimilarity metric at every feasible position and identifying the location with the optimal score.

Two control strategies used to govern the search process are:

1. **Exhaustive (Brute-Force) Search:** The template is slid across every possible position in the image in raster order ŌĆö guaranteed to find the global optimum but has complexity $\mathcal{O}(M N W H)$.
2. **2D Logarithmic Search:** Evaluates only 5 positions per iteration with a halving step size ŌĆö achieves sub-linear complexity $\mathcal{O}(WH \log_2 MN)$ but may converge to local optima.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for the two strategies with brief justification.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** What is the **Normalized Cross-Correlation (NCC)** metric? Why is it preferred over SAD for outdoor template matching applications?

**Model Answer:**

Normalized Cross-Correlation is a similarity metric defined as:

$$
R_{NCC}(x,y) = \frac{\sum_{u,v} f(x+u, y+v) \cdot t(u,v)}{\sqrt{\sum_{u,v} f^{2} \cdot \sum_{u,v} t^{2}}}
$$

It returns a dimensionless value in $[-1, +1]$, where $+1$ indicates a perfect match.

NCC is preferred over SAD for **outdoor template matching** because:

- **Illumination invariance:** NCC normalizes out linear brightness and contrast changes (i.e., invariant to transformations $f' = a f + b$), making it robust to varying sunlight/shadow conditions.
- **Scale invariance (partial):** When zero-mean variants (ZNCC) are used, the metric compensates for mean-intensity offsets between image and template.
- SAD, in contrast, is highly sensitive to global illumination shifts and produces inconsistent scores under varying outdoor lighting.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for invariance property, 1 mark for SAD comparison.)*

---

## PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Choice Rule (PECST636):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ŌŁÉ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q:** With reference to control strategies in template matching:

**(a)** Explain the **Exhaustive Search** and **2D Logarithmic Search** strategies in detail. Compare their computational complexities with a numerical example.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Zero-mean Normalized Cross-Correlation (ZNCC)** expression and prove mathematically that it is invariant to linear illumination changes of the form $f'(x, y) = a \cdot f(x, y) + b$, where $a > 0$ and $b \in \mathbb{R}$.  **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**1. Exhaustive Search (Raster Scan):**
The template $t(u, v)$ of size $W \times H$ is placed at every valid position $(x, y)$ in the image $f$ where:
- $1 \leq x \leq M - W + 1$
- $1 \leq y \leq N - H + 1$

For each candidate position, a similarity score (e.g., SAD or NCC) is computed. The position with the **minimum dissimilarity** or **maximum similarity** is declared the match. This strategy guarantees the global optimum but evaluates **all** $(M-W+1)(N-H+1) \approx MN$ candidate windows.

**2. 2D Logarithmic Search:**
- **Step 1:** Initialize step size $s = 2^{\lfloor \log_2(\max(M,N)) \rfloor}$.
- **Step 2:** Evaluate 5 candidates ŌĆö center + 4 cardinal neighbors at offset $s$.
- **Step 3:** Choose the best-matching candidate. If the center wins, halve $s$; else shift center to the winner.
- **Step 4:** Repeat until $s = 1$, then refine locally.

**3. Numerical Example:**
Let $M = N = 512$ and $W = H = 32$.

- **Exhaustive cost:** $512 \times 512 \times 32 \times 32 = 268{,}435{,}456$ FLOPs.
- **Log search cost:** $5 \times 32 \times 32 \times \log_2(512 \times 512) = 5 \times 1024 \times 18 \approx 92{,}160$ FLOPs.
- **Speed-up:** $\eta = 268{,}435{,}456 / 92{,}160 \approx 2913\times$.

**4. Trade-off Table:**

| Property | Exhaustive | 2D Log |
|---|---|---|
| Optimality guarantee | Global optimum | Local optimum (usually) |
| Complexity | $\mathcal{O}(M N W H)$ | $\mathcal{O}(W H \log_2 MN)$ |
| Real-time suitability | Poor | Excellent |
| Robustness to noise | High | Moderate |

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Derivation of ZNCC:**

Let $\bar{f}_{x,y}$ and $\bar{t}$ denote local means of image window and template respectively.

$$
R_{ZNCC}(x,y) = \frac{\sum_{u=0}^{W-1}\sum_{v=0}^{H-1} [f(x+u, y+v) - \bar{f}_{x,y}][t(u,v) - \bar{t}]}{\sqrt{\sum_{u,v}[f(x+u, y+v) - \bar{f}_{x,y}]^{2} \cdot \sum_{u,v}[t(u,v) - \bar{t}]^{2}}}
$$

**Proof of invariance under $f' = a f + b$:**

The local mean transforms as:
$$
\bar{f}'_{x,y} = \frac{1}{WH} \sum [a f(x+u, y+v) + b] = a \bar{f}_{x,y} + b
$$

The centered values:
$$
f'(x+u, y+v) - \bar{f}'_{x,y} = a f(x+u, y+v) + b - a \bar{f}_{x,y} - b = a[f(x+u, y+v) - \bar{f}_{x,y}]
$$

Substituting into the numerator:
$$
\sum [a(f - \bar{f})][t - \bar{t}] = a \sum (f - \bar{f})(t - \bar{t})
$$

Substituting into the denominator's $f$-sum:
$$
\sum [a(f - \bar{f})]^{2} = a^{2} \sum (f - \bar{f})^{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{\ldots} = a \sqrt{\sum (f - \bar{f})^{2}}
$$

Therefore:
$$
R'_{ZNCC} = \frac{a \cdot \text{num}}{a \cdot \text{den}} = R_{ZNCC} \quad \blacksquare
$$

The constant $b$ is **eliminated by the mean-subtraction step**, and the constant $a$ is **canceled in the ratio**, establishing full invariance to linear illumination changes.

---

### ŌŁÉ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q:**

**(a)** Describe the **Hierarchical (Pyramid-based) Search** control strategy with a suitable block diagram. Explain how multi-resolution decomposition reduces computational cost while maintaining match accuracy.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a $256 \times 256$ image and a $16 \times 16$ template, compute the total number of FLOPs required by (i) brute-force exhaustive search and (ii) a 3-level Gaussian pyramid hierarchical search. Assume the pyramid reduces each dimension by a factor of 2 at every level.  **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Hierarchical Search Architecture:**

The source image and template are decomposed into a **Gaussian (or Laplacian) pyramid** with $L$ levels. The pyramid level $l$ has dimensions $M/2^{l} \times N/2^{l}$, while the template pyramid level has dimensions $W/2^{l} \times H/2^{l}$.

**Search Procedure:**

1. **Coarsest level (Level $L-1$):** Exhaustive search on the smallest image ŌĆö very few candidates, very cheap.
2. **Level-by-level refinement:** Project the best match coordinates from level $l+1$ up to level $l$ by multiplying by 2.
3. **Local search at level $l$:** Evaluate only a small neighborhood (e.g., $\pm 2$ pixels) around the projected location.
4. **Final level:** Sub-pixel refinement using parabolic interpolation.

**Why It Reduces Cost:**

- The **coarse level** uses a tiny image and tiny template ŌåÆ trivially fast.
- The **refinement levels** only evaluate $\mathcal{O}(1)$ local candidates instead of $\mathcal{O}(MN)$.
- Total complexity is dominated by the coarsest level:
$$
\mathcal{O}_{\text{hier}} = \sum_{l=0}^{L-1} \mathcal{O}\!\left(\frac{MN \cdot WH}{4^{l}} \cdot k^{2}\right) \approx \mathcal{O}(MN \cdot WH) \cdot \frac{1}{1 - 1/4} \cdot \frac{k^{2}}{4^{L-1}}
$$
where $k$ is the local search radius (typically 3ŌĆō5).

**Accuracy Maintenance:** Because the coarse level captures global structural information (low-frequency content) and refinement levels recover fine details, the final match accuracy is comparable to brute-force search.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Given:** $M = N = 256$, $W = H = 16$, $L = 3$ levels, decimation factor = 2.

**(i) Brute-Force FLOPs:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total} &= (M - W + 1)(N - H + 1) \cdot WH \\
&= (256 - 16 + 1)^{2} \cdot (16 \times 16) \\
&= 241^{2} \cdot 256 \\
&= 58{,}081 \cdot 256 \\
&= 14{,}868{,}736 \text{ FLOPs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Hierarchical Search FLOPs:**

**Level 2 (coarsest):** $M_2 = N_2 = 256/4 = 64$, $W_2 = H_2 = 16/4 = 4$.
Candidates: $(64 - 4 + 1)^{2} = 61^{2} = 3721$.
Ops per candidate: $4 \times 4 = 16$.
Level 2 cost: $3721 \times 16 = 59{,}536$ FLOPs.

**Level 1:** $M_1 = N_1 = 128$, $W_1 = H_1 = 8$.
Local search with $k = 3$ (i.e., $7 \times 7 = 49$ candidates).
Ops per candidate: $8 \times 8 = 64$.
Level 1 cost: $49 \times 64 = 3136$ FLOPs.

**Level 0 (finest):** $M_0 = N_0 = 256$, $W_0 = H_0 = 16$.
Local search with $k = 3$: 49 candidates.
Ops per candidate: $16 \times 16 = 256$.
Level 0 cost: $49 \times 256 = 12{,}544$ FLOPs.

**Total hierarchical cost:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total}_{\text{hier}} &= 59{,}536 + 3136 + 12{,}544 \\
&= 75{,}216 \text{ FLOPs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Speed-up factor:**
$$
\eta = \frac{14{,}868{,}736}{75{,}216} \approx 197.7 \times
$$

The hierarchical strategy achieves nearly **200├Ś speed-up** while preserving accuracy to within sub-pixel tolerance ŌĆö demonstrating the engineering value of multi-resolution control strategies.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in NCC formula:** Many students write $R_{NCC}$ without the denominator's square root ŌĆö partial credit only.
> 2. **Confusing SAD with SSD:** SAD uses absolute value; SSD uses squared differences. Markers deduct 1 mark for interchange.
> 3. **Ignoring boundary conditions:** Always state that valid candidate positions require $(x, y) \in [1, M-W+1] \times [1, N-H+1]$.
> 4. **Forgetting illumination invariance proof:** When asked to prove ZNCC invariance, students often forget the role of the $b$ (additive offset) term ŌĆö must explicitly show it cancels in the centered difference.
> 5. **Pyramid dimension computation errors:** Each level divides by the **decimation factor squared**, not just the factor. A 2├Ś decimation means image area reduces by 4├Ś.
> 6. **Complexity notation:** Use $\mathcal{O}$ notation only after explicit derivation ŌĆö never quote $\mathcal{O}(MNWH)$ without showing the $(M-W+1)(N-H+1)$ origin.

---

## ­¤ōī TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Template matching** locates a sub-image $t(u, v)$ within $f(x, y)$ by sliding and computing similarity/dissimilarity.
- **Control strategies** govern the order and extent of candidate evaluation.
- The **exhaustive search** has complexity $\mathcal{O}(M N W H)$ and guarantees global optimality.
- The **2D log search** evaluates 5 candidates per step with halving step size ŌĆö complexity $\mathcal{O}(W H \log_2 MN)$.
- **Hierarchical pyramid search** performs coarse-to-fine matching ŌĆö ideal for real-time applications.
- **SAD** is fast but illumination-sensitive; **SSD** is differentiable; **NCC** is bounded; **ZNCC** is illumination-invariant.
- The **search reduction ratio** $\rho$ and **speed-up factor** $\eta = 1/\rho$ are the standard KTU performance metrics.
- **ZNCC invariance proof** requires showing cancellation of both multiplicative ($a$) and additive ($b$) illumination terms.
- **Boundary validity:** Candidate positions are restricted to $(M-W+1)(N-H+1)$.
- **Pyramid levels** reduce both image and template dimensions by the decimation factor at each level.
- **Real-world applications** include medical imaging, biometrics, autonomous vehicles, and PCB inspection.
- **PSNR** with $L = 255$ quantifies match quality in 8-bit grayscale templates.
- Always **distinguish between similarity ($R$) and dissimilarity ($D$)** metrics in KTU answers ŌĆö they have opposite optimality directions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation Issues in Segmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST636 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Evaluation Issues in Segmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Evaluation" in Image Segmentation?

**Segmentation Evaluation** is the systematic process of measuring how *good*, *accurate*, or *useful* the output of a segmentation algorithm actually is, by comparing the algorithmically generated segments against either a known ground-truth reference (supervised) or a set of intrinsic statistical criteria (unsupervised).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, evaluation is a **post-processing analytical step** that determines the *quality*, *robustness*, and *clinical/applicative validity* of a segmentation method. It answers the single most important engineering question:

> *"How do I know my segmentation is correct if segmentation itself is an ill-posed problem?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Segmentation is an Ill-Posed Problem (Hadamard, 1902)**
> Segmentation is classified as ill-posed because:
> 1. **Non-uniqueness** ŌĆö multiple valid partitions of the same image exist.
> 2. **Instability** ŌĆö tiny input noise produces drastically different segmentations.
> 3. **No universal solution** ŌĆö what is "correct" depends on the application (medical vs. satellite vs. industrial).
> Hence, evaluation metrics become a *necessity*, not a luxury.

---

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The "Cooking Judge" Metaphor

Imagine a cooking competition where **multiple chefs (segmentation algorithms)** cook a "Biriyani" (the image). There is **no single recipe** the judge insists on. But the judge must still rank them.

| Judge's Tool (Metric) | What it Measures | Chef Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Taste test** (Visual) | Looks like Biriyani? | Boundary-based metric |
| **Ingredient match** | Right ingredients? | Region-based metric |
| **Expert reference dish** | Matches Chef Master? | Supervised (Ground Truth) |
| **Aroma & texture alone** | Internally consistent? | Unsupervised (Goodness) |

The judge uses multiple metrics because **no single metric tells the full story**. Similarly, no single evaluation metric can capture all aspects of segmentation quality.

---

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Evaluation? (Engineering Motivation)

> [!NOTE]
> **The 5 Pillars of Segmentation Evaluation (Per KTU Syllabus)**
> 1. **Algorithm Comparison** ŌĆö to choose the best algorithm among many.
> 2. **Parameter Tuning** ŌĆö to optimize threshold $T$, cluster count $K$, etc.
> 3. **Quality Assurance** ŌĆö for medical, military, autonomous-vehicle deployments where errors are catastrophic.
> 4. **Benchmarking** ŌĆö reproducible comparisons on standard datasets (BSDS, Pascal VOC, ISIC).
> 5. **Ground-Truth Validation** ŌĆö verifying that the "ground truth" itself is correct (since human annotators disagree).

---

## 1.4 Formal Definitions of Core Evaluation Terms

| Term | Formal Definition | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Ground Truth (GT)** | Manually-labeled correct segmentation used as the *reference standard*. | $S_{GT}$ |
| **Segmentation Result (SE)** | Output produced by the algorithm under test. | $S_{SE}$ |
| **Pixel Set** | Set of pixels belonging to a region/segment. | $P_i$ |
| **Discrepancy Method** | Method that *compares* $S_{SE}$ against $S_{GT}$ (supervised). | ŌĆö |
| **Goodness Method** | Method that judges $S_{SE}$ *on its own merits* (unsupervised). | ŌĆö |
| **Figure of Merit (FOM)** | Scalar value (typically 0 to 1) summarizing quality. | $F$ or $FOM$ |

---

## 1.5 The Three Broad Evaluation Philosophies

1. **Subjective (Qualitative) Evaluation** ŌĆö Humans visually inspect results. Fast, but biased and non-reproducible.
2. **Objective (Quantitative) Evaluation** ŌĆö Mathematical metrics computed pixel-by-pixel. Reproducible and the KTU-preferred method.
3. **Empirical Evaluation** ŌĆö Performance on real-world datasets under real-world noise. Used in final validation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus** ŌĆö The course emphasizes **Objective Empirical Discrepancy Methods** (Pratt's FOM, Rand Index, Jaccard, Hausdorff) and **Empirical Goodness Methods** (uniformity, contrast, shape measures). Both are high-weightage topics for ESE.

---

## 1.6 Categories of Evaluation Methods ŌĆö Master Map

| Category | Sub-Type | Ground Truth Required? | KTU Priority |
|---|---|:---:|:---:|
| **Analytical** | Algorithm complexity, convergence | ŌØī No | Low |
| **Empirical Goodness** | Region uniformity, intra-region variance, contrast | ŌØī No | **High** |
| **Empirical Discrepancy** | Pratt FOM, Rand, Jaccard, Hausdorff, Boundary F1 | Ō£ģ Yes | **High** |
| **Subjective** | Human visual scoring | Optional | Low |

The remainder of these notes will focus on the **two high-priority categories** ŌĆö *Empirical Goodness* and *Empirical Discrepancy*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Empirical Goodness Methods (Unsupervised Evaluation)

These methods do **not require ground truth**. They evaluate the segmentation based on the *internal statistical properties* of the segmented regions. They are useful when GT is unavailable, expensive, or unreliable.

### 2.1.1 Region Uniformity (Intra-Region Variance)

A good segmentation should produce regions where pixels are *highly similar* (low variance).

$$U = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{R} \sigma_i^2 \cdot n_i}{N \cdot \sigma_{total}^2}$$

Where:
- $R$ = number of regions produced
- $\sigma_i^2$ = variance of intensities in region $i$
- $n_i$ = number of pixels in region $i$
- $N$ = total number of pixels
- $\sigma_{total}^2$ = variance of the entire image

**Interpretation:** $U \to 1$ indicates high uniformity (good segmentation); $U \to 0$ indicates poor uniformity.

### 2.1.2 Intra-Region Contrast (Inter-Region Contrast)

Measures the average contrast between adjacent regions.

$$C_{intra} = \frac{\sum_{(i,j) \in Adj} \vert \mu_i - \mu_j \vert}{\sum_{(i,j) \in Adj} (\mu_i + \mu_j + \epsilon)}$$

Where $\mu_i$ and $\mu_j$ are mean intensities of adjacent regions $i$ and $j$.

**Higher $C_{intra}$** = better separation between regions.

### 2.1.3 Shape Measure (Boundary Smoothness)

Evaluates the *smoothness* of region boundaries. A penalization is applied to jagged or noisy boundaries.

$$S = \frac{1}{R} \sum_{i=1}^{R} \frac{L_i^2}{A_i}$$

Where $L_i$ is the perimeter of region $i$ and $A_i$ is its area. For a circle, $\frac{L^2}{A} = 4\pi$ (minimum).

---

## 2.2 Empirical Discrepancy Methods (Supervised Evaluation)

These methods **require ground truth**. They directly compare $S_{SE}$ to $S_{GT}$ at the pixel or boundary level.

### 2.2.1 Confusion Matrix Foundation

For a binary segmentation (foreground vs. background), each pixel can be classified as:

| Term | Meaning | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **True Positive** | Foreground pixel correctly classified | $TP$ |
| **True Negative** | Background pixel correctly classified | $TN$ |
| **False Positive** | Background pixel wrongly classified as foreground | $FP$ |
| **False Negative** | Foreground pixel wrongly classified as background | $FN$ |

Total pixels: $N = TP + TN + FP + FN$.

### 2.2.2 Pixel-Level Discrepancy Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Range | Ideal Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Misclassification Error (ME)** | $ME = \dfrac{FP + FN}{N}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $0$ |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $0$ |
| **False Negative Rate (FNR)** | $FNR = \dfrac{FN}{TP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $0$ |
| **Sensitivity (Recall)** | $Recall = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $1$ |
| **Specificity** | $Specificity = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $1$ |
| **Precision** | $Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $1$ |
| **Dice / F1 Score** | $F1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall} = \dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $1$ |
| **Jaccard Index (IoU)** | $JI = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $1$ |
| **Rand Index (RI)** | $RI = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $1$ |
| **Adjusted Rand Index (ARI)** | $ARI = \dfrac{RI - E[RI]}{max(RI) - E[RI]}$ | $[-1, 1]$ | $1$ |

### 2.2.3 Pratt's Figure of Merit (FOM) ŌĆö Boundary Discrepancy

This is the **most important KTU-high-yield formula** for boundary-based evaluation, designed specifically for edge-detection evaluation.

$$FOM = \frac{1}{\max(N_{GT}, \, N_{SE})} \sum_{i=1}^{N_{SE}} \frac{1}{1 + \alpha \cdot d_i^2}$$

Where:
- $N_{GT}$ = number of edge points in ground truth
- $N_{SE}$ = number of edge points in segmentation result
- $d_i$ = Euclidean distance from $i$-th detected edge point to the nearest true edge point
- $\alpha$ = scaling constant (typically $\alpha = 1/9$ chosen by Pratt)
- $FOM = 1$ indicates perfect match; $FOM \to 0$ indicates poor match.

### 2.2.4 Hausdorff Distance (Boundary Geometric Error)

Measures the *worst-case* distance between two boundary sets $A$ (GT) and $B$ (SE).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Directed Hausdorff:**
$$h(A, B) = \max_{a \in A} \min_{b \in B} \vert\vert a - b \vert\vert$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Bidirectional Hausdorff:**
$$H(A, B) = \max(h(A, B), \, h(B, A))$$

A smaller Hausdorff distance means better boundary alignment. Units are in *pixels*.

### 2.2.5 Boundary F1 (BF1) ŌĆö Modern Deep Learning Standard

Used in **all modern KTU 2024 Scheme deep learning courses** for boundary evaluation.

$$P_{boundary} = \frac{B_{SE} \cap B_{GT}^{tolerance}}{B_{SE}}$$

$$R_{boundary} = \frac{B_{SE}^{tolerance} \cap B_{GT}}{B_{GT}}$$

$$BF1 = \frac{2 \cdot P_{boundary} \cdot R_{boundary}}{P_{boundary} + R_{boundary}}$$

The $tolerance$ is a small pixel band (usually 1ŌĆō3 pixels) around boundaries.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Cheat Table ŌĆö Memorize for ESE (End Semester Exam)**

| Method | Formula Summary | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Pratt's FOM | $\dfrac{1}{\max(N_{GT},N_{SE})} \sum \dfrac{1}{1 + \alpha d_i^2}$ | Edge detection evaluation |
| Misclassification Error | $\dfrac{FP + FN}{N}$ | Simple binary segmentation |
| Dice / F1 Score | $\dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2TP + FP + FN}$ | Medical image segmentation |
| Jaccard / IoU | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FP + FN}$ | Pascal VOC, Mask R-CNN |
| Rand Index | $\dfrac{TP + TN}{N}$ | Clustering-style segmentation |
| Hausdorff Distance | $\max(h(A,B), h(B,A))$ | Boundary geometric error |
| Uniformity | $1 - \dfrac{\sum \sigma_i^2 n_i}{N \sigma_{total}^2}$ | Unsupervised goodness |
| Inter-Region Contrast | $\dfrac{\sum \vert \mu_i - \mu_j \vert}{\sum (\mu_i + \mu_j)}$ | Unsupervised goodness |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Why Evaluation Matters |
|---|---|
| **Medical Imaging (Tumor Segmentation)** | Dice coefficient is the *de-facto* standard. A 1% drop in IoU can mean missing a tumor. |
| **Autonomous Vehicles (Lane/Road Segmentation)** | Boundary F1 and IoU are critical for safety certification. |
| **Satellite Imaging (Land Cover Mapping)** | Rand Index and ARI used to compare against human-annotated maps. |
| **Industrial Defect Detection** | Sensitivity is critical ŌĆö false negatives are dangerous, false positives are merely costly. |
| **Document Binarization (OCR Pre-processing)** | F-Measure used to grade algorithms like Sauvola, Otsu. |
| **Agriculture (Crop/Disease Segmentation)** | Jaccard Index correlates with yield-estimation accuracy. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Connection Between F1 Score and Jaccard Index

This is a **classic KTU derivable relationship** that frequently appears as a 7-mark sub-question.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Start with F1 Definition

$$F1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Start with Jaccard Index

$$JI = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Express F1 in Terms of Jaccard

$$\begin{aligned}
F1 &= \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} \\
&= \frac{2 \cdot TP}{TP + (TP + FP + FN)} \\
&= \frac{2 \cdot TP}{TP + \frac{TP}{JI}} \quad \text{(since } JI = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN} \Rightarrow TP + FP + FN = \frac{TP}{JI}\text{)} \\
&= \frac{2 \cdot TP \cdot JI}{TP \cdot (1 + JI)} \\
&= \frac{2 \cdot JI}{1 + JI}
\end{aligned}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{F1 = \frac{2 \cdot JI}{1 + JI}}$$

Or equivalently, the **inverse relationship**:

$$\boxed{JI = \frac{F1}{2 - F1}}$$

**Interpretation:** F1 is *always greater than or equal to* Jaccard for the same segmentation, and the two are monotonically related.

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example**
> Suppose $TP = 80$, $FP = 10$, $FN = 10$.
> - $JI = \frac{80}{80+10+10} = 0.80$
> - $F1 = \frac{2 \times 80}{160 + 20} = \frac{160}{180} = 0.8889$
> - Verify: $F1 = \frac{2 \times 0.80}{1 + 0.80} = \frac{1.60}{1.80} = 0.8889$ Ō£ō

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Pratt's Figure of Merit ŌĆö Intuition Behind the $\alpha$ Constant

### Step 1 ŌĆö Penalization Function Goal

We want a function $f(d)$ that satisfies:
- $f(0) = 1$ (perfect match)
- $f(\infty) = 0$ (penalty saturates)
- $f$ monotonically decreasing
- $f$ should be smooth (differentiable)

### Step 2 ŌĆö Pratt's Choice

Pratt chose an inverse-quadratic penalization:

$$f(d_i) = \frac{1}{1 + \alpha \cdot d_i^2}$$

For $d_i = 0$: $f = 1$ Ō£ō
For $d_i = \infty$: $f \to 0$ Ō£ō

### Step 3 ŌĆö Selection of $\alpha$

Pratt (1978) experimentally found that $\alpha = 1/9$ gives the best balance between:
- Tolerating small pixel-level misalignments
- Severely penalizing large edge misalignments

At $d_i = 1$ pixel offset:
$$f(1) = \frac{1}{1 + 1/9} = \frac{9}{10} = 0.9$$

At $d_i = 3$ pixel offset:
$$f(3) = \frac{1}{1 + 9 \cdot 1/9} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

This means an edge point 3 pixels off the truth contributes half credit.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Normalization

The sum is divided by $\max(N_{GT}, N_{SE})$ to bound the metric to $[0, 1]$.

$$\begin{aligned}
FOM &\in [0, 1] \\
FOM = 1 &\iff \text{perfect match (all } d_i = 0\text{)} \\
FOM = 0 &\iff \text{all detected edges are infinitely far from truth}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Hausdorff Distance ŌĆö Step-by-Step

### Step 1 ŌĆö Forward Directed Distance (a ŌåÆ b)

For each point $a$ in set $A$, find the nearest point in $B$:

$$\delta_{min}(a) = \min_{b \in B} \vert\vert a - b \vert\vert$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Maximum of all forward distances

The forward directed Hausdorff distance is the *worst point* in $A$:

$$h(A, B) = \max_{a \in A} \delta_{min}(a)$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Reverse Directed Distance (b ŌåÆ a)

Similarly:

$$h(B, A) = \max_{b \in B} \min_{a \in A} \vert\vert b - a \vert\vert$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Bidirectional Hausdorff

$$H(A, B) = \max(h(A, B), h(B, A))$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Worked Numerical Example

Let $A = \{(0,0), (1,0), (2,0)\}$ (true boundary)
Let $B = \{(0,1), (1,1), (2,1)\}$ (detected boundary, offset by 1 pixel)

**Forward:** For each $a$, nearest $b$ is directly above, distance = 1.
$$h(A, B) = \max(1, 1, 1) = 1$$

**Reverse:** Similarly, $h(B, A) = 1$.

$$H(A, B) = \max(1, 1) = 1 \text{ pixel}$$

This is a *perfect shift* ŌĆö every point is exactly 1 pixel off, and Hausdorff correctly returns 1.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Rand Index ŌĆö Connection to Pairwise Agreement

### Step 1 ŌĆö All Possible Pixel Pairs

Total number of pixel pairs in an $N$-pixel image:

$$\binom{N}{2} = \frac{N(N-1)}{2}$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Pairs Agreement Categories

For a pair of pixels $(p_1, p_2)$:
- **Same segment in both GT and SE** ŌåÆ Count: $a$
- **Different segments in both GT and SE** ŌåÆ Count: $b$
- **Same in GT, Different in SE** ŌåÆ Count: $c$
- **Different in GT, Same in SE** ŌåÆ Count: $d$

Note: $a + b + c + d = \binom{N}{2}$.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Rand Index Formula

$$RI = \frac{a + b}{a + b + c + d} = \frac{a + b}{\binom{N}{2}}$$

In terms of confusion matrix:

$$RI = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Why It Works

Rand Index measures the *probability* that a randomly chosen pair of pixels is in agreement. A perfect segmentation gives $RI = 1$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix ŌĆö Which Metric When?

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Table for Choosing an Evaluation Metric**

| Situation | Best Metric | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Medical tumor (region overlap) | Dice / Jaccard | Standard in MICCAI challenge |
| Edge detection | Pratt FOM | Designed for edge-point sets |
| Boundary alignment | Hausdorff / Boundary F1 | Measures geometric mismatch |
| Clustering-style | Rand / ARI | Treats pixels as cluster labels |
| No ground truth | Uniformity + Contrast | Unsupervised goodness |
| Class imbalance | F1 / Dice | Insensitive to true negatives |
| Strict safety (no missed tumors) | Sensitivity (Recall) | FN cost is critical |
| Strict filtering (no false alarms) | Precision | FP cost is critical |

---

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Worked Example: Full Evaluation of a Binary Segmentation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comprehensive Worked Example (Likely 14-mark KTU Question)**

**Given:** A $10 \times 10$ binary segmentation result is compared to ground truth.
- Number of pixels: $N = 100$
- $TP = 18$, $FP = 2$, $FN = 3$, $TN = 77$

**Compute all standard metrics:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Verify Total:**
$$TP + FP + FN + TN = 18 + 2 + 3 + 77 = 100 = N \quad Ō£ō$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Misclassification Error:**
$$ME = \frac{FP + FN}{N} = \frac{2 + 3}{100} = 0.05 = 5\%$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Sensitivity (Recall):**
$$Recall = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{18}{18+3} = \frac{18}{21} = 0.8571$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Specificity:**
$$Specificity = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{77}{77+2} = \frac{77}{79} = 0.9747$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Precision:**
$$Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{18}{18+2} = \frac{18}{20} = 0.9$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö F1 Score:**
$$F1 = \frac{2 \times 0.9 \times 0.8571}{0.9 + 0.8571} = \frac{2 \times 0.7714}{1.7571} = 0.8780$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Jaccard Index:**
$$JI = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN} = \frac{18}{18+2+3} = \frac{18}{23} = 0.7826$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Verify F1 vs JI Relation:**
$$F1 = \frac{2 \times JI}{1 + JI} = \frac{2 \times 0.7826}{1.7826} = \frac{1.5652}{1.7826} = 0.8780 \quad Ō£ō$$

**Step 9 ŌĆö Rand Index:**
$$RI = \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{18 + 77}{100} = 0.95$$

**Final Summary:**

| Metric | Value | Quality |
|---|---|---|
| ME | 0.05 | Excellent |
| Recall | 0.857 | Good |
| Specificity | 0.975 | Excellent |
| Precision | 0.900 | Excellent |
| F1 | 0.878 | Good |
| Jaccard | 0.783 | Good |
| Rand | 0.950 | Excellent |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
